question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

A "personal story" is a story about something that happened to YOU. Which of these is a personal story?
options: A made-up story about a dragon who lives on the moon
The day my dog ran into the kitchen and stole the sausages! Personal stories are real things that happened to you - things you can remember.
A book about how the planets were formed
question_json.correct: The day my dog ran into the kitchen and stole the sausages! Personal stories are real things that happened to you - things you can remember.

feedback.correct: Correct! A personal story is a real event from YOUR life. The dog stealing sausages is something that actually happened to you - that is a personal story. You have many of these stories already, just waiting to be written down.
feedback.wrong: A personal story is something REAL that happened to YOU. Made-up adventures, facts, and lists are not personal stories. The story about your dog and the sausages is something you remember happening - that is what makes it personal.

answer_json.correct: The day my dog ran into the kitchen and stole the sausages! Personal stories are real things that happened to you - things you can remember.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Which of these is a personal story?
options: A list of all the colours of the rainbow
When I fell off my bike and skinned my knee at Grandma's house. This is a personal story because it actually happened to YOU and you remember it clearly.
A made-up story about a princess
question_json.correct: When I fell off my bike and skinned my knee at Grandma's house. This is a personal story because it actually happened to YOU and you remember it clearly.

feedback.correct: Correct! Falling off your bike at Grandma's house is something that really happened to you. You can remember the place, the moment, and how you felt. That is exactly what makes a story PERSONAL.
feedback.wrong: Personal stories are things that REALLY happened to YOU. You can remember them. The bike fall is a personal story. Lists and made-up stories are not.

answer_json.correct: When I fell off my bike and skinned my knee at Grandma's house. This is a personal story because it actually happened to YOU and you remember it clearly.

question_json.question:
Choose the BEST answer.

Why is it good to write about things from YOUR life?
options: Because you cannot make anything up
Because YOU are the only person who knows your stories. Nobody else has lived your life. Your stories are ones only YOU can write, and that makes them special.
Because lists are easier to write
Because writing must always be true
question_json.correct: Because YOU are the only person who knows your stories. Nobody else has lived your life. Your stories are ones only YOU can write, and that makes them special.

feedback.correct: Correct! Only YOU have lived your life. Only you know what happened on your first day of school, what your nan's kitchen smelled like, or how you felt when you got your puppy. These are stories ONLY YOU can write. That makes your personal stories special and important.
feedback.wrong: Personal stories matter because only YOU have lived your life. Nobody else can tell your stories the way you can. Your memories, your feelings, your moments - these are the stories that only you can write.

answer_json.correct: Because YOU are the only person who knows your stories. Nobody else has lived your life. Your stories are ones only YOU can write, and that makes them special.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Look at this list:

Mum, Dad, my sister Lily, my best friend Tom, my teacher Mrs Smith, my dog Buddy

What KIND of list is this?
options: A list of PEOPLE in my life - the family, friends, pets, and teachers I see every day. People are usually the most important characters in our personal stories.
A list of times of day
question_json.correct: A list of PEOPLE in my life - the family, friends, pets, and teachers I see every day. People are usually the most important characters in our personal stories.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a list of PEOPLE in your life. Your personal stories will often be ABOUT these people. They are the characters of your life, and writing about them brings your stories alive.
feedback.wrong: This is a list of PEOPLE - your family, friends, teachers, and pets. People are usually the main characters in personal stories. The story is often about something that happened with one of them.

answer_json.correct: A list of PEOPLE in my life - the family, friends, pets, and teachers I see every day. People are usually the most important characters in our personal stories.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Look at this list:

my bedroom, the school playground, Grandma's garden, the park near my house, the seaside where we go on holiday

What KIND of list is this?
options: A list of PLACES I go - the spots where my stories happen. The setting (where the story takes place) is a key part of every personal story.
question_json.correct: A list of PLACES I go - the spots where my stories happen. The setting (where the story takes place) is a key part of every personal story.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a list of PLACES. Personal stories always happen SOMEWHERE - in a room, a park, a school, on holiday. The setting brings the story alive in the reader's mind. Knowing where a story happened helps you write it well.
feedback.wrong: This is a list of PLACES - the locations where your stories happen. Every story has a setting. Knowing where the story takes place helps you describe it.

answer_json.correct: A list of PLACES I go - the spots where my stories happen. The setting (where the story takes place) is a key part of every personal story.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Look at this list:

my teddy bear, my favourite jumper, the necklace from Nana, my football boots, my reading book

What KIND of list is this?
options: A list of THINGS I love - special objects that have memories or meaning. Things from your life often have stories attached: where they came from, who gave them to you, why they matter.
question_json.correct: A list of THINGS I love - special objects that have memories or meaning. Things from your life often have stories attached: where they came from, who gave them to you, why they matter.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a list of THINGS - special objects with meaning. Things in your life often have stories: the necklace from Nana, the teddy you have had since you were a baby. These objects can be the heart of a personal story.
feedback.wrong: This is a list of THINGS that matter to you. Special objects often have stories attached - someone gave them to you, you got them on a special day, or they helped you through something. Things can be the centre of a story.

answer_json.correct: A list of THINGS I love - special objects that have memories or meaning. Things from your life often have stories attached: where they came from, who gave them to you, why they matter.

question_json.question:
Click an item on the left, then its matching list on the right.

Match each item to the correct LIST it belongs in.
question_json.instruction: Click an item on the left, then its matching list on the right.

pairs.right: PEOPLE list (a character)
PLACES list (a setting)
THINGS list (a special object)
PLACES list (where stories happen)
PEOPLE list (a character)
PLACES list (a setting)
THINGS list (a special object)
PLACES list (where stories happen)

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this sentence:

"I went to the park."

Which version has more sensory DETAIL added?
options: "I ran across the green grass at Sunny Park, where the warm sun shone and the swings squeaked as my friend pushed me higher and higher." This version uses what you can SEE, HEAR, and FEEL to bring the moment alive.
question_json.correct: "I ran across the green grass at Sunny Park, where the warm sun shone and the swings squeaked as my friend pushed me higher and higher." This version uses what you can SEE, HEAR, and FEEL to bring the moment alive.

feedback.correct: Correct! Adding details about what you SEE (green grass, sunny park), HEAR (squeaking swings), and FEEL (warm sun) brings a memory alive on the page. Details transform a flat sentence into a story the reader can picture.
feedback.wrong: Sensory details (sight, sound, feel) are what bring writing alive. The strong version describes the green grass, the squeaking swings, and the warm sun. Saying "the park was nice" tells us almost nothing.

answer_json.correct: "I ran across the green grass at Sunny Park, where the warm sun shone and the swings squeaked as my friend pushed me higher and higher." This version uses what you can SEE, HEAR, and FEEL to bring the moment alive.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

A flat sentence: "I went to my Nan's house."

Which rewrite adds the strongest DETAILS?
options: "On Sunday I walked to Nan's old house, where the smell of warm apple pie filled the kitchen and her cat Bella sat purring on the windowsill."
"I went to Nan's. It was good."
"I went to my Nan's house yesterday I think."
question_json.correct: "On Sunday I walked to Nan's old house, where the smell of warm apple pie filled the kitchen and her cat Bella sat purring on the windowsill."

feedback.correct: Correct! "On Sunday I walked to Nan's old house..." has SMELL (apple pie), SIGHT (Bella the cat), SOUND (purring), and a SETTING (Sunday, kitchen, windowsill). All these details build a real moment that the reader can picture.
feedback.wrong: Strong details use what you can see, smell, hear, and feel. The first option includes apple pie smell, Bella the cat purring on the windowsill, and the day (Sunday). Other options just say "I went" without bringing the moment alive.

answer_json.correct: "On Sunday I walked to Nan's old house, where the smell of warm apple pie filled the kitchen and her cat Bella sat purring on the windowsill."

question_json.question:
Click a part on the left, then the matching detail type on the right.

Each story needs three things: a setting (place), characters (people), and what they do (action). Match each part of the sentence to the type of detail it gives.
question_json.instruction: Click a part on the left, then the matching detail type on the right.

pairs.right: SETTING - where the story happens
CHARACTERS - the people in the story
ACTION - what they do
SETTING - where the story happens
CHARACTERS - the people in the story
ACTION - what they do

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

A complete story has THREE parts. What are they?
options: Beginning, Middle, and End. Every personal story has these three parts: how it started (beginning), what happened (middle), and how it finished (end). Without all three, the story feels incomplete.
question_json.correct: Beginning, Middle, and End. Every personal story has these three parts: how it started (beginning), what happened (middle), and how it finished (end). Without all three, the story feels incomplete.

feedback.correct: Correct! A complete story has Beginning, Middle, and End. The beginning sets up what is happening. The middle is where the action takes place. The end shows how things finished. Every personal story should have all three parts.
feedback.wrong: Stories have Beginning, Middle, and End. The beginning starts the story. The middle is where the action happens. The end finishes the story. If any of these is missing, the story feels broken.

answer_json.correct: Beginning, Middle, and End. Every personal story has these three parts: how it started (beginning), what happened (middle), and how it finished (end). Without all three, the story feels incomplete.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read these three sentences from a story. Which is the BEGINNING?
options: My puppy was found at the back of the garden, eating mud from the flower pot.

Last summer, on a sunny afternoon, my new puppy went missing. This sentence sets up the WHO (my puppy), WHEN (last summer, sunny afternoon), and WHAT IS HAPPENING (puppy went missing) - that is what beginnings do.
I felt happy and gave my puppy a big cuddle.
question_json.correct: Last summer, on a sunny afternoon, my new puppy went missing. This sentence sets up the WHO (my puppy), WHEN (last summer, sunny afternoon), and WHAT IS HAPPENING (puppy went missing) - that is what beginnings do.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Last summer..." is the beginning because it sets up the story - WHO (my puppy), WHEN (sunny afternoon), and WHAT IS HAPPENING (the puppy went missing). The other two sentences come later.
feedback.wrong: The beginning SETS UP the story. It tells us who is in the story, when it happened, and what is happening. "Last summer, on a sunny afternoon, my new puppy went missing" sets all of these up. The other sentences come after.

answer_json.correct: Last summer, on a sunny afternoon, my new puppy went missing. This sentence sets up the WHO (my puppy), WHEN (last summer, sunny afternoon), and WHAT IS HAPPENING (puppy went missing) - that is what beginnings do.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this story. What part is MISSING?

"Last summer, my puppy went missing. I felt happy and gave my puppy a big cuddle."
options: The middle - the part where you searched for the puppy and found him! Without the middle, we jump from puppy missing to puppy hugged. We do not know HOW the story got from one to the other.
question_json.correct: The middle - the part where you searched for the puppy and found him! Without the middle, we jump from puppy missing to puppy hugged. We do not know HOW the story got from one to the other.

feedback.correct: Correct! The MIDDLE is missing. We have the beginning (puppy went missing) and the end (I cuddled the puppy). But what happened between? Where did you search? Who helped? When did you find him? The middle is where the action happens.
feedback.wrong: The middle is missing - the part where the action happens. We jump from "puppy went missing" to "I gave my puppy a cuddle". What about the searching, the worry, the moment of finding? Those belong in the middle.

answer_json.correct: The middle - the part where you searched for the puppy and found him! Without the middle, we jump from puppy missing to puppy hugged. We do not know HOW the story got from one to the other.

question_json.question:
Click a sentence on the left, then its matching part on the right.

Match each story sentence to the part it belongs to (Beginning, Middle, or End).

pairs.right: END - how the story finished

pairs.right: BEGINNING - what set up the story

pairs.left: "Mum drove back, we ran along the road, and finally I spotted teddy lying in a puddle."

question_json.question:
Your sentence should tell us WHO (you and other people), WHEN (when it happened), and WHERE (the place). Write 1-2 lines.

Pick ONE memory from your life. Write a 1-sentence Beginning for that story.

Example beginnings:

"Last winter, on a snowy morning, my brother and I went outside to build a snowman in our garden."
"On my birthday last year, Mum took me and my friend to the seaside for the day."
"One Saturday, while staying at Nan's, I tried to make a cake all by myself."
question_json.prompt: Last [when]... at/in [where]... [who] [what is happening].
question_json.instruction: Your sentence should tell us WHO (you and other people), WHEN (when it happened), and WHERE (the place). Write 1-2 lines.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong beginning sentence sets up THREE things:

1. WHEN - last summer, on my birthday, one Saturday morning, last winter

2. WHERE - the place: in our garden, at the seaside, in Nan's kitchen, at school

3. WHO - you and others: me and my brother, Mum and I, my best friend Tom and I

Example: "Last summer, in Grandad's garden, my cousin and I tried to climb the tall apple tree."

This sentence tells us WHEN (last summer), WHERE (Grandad's garden), and WHO (my cousin and I). Now the reader is ready for what happens next - the MIDDLE of the story.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong beginning sentence sets up THREE things:

1. WHEN - last summer, on my birthday, one Saturday morning, last winter

2. WHERE - the place: in our garden, at the seaside, in Nan's kitchen, at school

3. WHO - you and others: me and my brother, Mum and I, my best friend Tom and I

Example: "Last summer, in Grandad's garden, my cousin and I tried to climb the tall apple tree."

This sentence tells us WHEN (last summer), WHERE (Grandad's garden), and WHO (my cousin and I). Now the reader is ready for what happens next - the MIDDLE of the story.

question_json.question:
Write 2-3 sentences describing the action - what you did, what others did, and what happened. Try to use sensory details (what you saw, heard, or felt).

Now write the MIDDLE of the same story. What happened?

Example middle (continuing the snowman story):

"We rolled three big snowballs - one for the body, one for the tummy, and one for the head. The snow was so cold it made my fingers tingle, but my brother giggled the whole time. We used pebbles for the eyes and a long stick for the smile, and we put Dad's old scarf around its neck."
question_json.prompt: First we... then... and then...
question_json.instruction: Write 2-3 sentences describing the action - what you did, what others did, and what happened. Try to use sensory details (what you saw, heard, or felt).
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong middle does TWO things:

1. SHOWS THE ACTION - what you did, what happened, in order. Use words like "first", "then", "after that", "suddenly".

2. ADDS SENSORY DETAILS - what you saw, heard, smelt, or felt. These details make the reader feel like they are there with you.

Example (continuing the apple tree story):
"My cousin climbed up first while I held the wobbly branch. The leaves rustled and a few apples thudded down into the grass. Halfway up, I slipped and gave out a yelp. My cousin laughed, but Grandad rushed over to make sure I was alright."

Notice the action (climbing, slipping, laughing, rushing over) AND the senses (rustling leaves, thudding apples, my yelp). Both work together to bring the middle alive.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong middle does TWO things:

1. SHOWS THE ACTION - what you did, what happened, in order. Use words like "first", "then", "after that", "suddenly".

2. ADDS SENSORY DETAILS - what you saw, heard, smelt, or felt. These details make the reader feel like they are there with you.

Example (continuing the apple tree story):
"My cousin climbed up first while I held the wobbly branch. The leaves rustled and a few apples thudded down into the grass. Halfway up, I slipped and gave out a yelp. My cousin laughed, but Grandad rushed over to make sure I was alright."

Notice the action (climbing, slipping, laughing, rushing over) AND the senses (rustling leaves, thudding apples, my yelp). Both work together to bring the middle alive.

question_json.question:
Write 1-2 sentences. The end should bring the story to a close - how it finished, how you felt, or what you learned.

Now write the END of your story. How did it finish?

Example endings (continuing the snowman story):

"By the time the snowman was finished, my fingers were freezing but my heart was warm. We named him Frosty Bob and he watched our garden for three whole days."
question_json.prompt: In the end... or At the end... I felt...
question_json.instruction: Write 1-2 sentences. The end should bring the story to a close - how it finished, how you felt, or what you learned.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong ending does ONE OR MORE of these:

1. SHOWS HOW IT FINISHED - the last thing that happened

2. SHARES HOW YOU FELT - cold but happy, proud, scared, surprised

3. SHOWS WHAT YOU LEARNED OR REMEMBERED - "I will never forget that day", "I learned not to..."

Example (continuing the apple tree story):
"In the end, Grandad helped us both down and gave us a glass of homemade lemonade. We never did get an apple from the tree, but we both still talk about that climb today."

This ending shows how it finished (Grandad helping us down, lemonade), shares a feeling (still talking about it today), and reminds the reader why the story mattered.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong ending does ONE OR MORE of these:

1. SHOWS HOW IT FINISHED - the last thing that happened

2. SHARES HOW YOU FELT - cold but happy, proud, scared, surprised

3. SHOWS WHAT YOU LEARNED OR REMEMBERED - "I will never forget that day", "I learned not to..."

Example (continuing the apple tree story):
"In the end, Grandad helped us both down and gave us a glass of homemade lemonade. We never did get an apple from the tree, but we both still talk about that climb today."

This ending shows how it finished (Grandad helping us down, lemonade), shares a feeling (still talking about it today), and reminds the reader why the story mattered.

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

MEMORY DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil writes:
"I went somewhere. It was good. We did stuff. The end."

What is wrong, and what should the Memory Doctor prescribe?
options: The story is fine - it has a beginning, middle, and end

The story has NO DETAILS. We do not know WHO was there, WHEN it happened, WHERE it was, or WHAT they did. The Memory Doctor would prescribe: add details. WHEN happened? WHERE? WHO was there? WHAT did you actually do? Replace "stuff" with specific actions.
The pupil should write about something different
question_json.correct: The story has NO DETAILS. We do not know WHO was there, WHEN it happened, WHERE it was, or WHAT they did. The Memory Doctor would prescribe: add details. WHEN happened? WHERE? WHO was there? WHAT did you actually do? Replace "stuff" with specific actions.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The story has the SHAPE of a story (beginning, middle, end) but NO DETAILS. "Somewhere", "good", "stuff" tell us nothing. The Memory Doctor prescribes: WHEN? WHERE? WHO? WHAT? Once those questions are answered, the story comes alive.
feedback.wrong: The Memory Doctor checks for DETAILS. This story has none - just "somewhere", "good", "stuff". To fix it, the pupil must add: WHEN (last week, last summer), WHERE (the park, my Nan's house), WHO (with whom), and WHAT exactly they did.

answer_json.correct: The story has NO DETAILS. We do not know WHO was there, WHEN it happened, WHERE it was, or WHAT they did. The Memory Doctor would prescribe: add details. WHEN happened? WHERE? WHO was there? WHAT did you actually do? Replace "stuff" with specific actions.

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

MEMORY DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil writes:
"Last summer at the beach, I built a sandcastle. I was so proud!"

The pupil says: "I have a beginning, middle, and end - is my story good?" What is the Doctor's diagnosis?
options: Yes, the story is good - leave it as it is

The story has details (last summer, beach, sandcastle, proud) but the MIDDLE is missing. The reader jumps from "I built a sandcastle" to "I was proud" - we do not know HOW it was built, what happened, who helped, or any of the action. The Memory Doctor would prescribe: add 2-3 sentences in the middle showing the ACTION of building the sandcastle.
Take out the part about the beach
question_json.correct: The story has details (last summer, beach, sandcastle, proud) but the MIDDLE is missing. The reader jumps from "I built a sandcastle" to "I was proud" - we do not know HOW it was built, what happened, who helped, or any of the action. The Memory Doctor would prescribe: add 2-3 sentences in the middle showing the ACTION of building the sandcastle.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The pupil has a beginning ("last summer at the beach") and an end ("I was so proud") but NO MIDDLE - no action between. The Memory Doctor prescribes: show the action of building. "I dug the moat with a plastic spade. My little brother pressed shells around the towers. The sea splashed at our feet as we worked." THAT is the middle.
feedback.wrong: The story is missing its MIDDLE - the action. The pupil jumps from beach to pride without showing what happened between. The Memory Doctor prescribes: add 2-3 sentences showing the action of building the sandcastle. What did you do? What happened? Use details and senses.

answer_json.correct: The story has details (last summer, beach, sandcastle, proud) but the MIDDLE is missing. The reader jumps from "I built a sandcastle" to "I was proud" - we do not know HOW it was built, what happened, who helped, or any of the action. The Memory Doctor would prescribe: add 2-3 sentences in the middle showing the ACTION of building the sandcastle.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on personal stories.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) what a personal story is, (2) the three parts every story needs, and (3) ONE personal story idea you would like to write next.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 1 Reflection

Example reflection:

"A personal story is a real thing that happened to ME. Every personal story needs a beginning, a middle, and an end. The story I would like to write next is about the day my dog Buddy ran into the kitchen and stole the sausages off the table - the beginning was Saturday morning, the middle was Mum chasing him round the kitchen, and the end was us all laughing as Buddy finished the sausages under the table."
question_json.prompt:
A personal story is...
Every story needs...
The story I would like to write next is...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on personal stories.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) what a personal story is, (2) the three parts every story needs, and (3) ONE personal story idea you would like to write next.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. WHAT A PERSONAL STORY IS - a real event from YOUR life. Only you have lived your life, so only you can tell your stories.

2. THE THREE PARTS - Beginning (sets up who, when, where), Middle (the action), End (how it finished).

3. ONE STORY YOU WOULD LIKE TO WRITE - your own choice. Pick a memory that you remember clearly. Even a small moment can be a strong story if you tell it well. The day your dog stole the sausages. The time you fell off your bike. Your first day at school. The trip to the seaside. Whatever you choose, picture the WHO, WHEN, WHERE.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. WHAT A PERSONAL STORY IS - a real event from YOUR life. Only you have lived your life, so only you can tell your stories.

2. THE THREE PARTS - Beginning (sets up who, when, where), Middle (the action), End (how it finished).

3. ONE STORY YOU WOULD LIKE TO WRITE - your own choice. Pick a memory that you remember clearly. Even a small moment can be a strong story if you tell it well. The day your dog stole the sausages. The time you fell off your bike. Your first day at school. The trip to the seaside. Whatever you choose, picture the WHO, WHEN, WHERE.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What are SENSORY DETAILS in writing?
options: Details about what we SEE, HEAR, SMELL, TASTE, and FEEL. These are the five senses. Sensory details put the reader inside the moment so they can almost experience it themselves.
Words at the end of sentences
question_json.correct: Details about what we SEE, HEAR, SMELL, TASTE, and FEEL. These are the five senses. Sensory details put the reader inside the moment so they can almost experience it themselves.

feedback.correct: Correct! Sensory details describe what you SEE, HEAR, SMELL, TASTE, and FEEL. These are the five senses. When writers add sensory details, the reader feels like they are right there in the story.
feedback.wrong: Sensory details are descriptions that use the five SENSES: sight (see), hearing (hear), smell, taste, and touch (feel). They help the reader picture and feel what is happening.

answer_json.correct: Details about what we SEE, HEAR, SMELL, TASTE, and FEEL. These are the five senses. Sensory details put the reader inside the moment so they can almost experience it themselves.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

How many senses do humans have?
options: Five - sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each one gives us different information about the world. Each one can be used in writing to bring a moment alive.
question_json.correct: Five - sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each one gives us different information about the world. Each one can be used in writing to bring a moment alive.

feedback.correct: Correct! We have FIVE senses: sight (eyes), hearing (ears), smell (nose), taste (tongue), and touch (skin/hands). Each one gives us different information about the world, and each one can be used in writing.
feedback.wrong: We have five senses: sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each one helps us experience the world in a different way. Writers use all five to bring scenes alive.

answer_json.correct: Five - sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each one gives us different information about the world. Each one can be used in writing to bring a moment alive.

question_json.question:
Choose the BEST answer.

Why do writers use sensory details?
options: To put the reader INSIDE the moment so they can see, hear, smell, taste, and feel what the character does. Sensory details turn flat sentences into experiences.
Because every sentence needs five details
question_json.correct: To put the reader INSIDE the moment so they can see, hear, smell, taste, and feel what the character does. Sensory details turn flat sentences into experiences.

feedback.correct: Correct! Sensory details put the reader INSIDE the moment. Instead of just telling them "the kitchen was nice", you let them SMELL the bread, HEAR the kettle, and FEEL the warmth. The reader experiences the moment along with you.
feedback.wrong: Sensory details bring the reader INTO the moment. Without them, writing stays flat. With them, the reader can almost taste, smell, and feel what is happening - they are inside the story.

answer_json.correct: To put the reader INSIDE the moment so they can see, hear, smell, taste, and feel what the character does. Sensory details turn flat sentences into experiences.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct sense.

Read this sentence:

"The bell rang loudly across the playground."

Which SENSE is this detail using?
options: Hearing - the word "loudly" describes how the bell SOUNDED. We hear bells, not see them. "Loudly" is a hearing word.
question_json.correct: Hearing - the word "loudly" describes how the bell SOUNDED. We hear bells, not see them. "Loudly" is a hearing word.

feedback.correct: Correct! "The bell rang loudly" is a HEARING detail. The word "loudly" describes the sound. Other hearing words include quiet, whispered, shouted, squeaked, banged, hummed.
feedback.wrong: The word "loudly" tells us how the bell SOUNDED. We HEAR sounds, so this is a hearing detail. Bells, voices, music, doors slamming - all of these go with the sense of HEARING.

question_json.question:
Choose the answer that uses the most senses.

Look at this sentence. Which version uses the MOST sensory details?
options: "I ran across the green grass at Sunny Park, where I heard the squeaky swings, smelled hot chips from the cafe, and felt the warm sun on my face." This sentence uses SIGHT (green grass), HEARING (squeaky swings), SMELL (hot chips), and TOUCH (warm sun). Four senses!
question_json.correct: "I ran across the green grass at Sunny Park, where I heard the squeaky swings, smelled hot chips from the cafe, and felt the warm sun on my face." This sentence uses SIGHT (green grass), HEARING (squeaky swings), SMELL (hot chips), and TOUCH (warm sun). Four senses!

feedback.correct: Correct! The strongest sentence uses FOUR senses: SIGHT (green grass), HEARING (squeaky swings), SMELL (hot chips), and TOUCH (warm sun). The reader can almost picture, hear, smell, and feel the park. That is sensory writing.
feedback.wrong: The strong sentence uses FOUR senses: sight (green grass), hearing (squeaky swings), smell (hot chips), and touch (warm sun). More sensory details = more vivid writing. The reader is INSIDE the scene.

answer_json.correct: "I ran across the green grass at Sunny Park, where I heard the squeaky swings, smelled hot chips from the cafe, and felt the warm sun on my face." This sentence uses SIGHT (green grass), HEARING (squeaky swings), SMELL (hot chips), and TOUCH (warm sun). Four senses!

question_json.question:

options: "The cold strawberry ice cream tasted sweet and felt smooth on my tongue, dripping pink down the side of the cone." This uses TASTE (sweet, strawberry), TOUCH (cold, smooth), and SIGHT (pink dripping down).
question_json.correct: "The cold strawberry ice cream tasted sweet and felt smooth on my tongue, dripping pink down the side of the cone." This uses TASTE (sweet, strawberry), TOUCH (cold, smooth), and SIGHT (pink dripping down).

feedback.correct: Correct! The strong version uses TASTE (sweet, strawberry), TOUCH (cold, smooth), and SIGHT (pink dripping). It paints a picture in the reader's mind so they can almost taste the ice cream too.
feedback.wrong: "Tasty" is a flat word. Strong sensory writing tells us specifically what TASTE (sweet, strawberry), TOUCH (cold, smooth), and SIGHT (pink dripping) the ice cream had. The more senses, the more the reader can imagine the moment.

answer_json.correct: "The cold strawberry ice cream tasted sweet and felt smooth on my tongue, dripping pink down the side of the cone." This uses TASTE (sweet, strawberry), TOUCH (cold, smooth), and SIGHT (pink dripping down).

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this sentence:

"The kitchen was nice."

What is missing from this sentence?
options: SENSORY DETAILS - the word "nice" tells us nothing. What did the kitchen LOOK like? What did it SMELL like? What sounds were there? What did it FEEL like? Adding sensory details would bring this kitchen alive.
question_json.correct: SENSORY DETAILS - the word "nice" tells us nothing. What did the kitchen LOOK like? What did it SMELL like? What sounds were there? What did it FEEL like? Adding sensory details would bring this kitchen alive.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Nice" is a flat word that tells us nothing. To bring this kitchen alive, the writer needs SENSORY DETAILS: the smell of bread baking, the bubbling sound of soup, the warm light through the window, the cool tiles under bare feet. Senses make the kitchen real.
feedback.wrong: "Nice" is too flat. The kitchen needs SENSORY DETAILS - what did the writer SEE, HEAR, SMELL, TASTE, or FEEL there? Without sensory details, the reader has nothing to picture.

answer_json.correct: SENSORY DETAILS - the word "nice" tells us nothing. What did the kitchen LOOK like? What did it SMELL like? What sounds were there? What did it FEEL like? Adding sensory details would bring this kitchen alive.

question_json.question:

Read these sentences. Which is using SENSORY DETAILS WELL?
options: "My nan's kitchen was nice. The food was good. We had a lovely time."

"My nan's warm kitchen smelled of fresh apple pie. The kettle whistled on the stove and I felt the soft tea-towel as I helped her dry the dishes." This sentence uses SMELL (apple pie), HEARING (whistling kettle), and TOUCH (soft tea-towel).
"My nan's kitchen was the colour of a kitchen."
"I went to my nan's. It was nice."
question_json.correct: "My nan's warm kitchen smelled of fresh apple pie. The kettle whistled on the stove and I felt the soft tea-towel as I helped her dry the dishes." This sentence uses SMELL (apple pie), HEARING (whistling kettle), and TOUCH (soft tea-towel).

feedback.correct: Correct! The strong example uses SMELL (apple pie), HEARING (whistling kettle), and TOUCH (soft tea-towel). It puts us right inside that kitchen with Nan. The flat sentences tell us almost nothing.
feedback.wrong: The strong sentence uses three senses: SMELL (apple pie), HEARING (whistling kettle), and TOUCH (soft tea-towel). Sentences like "it was nice" or "the food was good" use NO senses, so the reader cannot picture anything.

answer_json.correct: "My nan's warm kitchen smelled of fresh apple pie. The kettle whistled on the stove and I felt the soft tea-towel as I helped her dry the dishes." This sentence uses SMELL (apple pie), HEARING (whistling kettle), and TOUCH (soft tea-towel).

question_json.question:

options: SIGHT (the colour of the sea, the seagulls), HEARING (the waves crashing, seagulls calling), and SMELL (salty air, fish and chips). The seaside is famous for these senses, and they will bring the writing alive.
Hearing only, because the sea is loud
question_json.correct: SIGHT (the colour of the sea, the seagulls), HEARING (the waves crashing, seagulls calling), and SMELL (salty air, fish and chips). The seaside is famous for these senses, and they will bring the writing alive.

feedback.correct: Correct! At the seaside, SIGHT (colours, seagulls), HEARING (crashing waves, seagulls calling), and SMELL (salty air, fish and chips) are the strongest senses. Adding these would turn the flat writing into a real seaside scene the reader could picture.
feedback.wrong: The seaside has many senses to use: SIGHT (the colour of the sea, the seagulls), HEARING (crashing waves, seagulls calling), and SMELL (salty air, chips). Sentences like "it was nice" use NO senses - the writing needs all three to come alive.

answer_json.correct: SIGHT (the colour of the sea, the seagulls), HEARING (the waves crashing, seagulls calling), and SMELL (salty air, fish and chips). The seaside is famous for these senses, and they will bring the writing alive.

question_json.correct: "Bees buzzed around the bright purple lavender, and the smell of cut grass filled the warm air." This sentence uses HEARING (bees buzzing), SIGHT (bright purple lavender), SMELL (cut grass), and TOUCH (warm air). Four senses in one sentence!

feedback.wrong: The strong sentence uses HEARING (bees buzzing), SIGHT (bright purple lavender), SMELL (cut grass), and TOUCH (warm air). Combining senses in one sentence brings the scene fully alive.

question_json.question:
Use AT LEAST 3 senses (sight, hearing, smell, taste, or touch). Write 1-2 sentences.

Rewrite this flat sentence with SENSORY DETAILS:

"I ate breakfast."

Example rewrites:

"I crunched the warm golden toast spread thick with sweet strawberry jam, while the kettle whistled in the kitchen and the smell of frying bacon filled the room."

"My mug of hot chocolate steamed in front of me. I tasted the sweet warmth and felt the soft cushion of the kitchen chair as I crunched my buttery toast."
question_json.prompt: I [verb]... I saw... I heard... I smelt... I tasted... I felt...
question_json.instruction: Use AT LEAST 3 senses (sight, hearing, smell, taste, or touch). Write 1-2 sentences.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong sensory rewrite of "I ate breakfast" uses at least 3 senses:

Example: "I crunched warm golden toast (SIGHT: golden + TOUCH: warm + HEARING: crunched) spread with sweet strawberry jam (TASTE: sweet) while the kettle whistled (HEARING) and bacon sizzled in the pan (HEARING + SMELL implied)."

Notice the senses chosen:
- SIGHT: golden, sizzling, steam rising
- HEARING: crunched, whistled, sizzled
- SMELL: bacon frying, fresh bread
- TASTE: sweet jam, salty bacon
- TOUCH: warm toast, hot mug

The more senses you weave in, the more the reader can picture the breakfast.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong sensory rewrite of "I ate breakfast" uses at least 3 senses:

Example: "I crunched warm golden toast (SIGHT: golden + TOUCH: warm + HEARING: crunched) spread with sweet strawberry jam (TASTE: sweet) while the kettle whistled (HEARING) and bacon sizzled in the pan (HEARING + SMELL implied)."

Notice the senses chosen:
- SIGHT: golden, sizzling, steam rising

- SMELL: bacon frying, fresh bread
- TASTE: sweet jam, salty bacon
- TOUCH: warm toast, hot mug

The more senses you weave in, the more the reader can picture the breakfast.

question_json.question:
Use AT LEAST 3 senses to bring this moment alive. Write 1-2 sentences.

Rewrite this flat sentence with SENSORY DETAILS:

"My friend visited."

Example rewrites:

"When Lily knocked on the door, her bright red coat dripping with rain (SIGHT) and her cold cheeks pink from the wind (TOUCH), I could smell the cinnamon biscuits she had brought (SMELL)."

"Tom arrived with his loud laugh (HEARING) and a warm hug (TOUCH). I caught the smell of chips from the bag he held out (SMELL) and saw the surprise gift behind his back (SIGHT)."
question_json.prompt: When [name] arrived, I saw... I heard... I smelt... I felt...
question_json.instruction: Use AT LEAST 3 senses to bring this moment alive. Write 1-2 sentences.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong sensory rewrite of "my friend visited" uses 3+ senses:

Example: "When Tom rang the bell, his cheery laugh filled the hallway (HEARING). His coat smelled of fresh rain (SMELL). I felt his cold hand as he passed me a warm bag of chips (TOUCH + SIGHT)."

Think about:
- SIGHT: what they were wearing, holding, their face
- HEARING: their voice, footsteps, laughter, the doorbell
- SMELL: rain on their coat, food they brought, perfume
- TOUCH: cold hands, warm hug, soft cardigan
- TASTE: only if they bring food you ate together

Even a simple visit can be brought alive with 3 senses.

question_json.question:
Write 3-4 sentences using SIGHT, HEARING, SMELL, TASTE, and TOUCH. (If TASTE does not fit naturally, use the other 4 strongly.)

Pick a place you know well (your bedroom, school, the park, your kitchen). Describe it using ALL 5 SENSES.

Example - my kitchen:

"Our bright kitchen has yellow walls and a large window looking onto the garden (SIGHT). The kettle hisses on the stove and the radio hums quietly (HEARING). It smells of freshly brewed coffee and toast (SMELL). The wooden table feels smooth under my fingers (TOUCH), and on Sunday mornings I taste the sweet jam Mum spreads on my toast (TASTE)."
question_json.prompt: My [place] - what I see, hear, smell, taste, and feel.
question_json.instruction: Write 3-4 sentences using SIGHT, HEARING, SMELL, TASTE, and TOUCH. (If TASTE does not fit naturally, use the other 4 strongly.)
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong full-sensory description of a place uses all 5 senses:

Example - the school playground:

"At lunchtime, the playground is full of bright colours (SIGHT - children's coats, painted lines on the ground). I can hear children shouting and the football thudding against the fence (HEARING). The smell of school dinners drifts out from the kitchen door (SMELL). The metal climbing frame feels cold and smooth in my hands (TOUCH), and at break I sometimes have a cool, salty crisp from my friend's packet (TASTE)."

Notice that the description uses:
- SIGHT: bright colours, painted lines, coats
- HEARING: shouting, football thudding
- SMELL: school dinners drifting
- TOUCH: cold, smooth metal
- TASTE: cool, salty crisp

A description like this puts the reader RIGHT THERE in the playground.

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

SENSORY DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil writes:
"The market was nice. There were people. I saw stuff. It was good."

What is the Sensory Doctor's diagnosis?
options: The pupil should write about something other than a market

The writing has NO SENSORY DETAILS at all. Words like "nice", "stuff", and "good" tell us nothing. The Sensory Doctor would prescribe: replace these flat words with specifics from the FIVE senses. What did the pupil SEE (the colours, the stalls)? HEAR (shoppers, market traders shouting)? SMELL (fresh fish, baking bread)? TASTE (free samples)? FEEL (the cold air, the busy crowd)?
The pupil should add more flat words
question_json.correct: The writing has NO SENSORY DETAILS at all. Words like "nice", "stuff", and "good" tell us nothing. The Sensory Doctor would prescribe: replace these flat words with specifics from the FIVE senses. What did the pupil SEE (the colours, the stalls)? HEAR (shoppers, market traders shouting)? SMELL (fresh fish, baking bread)? TASTE (free samples)? FEEL (the cold air, the busy crowd)?

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The market writing has zero sensory details - just empty words like "nice", "stuff", and "good". The Sensory Doctor prescribes: replace each flat word with a specific sensory detail. A market is full of senses (smells, sounds, sights, tastes), so this should be easy to bring alive.
feedback.wrong: The pupil's writing is FLAT - "nice", "stuff", "good" tell us nothing. The Sensory Doctor checks for sensory details. The fix: add what the pupil SAW (colours, stalls), HEARD (shopping noise), SMELT (fresh fish, bread), TASTED (samples), and FELT (cold air, busy crowd).

answer_json.correct: The writing has NO SENSORY DETAILS at all. Words like "nice", "stuff", and "good" tell us nothing. The Sensory Doctor would prescribe: replace these flat words with specifics from the FIVE senses. What did the pupil SEE (the colours, the stalls)? HEAR (shoppers, market traders shouting)? SMELL (fresh fish, baking bread)? TASTE (free samples)? FEEL (the cold air, the busy crowd)?

question_json.question:
Choose the best advice.

SENSORY DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil writes:
"The classroom was loud, very loud, super loud, really loud. It was so loud."

The pupil says: "I have used the sense of hearing five times - is my writing strong now?" What does the Doctor advise?
options: Yes, repeating words shows the sound was very loud

No - the pupil has only used ONE SENSE (hearing) and just keeps repeating "loud". The Sensory Doctor would prescribe: use DIFFERENT senses. What did the noisy classroom LOOK like (children running, papers flying)? SMELL like (chalk, plimsoll bag)? FEEL like (busy bodies bumping past)? TASTE? Variety of senses, not repetition of one word, makes writing strong.
The pupil should remove all the "loud" words
Sound is the only sense that works in classrooms
question_json.correct: No - the pupil has only used ONE SENSE (hearing) and just keeps repeating "loud". The Sensory Doctor would prescribe: use DIFFERENT senses. What did the noisy classroom LOOK like (children running, papers flying)? SMELL like (chalk, plimsoll bag)? FEEL like (busy bodies bumping past)? TASTE? Variety of senses, not repetition of one word, makes writing strong.

feedback.correct: Correct prescription! Repeating "loud" five times is weaker than using different senses ONCE each. The Sensory Doctor advises: keep ONE strong hearing detail (e.g., "the air was full of voices and scraping chairs"), then add SIGHT (children running between desks), SMELL (chalk and plimsolls), and TOUCH (bumping into people). Variety of senses beats repetition of one word.
feedback.wrong: The Sensory Doctor knows that GOOD writing uses different senses, not the same word repeated. "Loud, loud, loud" is weaker than ONE hearing detail plus other senses (sight, smell, touch). Variety brings writing alive; repetition flattens it.

answer_json.correct: No - the pupil has only used ONE SENSE (hearing) and just keeps repeating "loud". The Sensory Doctor would prescribe: use DIFFERENT senses. What did the noisy classroom LOOK like (children running, papers flying)? SMELL like (chalk, plimsoll bag)? FEEL like (busy bodies bumping past)? TASTE? Variety of senses, not repetition of one word, makes writing strong.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on sensory details.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the FIVE senses, (2) why sensory details make writing stronger, and (3) ONE example of a sentence YOU could write using 3 or more senses.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 2 Reflection

Example reflection:

"The five senses are sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Sensory details make writing stronger because they put the reader inside the moment - they can almost see, hear, and feel what is happening. One sentence I could write: 'In Nan's warm kitchen, I smelled fresh apple pie, heard the kettle whistling, and felt the soft cushion of her old armchair.'"
question_json.prompt:
The five senses are...
Sensory details make writing stronger because...
A sentence I could write is...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on sensory details.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the FIVE senses, (2) why sensory details make writing stronger, and (3) ONE example of a sentence YOU could write using 3 or more senses.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE FIVE SENSES - sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each one gives different information about the world.

2. WHY SENSORY DETAILS MAKE WRITING STRONGER - they put the reader INSIDE the moment. Without senses, writing stays flat ("the kitchen was nice"). With senses, the reader feels they are right there with you.

3. ONE OF YOUR OWN EXAMPLES - a sentence using 3 or more senses. Pick a place you know well. Write what you SEE, HEAR, and SMELL there. The more specific, the stronger.

Example sentence: "In Grandad's shed, I smelled sawdust and oil, heard the radio buzzing on a low shelf, and felt the rough wood of the workbench under my hands." This uses SMELL (sawdust, oil), HEARING (radio buzzing), and TOUCH (rough wood).

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE FIVE SENSES - sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch. Each one gives different information about the world.

2. WHY SENSORY DETAILS MAKE WRITING STRONGER - they put the reader INSIDE the moment. Without senses, writing stays flat ("the kitchen was nice"). With senses, the reader feels they are right there with you.

3. ONE OF YOUR OWN EXAMPLES - a sentence using 3 or more senses. Pick a place you know well. Write what you SEE, HEAR, and SMELL there. The more specific, the stronger.

Example sentence: "In Grandad's shed, I smelled sawdust and oil, heard the radio buzzing on a low shelf, and felt the rough wood of the workbench under my hands." This uses SMELL (sawdust, oil), HEARING (radio buzzing), and TOUCH (rough wood).

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read the sentence:

"Once upon a time, there was a princess in a tall tower."

Is this a PERSONAL story or NOT PERSONAL?
options: Personal - because the storyteller is the princess
NOT PERSONAL - this is a made-up story. Personal stories are about REAL things that happened to YOU. "Once upon a time" is the start of a fictional (made-up) story, not a personal one.
It is both at the same time
It cannot be a story at all
question_json.correct: NOT PERSONAL - this is a made-up story. Personal stories are about REAL things that happened to YOU. "Once upon a time" is the start of a fictional (made-up) story, not a personal one.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Once upon a time" is the start of a NOT PERSONAL story. Personal stories tell what really happened to YOU. Made-up stories like fairy tales are not personal but they can still be wonderful to read - we can connect them to our own lives.
feedback.wrong: A personal story is something that REALLY happened to YOU. "Once upon a time, there was a princess" is a made-up story, so it is NOT PERSONAL. We will read both kinds in this lesson and find connections between them.

answer_json.correct: NOT PERSONAL - this is a made-up story. Personal stories are about REAL things that happened to YOU. "Once upon a time" is the start of a fictional (made-up) story, not a personal one.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read the short story below. WHO is mentioned in the story?

"Sam woke up early. He had cereal for breakfast at home. Then Sam went to the library with his dad. They read books together."
options: Sam AND his dad - both are PEOPLE in the story. To find the WHO, look for names and family words like dad, mum, brother, friend.
question_json.correct: Sam AND his dad - both are PEOPLE in the story. To find the WHO, look for names and family words like dad, mum, brother, friend.

feedback.correct: Correct! The PEOPLE in this story are SAM and his DAD. People in stories are usually named characters and family words like dad, mum, brother, friend, granny.
feedback.wrong: WHO means the people in the story. This story has Sam AND his dad. The library is a PLACE, not a person. Always read carefully to find all the people mentioned.

answer_json.correct: Sam AND his dad - both are PEOPLE in the story. To find the WHO, look for names and family words like dad, mum, brother, friend.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this excerpt from "My Saturday":

"My Saturday was brilliant. I woke up early and had toast for breakfast. Then I went to the park with my brother."

Where did the storyteller go?

"My Saturday was brilliant. I woke up early and had toast for breakfast. Then I went to the park with my brother."
options: The PARK with their brother. The story tells us "I went to the park" - that is the WHERE. Reading carefully helps us find all the WHERE answers in any story.
question_json.correct: The PARK with their brother. The story tells us "I went to the park" - that is the WHERE. Reading carefully helps us find all the WHERE answers in any story.

feedback.correct: Correct! The storyteller went to the PARK with their brother. WHERE is the place where things happen in a story. Looking for these "place words" helps us picture the story in our minds.
feedback.wrong: The storyteller went to the PARK. Always read carefully and look for "place words" - the park, home, the library, the shops, school. These tell us WHERE the story happens.

answer_json.correct: The PARK with their brother. The story tells us "I went to the park" - that is the WHERE. Reading carefully helps us find all the WHERE answers in any story.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct answer.

Read this sentence:

"I woke up early."

What does the time word "EARLY" mean?
options: AT THE START of the day, in the morning. "Early" means near the beginning of something - early morning means the first part of the day. Time words help us understand WHEN things happen.
question_json.correct: AT THE START of the day, in the morning. "Early" means near the beginning of something - early morning means the first part of the day. Time words help us understand WHEN things happen.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Early" means at the START of something - usually early morning. Time words like "early", "then", "after", "later", "in the evening" tell us WHEN things happen and help us follow the story.

question_json.question:
Write 4-6 sentences. Use TIME WORDS (early, then, after, in the evening) to show the order. Include WHO was there, WHERE they went, and WHAT they did.

Reference text: "My Saturday was brilliant. I woke up early and had toast. Then I went to the park with my brother. We played football. After lunch, I visited grandma and baked cookies. In the evening, I read my favourite book."

Retell "My Saturday" in YOUR OWN words.

Example retelling:

"On Saturday morning, I woke up early and ate toast for breakfast. Then I went to the park with my brother. We played football together. After lunch, I went to my grandma's house. We baked cookies in her kitchen. In the evening, I read my dinosaur book at home."
question_json.prompt:
Early in the day...
Then...
After lunch...
In the evening...
question_json.instruction:
Write 4-6 sentences. Use TIME WORDS (early, then, after, in the evening) to show the order. Include WHO was there, WHERE they went, and WHAT they did.

Reference text: "My Saturday was brilliant. I woke up early and had toast. Then I went to the park with my brother. We played football. After lunch, I visited grandma and baked cookies. In the evening, I read my favourite book."
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong retelling does THREE things:

1. USES TIME WORDS - early, then, after, in the evening. These show the order.

2. INCLUDES WHO, WHERE, WHAT - the storyteller and brother (WHO), park and grandma's house (WHERE), playing football and baking cookies (WHAT).

3. KEEPS EVENTS IN THE RIGHT ORDER - what happened in the morning comes first, evening last.

Example: "On Saturday morning, the storyteller woke up early and ate toast. Then they went to the park with their brother and played football. After lunch, they visited grandma and baked cookies together. In the evening, they read a dinosaur book and felt happy and tired."

Notice the time words: morning, then, after lunch, in the evening. They guide the reader through the day.

question_json.question:
Pick ONE element to change: the place (park → ???), the person (brother → ???), the food (cookies → ???), or the activity (football → ???). Then rewrite that part with your change.

Write 3-4 sentences showing your changed version.

Now CHANGE ONE THING about "My Saturday". Keep the rest of the story the same.

Example - changing the activity (football → swimming):

"After breakfast, I went to the swimming pool with my brother. We swam in the cool water for an hour. We splashed each other and laughed. After lunch, I still visited grandma and baked cookies, just like in the original story."
question_json.prompt: I am changing... My new version: ...
question_json.instruction:
Pick ONE element to change: the place (park → ???), the person (brother → ???), the food (cookies → ???), or the activity (football → ???). Then rewrite that part with your change.

Write 3-4 sentences showing your changed version.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong change-one-thing rewrite does TWO things:

1. CHANGES ONE element clearly - you might swap the park for the beach, the brother for a friend, the cookies for cake, or football for cycling.

2. KEEPS THE REST THE SAME - the structure of the day (morning, then, after lunch, evening) stays. Only the one thing you chose changes.

Example - changing the place (park → seaside):
"On Saturday I woke up early and had toast. Then I went to the SEASIDE with my brother (instead of the park). We built a sandcastle and paddled in the sea. After lunch, I still visited grandma and baked cookies. In the evening, I read my book."

Notice that ONLY the place changed (park → seaside). The brother is still there, lunch is still lunch, grandma is still grandma. The story still works because only one thing was changed.

question_json.question:
Pick a day - My Sunday, My Monday, My Friday. Write 5-6 sentences using time words (early, then, after, in the evening). Include who was with you, where you went, what you did, and how you felt.

Write YOUR OWN "My [Day]" story following the same pattern.

Example - "My Sunday":

"My Sunday was lovely. Early in the morning, I woke up and had cereal with my mum. Then we walked to the park to feed the ducks. After lunch, I played a board game with my sister and we laughed a lot. In the evening, I helped Dad cook dinner. I felt happy and full as I went to bed."
question_json.prompt:
My [day] was...
Early...
Then...
After lunch...
In the evening...
I felt...
question_json.instruction: Pick a day - My Sunday, My Monday, My Friday. Write 5-6 sentences using time words (early, then, after, in the evening). Include who was with you, where you went, what you did, and how you felt.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong "My [Day]" story does FOUR things:

1. USES TIME WORDS - early, then, after, in the evening. These structure the day.

2. INCLUDES WHO, WHERE, WHAT - real or imagined people, places, activities.

3. SHARES A FEELING - happy, tired, surprised, proud.

4. SOUNDS LIKE YOU - it is YOUR day, told in your voice.

Example: "My Sunday was peaceful. Early in the morning, I made breakfast with my dad - we had pancakes. Then we walked to the library and chose new books. After lunch, I drew pictures of dragons in my room. In the evening, I read one of my new books in the garden. I felt calm and happy."

Notice the time words guide the reader. The WHO (dad), WHERE (library, garden), WHAT (pancakes, books, drawings) bring the day alive. The FEELING (calm, happy) tells the reader how the day was for you.

The Connection Doctor would prescribe: pick a SPECIFIC event from the story (the football match, the baking, the book) and link it to a SPECIFIC time in YOUR life.
The pupil should not connect at all

The Connection Doctor would prescribe: pick a SPECIFIC event from the story (the football match, the baking, the book) and link it to a SPECIFIC time in YOUR life.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! Vague connections are weak. The Connection Doctor prescribes: pick something SPECIFIC from the story (the football, the baking, the book), then link it to something SPECIFIC in your life. "I play football at the park every Saturday with MY brother" is a strong connection - "I exist on Saturdays" is not.
feedback.wrong: The connection is too vague. The Connection Doctor checks for SPECIFIC links between story and life. "I exist on Saturdays" is not a connection - everyone exists. A real connection: "I baked cookies with MY grandma when I was 6, just like Sam did with his grandma."

answer_json.correct: The connection is TOO VAGUE. "Existing on Saturdays" is something everyone does - it does not link a SPECIFIC story event to a SPECIFIC personal experience. question_json.question:
Choose the best advice.

CONNECTION DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil reads "My Saturday" and says:
"I have nothing in common with this story. I have never been to a park, I do not have a brother, I do not have a grandma nearby, and I do not read books for fun."

The pupil thinks they cannot connect at all. What does the Connection Doctor advise?
options: The pupil is right - they cannot connect

Connections do not require IDENTICAL experiences. The Connection Doctor advises: look for SIMILAR experiences. Have you ever had breakfast (like Sam)? Spent time with ANY family member (cousin, uncle, grandparent)? Played ANY sport or game outside? Read ANY story? The pupil can almost certainly find connections - they need to look for SIMILAR experiences, not identical ones.
Tell the pupil to read a different story
There is no need for connections
question_json.correct: Connections do not require IDENTICAL experiences. The Connection Doctor advises: look for SIMILAR experiences. Have you ever had breakfast (like Sam)? Spent time with ANY family member (cousin, uncle, grandparent)? Played ANY sport or game outside? Read ANY story? The pupil can almost certainly find connections - they need to look for SIMILAR experiences, not identical ones.

feedback.correct: Correct prescription! Connections do not require IDENTICAL experiences - SIMILAR is enough. The Connection Doctor advises: instead of "I have never X", look for "I have done SOMETHING LIKE X". Park → garden, swimming pool, beach. Brother → cousin, friend, sister. Grandma → any older family member or neighbour. Reading → any kind of story (TV, audio, picture book).
feedback.wrong: The Connection Doctor advises: connections need SIMILAR experiences, not identical ones. The pupil who has never been to a park might have been to a playground, a garden, or a beach. The pupil who has no brother might have a sister, cousin, or friend. Looking for similar (not identical) experiences opens up real connections.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on connections.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) what a CONNECTION between a story and your life is, (2) what makes a STRONG connection (specific) versus a weak one (vague), and (3) ONE strong connection between "My Saturday" and YOUR life.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 3 Reflection

Example reflection:

"A connection links something in a story to something in MY life. A strong connection is SPECIFIC - it links one exact event in the story to one exact event in my life. A weak connection is too vague (like 'I am a person too'). One strong connection between 'My Saturday' and my life is: just like the storyteller, I play football with my cousin every Saturday at the park near our house."
question_json.prompt:
A connection links...
A strong connection is...
My connection: ...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on connections.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) what a CONNECTION between a story and your life is, (2) what makes a STRONG connection (specific) versus a weak one (vague), and (3) ONE strong connection between "My Saturday" and YOUR life.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. WHAT A CONNECTION IS - a link between something in a story and something in your own life. Reading becomes more meaningful when you can see yourself in the story.

2. STRONG VERSUS WEAK - strong connections are SPECIFIC. They link ONE event in the story to ONE event in your life. Weak connections are too vague (like "I exist too" or "stories are stories"). The more specific, the stronger.

3. YOUR OWN STRONG CONNECTION - pick one specific moment from "My Saturday" (the toast breakfast, the football, the grandma visit, the cookies, the bedtime book) and link it to one specific moment in your life. The more details you include, the stronger.

Example: "Just like the storyteller, I have toast for breakfast on Saturdays. My toast is plum jam on white bread, and I eat it sitting at the kitchen table while my dog watches me. The story's breakfast and mine both feel cosy and slow - that is what Saturdays are like."

options: BEGINNING - this sentence introduces WHO (Humpty Dumpty), WHAT he is (a smooth round egg), and WHERE he lives (on a high wall). That is what beginnings do.
End - because the wall is high
question_json.correct: BEGINNING - this sentence introduces WHO (Humpty Dumpty), WHAT he is (a smooth round egg), and WHERE he lives (on a high wall). That is what beginnings do.

feedback.wrong: A beginning INTRODUCES the character and the setting. This sentence tells us WHO Humpty Dumpty is and WHERE he lives - that is what beginnings do.

MIDDLE - this is where the ACTION happens. Humpty wobbles, loses his balance, and falls. The main action of the story is in the middle.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is the MIDDLE. The action is happening - Humpty wobbles, loses his balance, and falls. Middles are where the main action takes place.
feedback.wrong: The MIDDLE is where the ACTION happens. The wobbling, losing balance, and falling are all action - that means we are in the middle of the story.

question_json.question:
Choose the right order.

Look at these THREE Humpty Dumpty events. Which order is CORRECT?
options: Humpty falls → Humpty sits on the wall → King's men try to help (this is the WRONG order - the fall cannot happen before he sits there)
Humpty sits on the wall → Humpty falls → King's men try to help. This is the CORRECT order. First Humpty sits on the wall (beginning), then he falls (middle), then the men try to help (end).
King's men try to help → Humpty falls → Humpty sits on the wall (this is BACKWARDS - it goes from end to beginning)
question_json.correct: Humpty sits on the wall → Humpty falls → King's men try to help. This is the CORRECT order. First Humpty sits on the wall (beginning), then he falls (middle), then the men try to help (end).

feedback.correct: Correct! Events must come in the right order. First Humpty sits on the wall (beginning). Then he falls (middle). Then the king's men try to help (end). If we put them in any other order, the story would not make sense.
feedback.wrong: Events go in time order: BEGINNING then MIDDLE then END. First Humpty sits on the wall. Then he falls. Then the king's men try to help. If you swap any of these, the story stops making sense.

answer_json.correct: Humpty sits on the wall → Humpty falls → King's men try to help. This is the CORRECT order. First Humpty sits on the wall (beginning), then he falls (middle), then the men try to help (end).

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Why do stories follow the order Beginning → Middle → End?
options: Because that is the order events HAPPEN in time. Stories follow time. First something starts (beginning), then it happens (middle), then it finishes (end). Without this order, stories would not make sense.
Because it is the only way to write
question_json.correct: Because that is the order events HAPPEN in time. Stories follow time. First something starts (beginning), then it happens (middle), then it finishes (end). Without this order, stories would not make sense.

feedback.correct: Correct! Stories follow the order events happen in time. First the story starts (beginning), then it unfolds (middle), then it finishes (end). The order matches how time works - so the reader can follow the story.
feedback.wrong: Stories follow the order events happen in time. Time goes forwards: first this, then that, then the end. If you tell events out of order, the reader cannot follow the story.

answer_json.correct: Because that is the order events HAPPEN in time. Stories follow time. First something starts (beginning), then it happens (middle), then it finishes (end). Without this order, stories would not make sense.

question_json.question:

A pupil writes:

The events are in the WRONG ORDER. The pupil starts with the breaking (which is the END), then mentions the sitting on the wall (which is the BEGINNING). The correct order is: sit on wall (B) → fall and break (M) → horses try to help (E).
question_json.correct: The events are in the WRONG ORDER. The pupil starts with the breaking (which is the END), then mentions the sitting on the wall (which is the BEGINNING). The correct order is: sit on wall (B) → fall and break (M) → horses try to help (E).

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The events are out of order. The pupil started with the END, then jumped to the BEGINNING. The reader cannot follow the story this way. The correct order is: sit on wall → fall → horses try to help.
feedback.wrong: The events are in the WRONG ORDER. Stories must go BEGINNING → MIDDLE → END. Here the pupil started with the breaking (end) before the sitting (beginning). Re-order: sit on wall, fall, horses try to help.

answer_json.correct: The events are in the WRONG ORDER. The pupil starts with the breaking (which is the END), then mentions the sitting on the wall (which is the BEGINNING). The correct order is: sit on wall (B) → fall and break (M) → horses try to help (E).

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

A pupil writes a story:

options: The MIDDLE - we have the start (puppy ran out the gate) and the end (went to bed upset). But we do not know what HAPPENED between - did the pupil look for the puppy? Did the puppy come home? The middle is the action.
question_json.correct: The MIDDLE - we have the start (puppy ran out the gate) and the end (went to bed upset). But we do not know what HAPPENED between - did the pupil look for the puppy? Did the puppy come home? The middle is the action.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The MIDDLE is missing. We have the beginning (puppy ran out) and the end (went to bed upset) but no middle. What happened in between? Did the family search? Did neighbours help? The middle is where the ACTION goes.
feedback.wrong: The MIDDLE is missing. We jump from "puppy ran out" (beginning) to "went to bed upset" (end). The action between - searching, calling, hoping - is the middle. Without the middle, the story has a hole in it.

answer_json.correct: The MIDDLE - we have the start (puppy ran out the gate) and the end (went to bed upset). But we do not know what HAPPENED between - did the pupil look for the puppy? Did the puppy come home? The middle is the action.

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

A pupil writes:

"On Saturday morning at the park, my friend and I tried to fly a kite. We pulled the string and ran. The kite went up high!"

What part is MISSING?

question_json.correct: The END - we have the beginning (Saturday morning at the park, with friend) and middle (pulling the string, running, kite went up). But we do not know how it FINISHED. Did the kite fly all afternoon? Did the string break? The end shows how things turned out.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The END is missing. We have the beginning (Saturday morning, the park, friends) and the middle (running, kite going up). But how did it finish? An ending tells us what happened in the end - the string snapped, the kite flew all afternoon, they put it away, etc.
feedback.wrong: The END is missing. The story has a beginning and middle but no ending. The reader does not know what happened in the end. Adding "the kite flew until lunchtime" or "the string snapped" would give it an ending.

answer_json.correct: The END - we have the beginning (Saturday morning at the park, with friend) and middle (pulling the string, running, kite went up). But we do not know how it FINISHED. Did the kite fly all afternoon? Did the string break? The end shows how things turned out.

question_json.question:
Write 1-2 sentences. Tell us WHEN (yesterday morning, after lunch), WHERE (at home, in the playground), and WHO was with you.

Think about something that happened to you yesterday. Write the BEGINNING.

Example beginnings:

"Yesterday morning at school, my best friend Tom and I were waiting in the playground before the bell."
"Last night after tea, I was in the kitchen helping Mum bake biscuits."
"Yesterday afternoon at the park, my brother and I tried to climb the big oak tree."
question_json.prompt: Yesterday [when]... at/in [where]... [who] [what was happening].
question_json.instruction: Write 1-2 sentences. Tell us WHEN (yesterday morning, after lunch), WHERE (at home, in the playground), and WHO was with you.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong beginning sentence sets up THREE things:

1. WHEN - yesterday morning, after school, last night, yesterday at lunchtime

2. WHERE - the place: at school, in the kitchen, at the park, in my bedroom

3. WHO - you and others: me and my brother, Mum and I, my best friend and I

Example: "Yesterday morning at school, my best friend Tom and I were waiting in the playground before the bell."

This sentence tells us WHEN (yesterday morning), WHERE (at school, in the playground), and WHO (me and Tom). Now the reader is ready for the MIDDLE - what HAPPENED.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong beginning sentence sets up THREE things:

1. WHEN - yesterday morning, after school, last night, yesterday at lunchtime

2. WHERE - the place: at school, in the kitchen, at the park, in my bedroom

3. WHO - you and others: me and my brother, Mum and I, my best friend and I

Example: "Yesterday morning at school, my best friend Tom and I were waiting in the playground before the bell."

This sentence tells us WHEN (yesterday morning), WHERE (at school, in the playground), and WHO (me and Tom). Now the reader is ready for the MIDDLE - what HAPPENED.

question_json.question:
Write 2-3 sentences. The middle is where the action happens. What did you do? What did others do? What was the main thing that took place?

Now write the MIDDLE of the same story. What HAPPENED?

Example middle (continuing the playground story):

"Tom dared me to skip on one foot all the way across the playground. I tried, wobbled, and fell over. Tom laughed so hard he fell over too! We were both still on the ground when the bell rang."
question_json.prompt: First we... then... and then...
question_json.instruction: Write 2-3 sentences. The middle is where the action happens. What did you do? What did others do? What was the main thing that took place?
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong middle does TWO things:

1. SHOWS THE ACTION - what you did, what happened, in order. Use words like "first", "then", "after that", "suddenly", "next".

2. ADDS DETAILS - what you saw, heard, felt, said. Details bring the action alive.

Example (continuing the kitchen story):
"First Mum measured out the flour, and a cloud of white dust puffed up. Then I cracked the eggs into the bowl, and one shell got mixed in. We laughed as we picked the shell out together."

Notice the time-order words (first, then, and) AND the details (cloud of dust, shell mixed in, laughing). Both help the middle come alive.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong middle does TWO things:

1. SHOWS THE ACTION - what you did, what happened, in order. Use words like "first", "then", "after that", "suddenly", "next".

2. ADDS DETAILS - what you saw, heard, felt, said. Details bring the action alive.

Example (continuing the kitchen story):
"First Mum measured out the flour, and a cloud of white dust puffed up. Then I cracked the eggs into the bowl, and one shell got mixed in. We laughed as we picked the shell out together."

Notice the time-order words (first, then, and) AND the details (cloud of dust, shell mixed in, laughing). Both help the middle come alive.

question_json.question:
Write 1-2 sentences. The end shows how the story finished and how you felt.

Now write the END of your story. How did it FINISH?

Example endings (continuing the playground story):

"In the end, we walked into class with grass stains on our knees and big grins on our faces. We were both still giggling when the teacher started the register."
question_json.prompt: In the end... or At the end... I felt...
question_json.instruction: Write 1-2 sentences. The end shows how the story finished and how you felt.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong ending does ONE OR MORE of these:

1. SHOWS HOW IT FINISHED - the last thing that happened

2. SHARES HOW YOU FELT - happy, proud, tired, surprised, embarrassed

3. ROUNDS OFF THE STORY - so the reader has a sense of completion

Example (continuing the kitchen story):
"By the time the biscuits came out of the oven, the kitchen smelled wonderful. Mum and I sat at the table and ate two each, fresh and warm."

This ending shows HOW IT FINISHED (biscuits out, eaten warm), shares a FEELING (wonderful), and ROUNDS OFF the story.

Now you have a complete story: BEGINNING + MIDDLE + END.
