question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

SEQUENCE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil writes:
"I went home tired and happy. We played football all afternoon. Yesterday, my dad and I went to the park."

What is the diagnosis?
options: The story is fine - it has all three parts

The events are in the WRONG ORDER. The pupil has put the END first, then the MIDDLE, then the BEGINNING. The Sequence Doctor would prescribe: re-order the sentences so they go BEGINNING → MIDDLE → END. The correct order is: "Yesterday, my dad and I went to the park. We played football all afternoon. I went home tired and happy."
The story has too many sentences
The story should be about something else
question_json.correct: The events are in the WRONG ORDER. The pupil has put the END first, then the MIDDLE, then the BEGINNING. The Sequence Doctor would prescribe: re-order the sentences so they go BEGINNING → MIDDLE → END. The correct order is: "Yesterday, my dad and I went to the park. We played football all afternoon. I went home tired and happy."

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The pupil has put the parts in the WRONG ORDER (end-middle-beginning). The Sequence Doctor prescribes: re-order so the parts go BEGINNING → MIDDLE → END. Time always moves forwards in stories.
feedback.wrong: The Sequence Doctor checks if events are in time order. Here, the pupil has reversed it: end first, then middle, then beginning. The correct order is "Yesterday, my dad and I went to the park" (B) → "We played football all afternoon" (M) → "I went home tired and happy" (E).

answer_json.correct: The events are in the WRONG ORDER. The pupil has put the END first, then the MIDDLE, then the BEGINNING. The Sequence Doctor would prescribe: re-order the sentences so they go BEGINNING → MIDDLE → END. The correct order is: "Yesterday, my dad and I went to the park. We played football all afternoon. I went home tired and happy."

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

SEQUENCE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil writes:
"Last weekend at my Nan's house, I helped bake a cake. The cake was lovely!"

The pupil says: "I have a beginning and an end, so my story must be complete." What is the doctor's diagnosis?
options: Yes the story is complete - leave it alone

The MIDDLE is missing. The pupil has the beginning ("at Nan's house, I helped bake a cake") and the end ("the cake was lovely!"), but no middle showing the ACTION. The Sequence Doctor would prescribe: add 2-3 sentences showing what happened during the baking - measuring, mixing, putting it in the oven. The middle is where the action goes.
Take out the part about Nan
question_json.correct: The MIDDLE is missing. The pupil has the beginning ("at Nan's house, I helped bake a cake") and the end ("the cake was lovely!"), but no middle showing the ACTION. The Sequence Doctor would prescribe: add 2-3 sentences showing what happened during the baking - measuring, mixing, putting it in the oven. The middle is where the action goes.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! Beginning + End is NOT enough - the story needs a MIDDLE too. The Sequence Doctor prescribes: add 2-3 sentences in the middle showing the ACTION of baking - "First we measured the flour. Then I mixed the batter while Nan added the eggs. Finally we put it in the oven and waited." That is the middle.
feedback.wrong: A complete story has Beginning + Middle + End. This pupil has B + E but no M. The Sequence Doctor prescribes: add the middle - the ACTION of baking. Mixing, measuring, putting in the oven - that is what middles contain.

answer_json.correct: The MIDDLE is missing. The pupil has the beginning ("at Nan's house, I helped bake a cake") and the end ("the cake was lovely!"), but no middle showing the ACTION. The Sequence Doctor would prescribe: add 2-3 sentences showing what happened during the baking - measuring, mixing, putting it in the oven. The middle is where the action goes.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on story sequencing.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the three parts every story needs, (2) why ORDER matters, and (3) ONE thing you will remember when sequencing your own stories.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 4 Reflection

Example reflection:

"Every story needs a beginning, a middle, and an end. The ORDER matters because that is how time works - first something starts, then it happens, then it finishes. If you mix up the order, the story does not make sense. When I write my own stories, I will always check that I have all three parts AND that they are in the right order."
question_json.prompt:
Every story needs...
The order matters because...
When I write, I will...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on story sequencing.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the three parts every story needs, (2) why ORDER matters, and (3) ONE thing you will remember when sequencing your own stories.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE THREE PARTS - Beginning, Middle, End. Every story needs all three. Beginning sets up the who/where/when. Middle is the action. End shows how it finished.

2. WHY ORDER MATTERS - because time works in one direction. First something starts, then it happens, then it ends. If you tell events out of order, the reader cannot follow the story.

3. WHAT YOU WILL REMEMBER - your specific takeaway. Examples: "I will check I have all three parts" / "I will write down events in time order" / "I will not skip the middle". Be specific about what YOU will do.

answer_json.modelAnswer:

2. WHY ORDER MATTERS - because time works in one direction. First something starts, then it happens, then it ends. If you tell events out of order, the reader cannot follow the story.

3. WHAT YOU WILL REMEMBER - your specific takeaway. Examples: "I will check I have all three parts" / "I will write down events in time order" / "I will not skip the middle". Be specific about what YOU will do.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

In Lesson 4 you learned that stories have THREE parts: Beginning, Middle, and End. In Lesson 5 we look more closely at the MIDDLE. The full story has FIVE parts. What are they?
options: Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. The middle of a story actually has THREE smaller parts: build-up (action growing), problem (the difficulty), and resolution (how it gets solved). Together with beginning and end, these make 5 parts.
question_json.correct: Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. The middle of a story actually has THREE smaller parts: build-up (action growing), problem (the difficulty), and resolution (how it gets solved). Together with beginning and end, these make 5 parts.

feedback.correct: Correct! The 5 parts of a story are Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. The middle of a simple story is actually made of three smaller parts: build-up (where the action grows), problem (the main difficulty), and resolution (how it gets solved). Knowing all 5 helps you write more interesting stories.
feedback.wrong: The 5 parts of a story are Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. Beginning sets up the story. Build-up is where the action grows. Problem is the main difficulty. Resolution is how the problem is solved. End shows how the story finished.

answer_json.correct: Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. The middle of a story actually has THREE smaller parts: build-up (action growing), problem (the difficulty), and resolution (how it gets solved). Together with beginning and end, these make 5 parts.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What does the BUILD-UP of a story do?
options: It tells us how the story finished

It BUILDS the action up to the problem. The build-up is where things start happening - characters do things, the action grows, and the reader feels something exciting is coming. It comes after the beginning and before the problem.
It introduces all the characters again
It is just a copy of the beginning
question_json.correct: It BUILDS the action up to the problem. The build-up is where things start happening - characters do things, the action grows, and the reader feels something exciting is coming. It comes after the beginning and before the problem.

feedback.correct: Correct! The BUILD-UP grows the action. After the beginning sets up who and where, the build-up shows characters doing things and the action growing - building up towards the main problem. Without a build-up, the story jumps straight from "introduction" to "problem" with no warning.
feedback.wrong: The BUILD-UP grows the action up to the main problem. It comes after the beginning and before the problem. Characters do things, the action grows, and the reader senses something is coming. The build-up gives the story TENSION before the problem hits.

answer_json.correct: It BUILDS the action up to the problem. The build-up is where things start happening - characters do things, the action grows, and the reader feels something exciting is coming. It comes after the beginning and before the problem.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is the PROBLEM (sometimes called the "dilemma") in a story?
options: The MAIN DIFFICULTY a character faces. Without a problem, there is no story - because nothing needs solving. The problem is the most exciting and important moment of the story. The reader wants to find out what will happen.
question_json.correct: The MAIN DIFFICULTY a character faces. Without a problem, there is no story - because nothing needs solving. The problem is the most exciting and important moment of the story. The reader wants to find out what will happen.

feedback.correct: Correct! The PROBLEM is the main difficulty - the heart of the story. It is what the character must face or solve. Stories live or die on their problem - if the problem is small or boring, the story is small or boring too. Strong problems make readers want to know what happens next.
feedback.wrong: The PROBLEM is the main difficulty the character faces. It is the most important part of the story because it is what the whole story is ABOUT. Without a problem, there is no story. The problem is what the reader most wants to find out about.

answer_json.correct: The MAIN DIFFICULTY a character faces. Without a problem, there is no story - because nothing needs solving. The problem is the most exciting and important moment of the story. The reader wants to find out what will happen.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct part.

Read this part of "The Tortoise and the Hare":

"In a green meadow, there lived a slow tortoise and a quick hare. Every animal in the meadow knew the hare was the fastest of them all."

Which part of the story is this?

"In a green meadow, there lived a slow tortoise and a quick hare. Every animal in the meadow knew the hare was the fastest of them all."
options: BEGINNING - this introduces WHO (slow tortoise, quick hare), WHERE (the green meadow), and gives us the basic SETUP we need to follow the story. That is what beginnings do.
question_json.correct: BEGINNING - this introduces WHO (slow tortoise, quick hare), WHERE (the green meadow), and gives us the basic SETUP we need to follow the story. That is what beginnings do.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is the BEGINNING. It introduces the characters (slow tortoise, quick hare) and the setting (green meadow). Beginnings always set up who and where - so the reader is ready for what happens next.
feedback.wrong: This is the BEGINNING. It introduces the WHO (slow tortoise, quick hare) and WHERE (green meadow). Beginnings set up the basic information we need before the action starts.

answer_json.correct: BEGINNING - this introduces WHO (slow tortoise, quick hare), WHERE (the green meadow), and gives us the basic SETUP we need to follow the story. That is what beginnings do.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct part.

Read this part of the story:

"The hare laughed at the tortoise every day. 'You are so slow!' he would tease. The tortoise grew tired of being teased. One day, he had had enough."

Which part of the story is this?

"The hare laughed at the tortoise every day. 'You are so slow!' he would tease. The tortoise grew tired of being teased. One day, he had had enough."
options: BUILD-UP - this shows the action GROWING. The hare keeps teasing, the tortoise keeps getting upset, and tension is building until something will happen. The build-up grows the story towards its main problem.
question_json.correct: BUILD-UP - this shows the action GROWING. The hare keeps teasing, the tortoise keeps getting upset, and tension is building until something will happen. The build-up grows the story towards its main problem.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is the BUILD-UP. The action is growing - the hare keeps teasing, the tortoise grows tired of it, and we sense something is going to happen. The build-up grows tension before the main problem hits.
feedback.wrong: This is the BUILD-UP. The action is growing - the daily teasing, the tortoise getting more upset, the feeling that something is about to happen. Build-ups grow tension. They make the reader want to know what will happen next.

answer_json.correct: BUILD-UP - this shows the action GROWING. The hare keeps teasing, the tortoise keeps getting upset, and tension is building until something will happen. The build-up grows the story towards its main problem.

"'I challenge you to a race!' said the tortoise. The animals gasped. The hare laughed louder than ever. 'A race? You? Against me? Yes - I accept!'"
options: PROBLEM - the tortoise has set up the main difficulty: a race between him (slow) and the hare (fast). This is the main thing the story is about. The reader now wants to know who will win.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is the PROBLEM. The tortoise has challenged the hare to a race - and the slow tortoise racing the fast hare is the main difficulty of the story. The reader now wants to know what will happen. Problems are usually where the most exciting moment of the story is set up.
feedback.wrong: This is the PROBLEM. The race itself - slow tortoise versus fast hare - is the main difficulty of the story. The whole story is about this race. Problems are usually announced in a clear moment, and they are what make the reader want to keep reading.

answer_json.correct: PROBLEM - the tortoise has set up the main difficulty: a race between him (slow) and the hare (fast). This is the main thing the story is about. The reader now wants to know who will win.

question_json.question:
Choose the right order.

Look at these story events. What is the CORRECT order?
options: Problem → Resolution → Beginning → Build-up → End (this is BACKWARDS)

Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. Stories follow time forwards. First the setup (beginning). Then tension grows (build-up). Then the main difficulty (problem). Then how it gets solved (resolution). Then how the story finishes (end).
question_json.correct: Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. Stories follow time forwards. First the setup (beginning). Then tension grows (build-up). Then the main difficulty (problem). Then how it gets solved (resolution). Then how the story finishes (end).

feedback.correct: Correct! The order is Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. Stories always follow this shape because it follows time. First we meet who and where (beginning). Then tension grows (build-up). Then the difficulty arrives (problem). Then the difficulty is solved (resolution). Then the story finishes (end).
feedback.wrong: The 5-part order is Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. Stories follow this shape because it follows TIME. Out of order, stories do not make sense. Always: introduction first, action growing, the problem, how it is solved, how it finishes.

answer_json.correct: Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. Stories follow time forwards. First the setup (beginning). Then tension grows (build-up). Then the main difficulty (problem). Then how it gets solved (resolution). Then how the story finishes (end).

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Why is the BUILD-UP important?
options: It is just there to make stories longer

It creates TENSION - the build-up makes the reader feel something is coming. Without a build-up, the problem appears suddenly with no warning, which feels jarring. The build-up grows excitement so the problem feels important when it arrives.
It tells the reader the answer in advance
It is the same as the resolution
question_json.correct: It creates TENSION - the build-up makes the reader feel something is coming. Without a build-up, the problem appears suddenly with no warning, which feels jarring. The build-up grows excitement so the problem feels important when it arrives.

feedback.correct: Correct! The BUILD-UP creates TENSION - it makes the reader sense that something is coming. Without a build-up, problems jump out of nowhere and feel jarring. With a build-up, the problem feels earned - the reader has been getting ready for it.
feedback.wrong: The BUILD-UP creates TENSION. It makes the reader feel that something is coming. Stories without a build-up feel jarring because the problem appears with no warning. The build-up is where good stories grow excitement before the problem arrives.

answer_json.correct: It creates TENSION - the build-up makes the reader feel something is coming. Without a build-up, the problem appears suddenly with no warning, which feels jarring. The build-up grows excitement so the problem feels important when it arrives.

Read this story. Which part is MISSING?

"In the woods lived a hungry rabbit. He saw a delicious carrot in a farmer's garden. The carrot was the best he had ever eaten."
options: The PROBLEM and RESOLUTION are missing - we go straight from the rabbit seeing the carrot (build-up) to him eating it (end). HOW did he get past the farmer? Was there a danger? The story is missing the MAIN DIFFICULTY and how it was solved.
question_json.correct: The PROBLEM and RESOLUTION are missing - we go straight from the rabbit seeing the carrot (build-up) to him eating it (end). HOW did he get past the farmer? Was there a danger? The story is missing the MAIN DIFFICULTY and how it was solved.

feedback.correct: Correct! The PROBLEM and RESOLUTION are missing. We jump from the rabbit seeing the carrot (build-up) straight to enjoying it (end) - but we do not know how he got past the farmer, the dog, or any danger. Without a problem and resolution, the story is too easy and not very interesting.
feedback.wrong: The PROBLEM and RESOLUTION are missing. The story jumps from "rabbit sees carrot" to "rabbit eats carrot" with no main difficulty in between. To fix this: add a problem (a farmer or dog) and a resolution (how the rabbit got past). That is what makes a story interesting.

answer_json.correct: The PROBLEM and RESOLUTION are missing - we go straight from the rabbit seeing the carrot (build-up) to him eating it (end). HOW did he get past the farmer? Was there a danger? The story is missing the MAIN DIFFICULTY and how it was solved.

question_json.question:

"Sam wanted to win the spelling bee. The other children laughed and said he could not. He made a tiny mistake and lost. He went home sad."
options: The RESOLUTION is missing. We have the beginning (Sam wants to win), build-up (other children laughing), problem (mistake, lost), and end (went home sad). But there is no RESOLUTION - HOW did Sam respond to the loss? Did he try again? Did he learn something? The resolution shows growth.
question_json.correct: The RESOLUTION is missing. We have the beginning (Sam wants to win), build-up (other children laughing), problem (mistake, lost), and end (went home sad). But there is no RESOLUTION - HOW did Sam respond to the loss? Did he try again? Did he learn something? The resolution shows growth.

feedback.correct: Correct! The RESOLUTION is missing. We have beginning, build-up, problem, and end - but no resolution. A strong story would show HOW Sam responded to losing: did he practise more for next year? Did he discover that doing his best was what mattered? Resolutions show growth or learning. Without one, the story leaves the reader feeling flat.
feedback.wrong: The RESOLUTION is missing. We see Sam fail and go home sad - but we do not see HOW he handled the loss. A resolution might show Sam practising more, finding his real talent, or learning that doing his best is enough. Resolutions show growth - they answer "and what came of all this?"

answer_json.correct: The RESOLUTION is missing. We have the beginning (Sam wants to win), build-up (other children laughing), problem (mistake, lost), and end (went home sad). But there is no RESOLUTION - HOW did Sam respond to the loss? Did he try again? Did he learn something? The resolution shows growth.

question_json.question:
Write 3-4 sentences. Beginning: introduce a character and setting. Build-up: show the action growing.

Use this prompt: "A child wants to be in the school play."

Plan the BEGINNING and BUILD-UP of a story.

Example beginning + build-up:

"BEGINNING: Lily loved drama club. Every Tuesday, she stayed after school to do acting games.

BUILD-UP: When Mrs Khan announced auditions for the school play, Lily practised her lines every night. She read them at breakfast, said them in the bath, and even whispered them in her sleep. By Friday, she was ready."
question_json.prompt:
Beginning: ...
Build-up: ...
question_json.instruction:
Write 3-4 sentences. Beginning: introduce a character and setting. Build-up: show the action growing.

Use this prompt: "A child wants to be in the school play."
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong beginning + build-up does TWO things:

1. BEGINNING - introduces who and where in 1-2 sentences. Lily, a child who loves drama club. The setting is school, especially Tuesday afternoons.

2. BUILD-UP - grows the action towards the problem in 2-3 sentences. Lily practises her lines: morning, bath, sleep. The build-up shows her getting more and more invested. Tension is growing - by Friday, she is ready, but we sense something is going to happen.

The build-up should give the reader a hint that something is coming. Practising hard is good - but stories are more interesting when difficulty arrives. The reader is wondering: what is going to go wrong?

Example: "BEGINNING: Tom always wanted to be in the school play, just like his older brother. BUILD-UP: When auditions were announced, Tom learned his lines every evening. He stood in front of the mirror, practising his voice. By the day of the audition, he could say his lines without thinking."

2. BUILD-UP - grows the action towards the problem in 2-3 sentences. Lily practises her lines: morning, bath, sleep. The build-up shows her getting more and more invested. Tension is growing - by Friday, she is ready, but we sense something is going to happen.

The build-up should give the reader a hint that something is coming. Practising hard is good - but stories are more interesting when difficulty arrives. The reader is wondering: what is going to go wrong?

Example: "BEGINNING: Tom always wanted to be in the school play, just like his older brother. BUILD-UP: When auditions were announced, Tom learned his lines every evening. He stood in front of the mirror, practising his voice. By the day of the audition, he could say his lines without thinking."

question_json.question:
Write 3-4 sentences. Problem: the main difficulty (something goes wrong). Resolution: how the problem gets solved.

Continuing your story about the school play.

Now write the PROBLEM and RESOLUTION of the same story.

Example problem + resolution:

"PROBLEM: On the day of the audition, Lily lost her voice! She woke up with a sore throat and could only whisper. She felt like all her practice had been for nothing.

RESOLUTION: Lily decided to try anyway. She drank warm honey tea, took deep breaths, and stood on the stage. When her turn came, her voice came back - just enough. She used her face and gestures to make her words feel important, and Mrs Khan smiled and gave her the part."
question_json.prompt:
Problem: ...
Resolution: ...
question_json.instruction:
Write 3-4 sentences. Problem: the main difficulty (something goes wrong). Resolution: how the problem gets solved.

Continuing your story about the school play.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong problem + resolution does TWO things:

1. PROBLEM - the MAIN DIFFICULTY in 1-2 sentences. Lily loses her voice on the day of the audition. This is the main thing the story is about - the moment everything could fall apart. Strong problems are big enough that the reader cares.

2. RESOLUTION - HOW the problem gets solved in 2-3 sentences. Lily drinks tea, breathes deeply, and goes on stage anyway. Her voice comes back enough, and her acting wins her the part. Resolutions usually involve the character DOING SOMETHING - making a choice, taking action, finding a way through.

Example: "PROBLEM: On audition day, Tom froze on stage. He could not remember a single line. The other children stared, and his face went red. RESOLUTION: Tom took a deep breath and remembered his older brother's advice: "Just look at one friendly face." He looked at his teacher, who was smiling. The lines came back to him, and he finished his audition strong."

Notice how the resolution shows the character GROWING - they find something inside themselves to overcome the problem.

2. RESOLUTION - HOW the problem gets solved in 2-3 sentences. Lily drinks tea, breathes deeply, and goes on stage anyway. Her voice comes back enough, and her acting wins her the part. Resolutions usually involve the character DOING SOMETHING - making a choice, taking action, finding a way through.

Example: "PROBLEM: On audition day, Tom froze on stage. He could not remember a single line. The other children stared, and his face went red. RESOLUTION: Tom took a deep breath and remembered his older brother's advice: "Just look at one friendly face." He looked at his teacher, who was smiling. The lines came back to him, and he finished his audition strong."

Notice how the resolution shows the character GROWING - they find something inside themselves to overcome the problem.

question_json.question:
Write 1-2 sentences. The END shows how the whole story finished off and what changed for the character.

Now write the END of the same story.

Example end (continuing the school play story):

"On opening night, Lily took the stage with confidence. As the audience clapped at the end, she realised that she did not just love drama - she could really do it."
question_json.prompt: In the end... or At the end... [character] ...
question_json.instruction: Write 1-2 sentences. The END shows how the whole story finished off and what changed for the character.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong end does TWO things:

1. SHOWS HOW THE STORY FINISHED OFF - the last thing that happened (Lily on opening night, the audience applauding).

2. SHOWS WHAT CHANGED - what is different about the character now, compared to the beginning? Lily started loving drama club; she ends knowing she can really act. The character has grown.

Example: "On opening night, Tom delivered every line perfectly. Walking off the stage to thunderous applause, he knew he would never freeze again."

Good ends are short. They do not retell the story - they just round it off and show change. The reader closes the book feeling satisfied because the character ends in a different (usually better) place than they started.

Notice that this is now a COMPLETE 5-part story:
- BEGINNING: Lily/Tom loves drama, wants to be in the play
- BUILD-UP: practises every day
- PROBLEM: lost voice / froze on stage
- RESOLUTION: pushed through anyway, used what they had
- END: opening night, knew they could really act

Example: "On opening night, Tom delivered every line perfectly. Walking off the stage to thunderous applause, he knew he would never freeze again."

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

STORY STRUCTURE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil writes:
"There was a dog. The dog was happy. The end."

The pupil says: "I have a beginning, middle, and end - my story is complete!"

What is the doctor's diagnosis?
options: The pupil is right - the story is complete

The pupil has confused HAPPINESS with PROBLEM. The story has NO PROBLEM (no difficulty) and NO RESOLUTION (nothing got solved). Without a problem, there is no real story - just a description. The Story Structure Doctor would prescribe: give the dog a real difficulty (lost his bone, fell ill, separated from owner) and show HOW the difficulty got solved.
question_json.correct: The pupil has confused HAPPINESS with PROBLEM. The story has NO PROBLEM (no difficulty) and NO RESOLUTION (nothing got solved). Without a problem, there is no real story - just a description. The Story Structure Doctor would prescribe: give the dog a real difficulty (lost his bone, fell ill, separated from owner) and show HOW the difficulty got solved.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! Without a PROBLEM and RESOLUTION, this is not a story - it is just a description of a happy dog. The Story Structure Doctor prescribes: give the dog a real difficulty (lost his bone, fell ill, ran away from home), grow the action (build-up), then show how the difficulty got SOLVED. Stories need conflict and change to be stories.
feedback.wrong: The Story Structure Doctor checks for ALL 5 PARTS. This story has only a beginning and an end - no build-up, no problem, no resolution. To fix: give the dog a real PROBLEM (something goes wrong), build it up, and show HOW the problem got solved. Without conflict, there is no story.

answer_json.correct: The pupil has confused HAPPINESS with PROBLEM. The story has NO PROBLEM (no difficulty) and NO RESOLUTION (nothing got solved). Without a problem, there is no real story - just a description. The Story Structure Doctor would prescribe: give the dog a real difficulty (lost his bone, fell ill, separated from owner) and show HOW the difficulty got solved.

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

STORY STRUCTURE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil writes:
"Sara wanted to win the swimming race. She trained every day for months. On the day of the race, she dived into the pool and swam. She won the race! She felt happy."

The pupil says: "I have all 5 parts!" Is the diagnosis correct?
options: Yes - the story has all 5 parts

NO - the PROBLEM is missing. The pupil has beginning (Sara wanted to win), build-up (she trained), and resolution+end (won and felt happy) - but no PROBLEM. Nothing went wrong. The race was easy because there was no real difficulty. The Story Structure Doctor prescribes: add something that goes wrong on race day - a cramp, a faster opponent, a sudden storm. The reader needs to feel that winning was NOT certain.
question_json.correct: NO - the PROBLEM is missing. The pupil has beginning (Sara wanted to win), build-up (she trained), and resolution+end (won and felt happy) - but no PROBLEM. Nothing went wrong. The race was easy because there was no real difficulty. The Story Structure Doctor prescribes: add something that goes wrong on race day - a cramp, a faster opponent, a sudden storm. The reader needs to feel that winning was NOT certain.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The PROBLEM is missing. Sara wanted to win, trained, raced, won, felt happy - but nothing actually went wrong. There is no DIFFICULTY for her to overcome. The Story Structure Doctor prescribes: add a problem (cramp, faster opponent, storm, doubt) so the reader feels Sara had to FIGHT for the win. Stories without problems are flat.
feedback.wrong: The PROBLEM is MISSING. Sara faces no real difficulty - she just trains and wins. The Story Structure Doctor prescribes: add something going wrong on race day. She gets a cramp halfway through. The strongest opponent breaks ahead. A storm makes the pool rough. Without difficulty, there is no real story.

answer_json.correct: NO - the PROBLEM is missing. The pupil has beginning (Sara wanted to win), build-up (she trained), and resolution+end (won and felt happy) - but no PROBLEM. Nothing went wrong. The race was easy because there was no real difficulty. The Story Structure Doctor prescribes: add something that goes wrong on race day - a cramp, a faster opponent, a sudden storm. The reader needs to feel that winning was NOT certain.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on story structure.

4-5 sentences. Cover: (1) the FIVE parts of a story IN ORDER, (2) why the PROBLEM is the most important part, and (3) ONE story idea you would like to write next, with a clear problem.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 5 Reflection

Example reflection:

"Stories have FIVE parts: Beginning, Build-up, Problem, Resolution, and End. The PROBLEM is the most important part because without a problem, there is no real story - just a description. The build-up grows tension before the problem hits, and the resolution shows the problem being solved. The story idea I want to write next is about a child who loses their pet at a busy park. The PROBLEM is finding the pet before it gets hurt. The RESOLUTION is using clever clues - the pet's favourite treat, calling its name in the right voice, asking other children for help."
question_json.prompt:
Stories have five parts: ...
The problem is most important because...
My story idea: ...
My problem: ...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on story structure.

4-5 sentences. Cover: (1) the FIVE parts of a story IN ORDER, (2) why the PROBLEM is the most important part, and (3) ONE story idea you would like to write next, with a clear problem.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE FIVE PARTS - Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. They go in this order. Beginning sets up who and where. Build-up grows the action. Problem is the main difficulty. Resolution is how it gets solved. End is how the story finishes.

2. WHY THE PROBLEM IS MOST IMPORTANT - because without a problem, there is no story. The problem is what the WHOLE STORY is about. Strong problems make readers want to keep reading. Weak problems (or missing ones) make stories feel flat.

3. YOUR OWN STORY IDEA WITH A CLEAR PROBLEM - pick something with REAL difficulty. The pet got lost at the park. The school cake fell on the floor before the bake-off. The new boy at school could not understand English yet. Difficulty is what makes a story.

Example story idea: "A child wants to be the best player on their football team. The PROBLEM is they keep missing important goals because they are afraid of letting the team down. The RESOLUTION is learning that bravery is not about never being afraid - it is about playing on anyway. The end shows them scoring the winning goal in the next match."

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE FIVE PARTS - Beginning → Build-up → Problem → Resolution → End. They go in this order. Beginning sets up who and where. Build-up grows the action. Problem is the main difficulty. Resolution is how it gets solved. End is how the story finishes.

2. WHY THE PROBLEM IS MOST IMPORTANT - because without a problem, there is no story. The problem is what the WHOLE STORY is about. Strong problems make readers want to keep reading. Weak problems (or missing ones) make stories feel flat.

3. YOUR OWN STORY IDEA WITH A CLEAR PROBLEM - pick something with REAL difficulty. The pet got lost at the park. The school cake fell on the floor before the bake-off. The new boy at school could not understand English yet. Difficulty is what makes a story.

Example story idea: "A child wants to be the best player on their football team. The PROBLEM is they keep missing important goals because they are afraid of letting the team down. The RESOLUTION is learning that bravery is not about never being afraid - it is about playing on anyway. The end shows them scoring the winning goal in the next match."

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Stories can make us feel different emotions. Which of these are the THREE main story types we will learn about?
options: Happy, Sad, Funny - the three main feelings stories give us. Happy stories make us feel good when problems are solved. Sad stories make us feel sorry when something is lost. Funny stories make us laugh.
question_json.correct: Happy, Sad, Funny - the three main feelings stories give us. Happy stories make us feel good when problems are solved. Sad stories make us feel sorry when something is lost. Funny stories make us laugh.

feedback.correct: Correct! The three story types are Happy, Sad, and Funny. Each makes the reader feel a different way. Happy stories make us feel good. Sad stories make us feel sorry. Funny stories make us laugh.
feedback.wrong: The three main story types are Happy, Sad, and Funny. They are different from each other in how they make the reader feel.

answer_json.correct: Happy, Sad, Funny - the three main feelings stories give us. Happy stories make us feel good when problems are solved. Sad stories make us feel sorry when something is lost. Funny stories make us laugh.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct answer.

Read the story below. What type of story is "The Lion and the Mouse"?

Once, a mighty lion was sleeping in the jungle when a tiny mouse ran across his nose. The lion woke up and caught the mouse. "Please let me go!" squeaked the mouse. "One day I might help you!" The lion laughed but let the mouse go. Later, hunters caught the lion in a net. The little mouse heard the lion roaring. The mouse gnawed through the ropes and freed the lion. "Thank you, little friend!" said the lion. From that day on, they were best friends.
options: Happy - because the lion is saved and they become friends. The problem (lion trapped) is solved, and the ending is good for everyone.
Sad - because the lion was caught in a net
Funny - because the mouse is very small
question_json.correct: Happy - because the lion is saved and they become friends. The problem (lion trapped) is solved, and the ending is good for everyone.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a happy story because the problem is solved and everyone feels good at the end. The lion was caught in a net (the problem), the mouse helped him escape (the solution), and they became friends (the happy ending).
feedback.wrong: Look at the ending. The lion is freed and they become friends. When a problem is solved and everyone feels good, that is a happy story.

answer_json.correct: Happy - because the lion is saved and they become friends. The problem (lion trapped) is solved, and the ending is good for everyone.

question_json.question:
Choose the BEST answer.

What makes a story HAPPY?
options: The problem is solved and everyone feels safe and good. Happy stories give the reader a warm feeling because everything works out in the end.
question_json.correct: The problem is solved and everyone feels safe and good. Happy stories give the reader a warm feeling because everything works out in the end.

feedback.correct: Correct! A happy story is happy because the PROBLEM is solved and everyone feels safe and good. The story makes us feel pleased when we read the ending.
feedback.wrong: A happy story is more than just having nice characters. The problem in the story must be SOLVED and the ending must make everyone feel safe and good.

answer_json.correct: The problem is solved and everyone feels safe and good. Happy stories give the reader a warm feeling because everything works out in the end.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read the start of "The Ugly Duckling". Why is this a SAD story?

A little duckling hatched from an egg. He looked different from his brothers and sisters. The other animals laughed at him. "You are so ugly!" they said. The duckling felt very sad and lonely. He swam away by himself, feeling that nobody wanted him.
options: Because the duckling feels lonely and unwanted, and the problem is not solved. The duckling has nobody to play with and feels he does not belong.
question_json.correct: Because the duckling feels lonely and unwanted, and the problem is not solved. The duckling has nobody to play with and feels he does not belong.

feedback.correct: Correct! This part of the story is sad because the duckling feels lonely and unwanted. The other animals are unkind to him. He has no friends and no place where he feels he belongs. Those feelings make a story sad.
feedback.wrong: A story is sad when characters feel hurt, lonely, or lose something. The duckling feels lonely and unwanted, and nobody is helping him. That is what makes this part of the story sad.

answer_json.correct: Because the duckling feels lonely and unwanted, and the problem is not solved. The duckling has nobody to play with and feels he does not belong.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Which of these things makes a story SAD?
options: Everyone is laughing and having fun
A character feels lonely or left out, someone loses something important, or the problem does not get solved. These are all things that make readers feel sorry for the characters.
There are lots of animals in the story
The story has a happy ending
question_json.correct: A character feels lonely or left out, someone loses something important, or the problem does not get solved. These are all things that make readers feel sorry for the characters.

feedback.correct: Correct! Sad stories often have characters who feel lonely or hurt, who lose something important, or whose problems do not get solved. The reader feels sorry for the characters.
feedback.wrong: Sad stories make us feel sorry for the characters. They often involve a character feeling lonely, losing something important, or facing a problem that does not get solved.

answer_json.correct: A character feels lonely or left out, someone loses something important, or the problem does not get solved. These are all things that make readers feel sorry for the characters.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this rhyme. Why is "Humpty Dumpty" a sad story?

Humpty Dumpty sat on a wall. Humpty Dumpty had a great fall. All the king's horses and all the king's men couldn't put Humpty together again.
options: Because Humpty Dumpty had a high seat on the wall
Because Humpty Dumpty falls and breaks, and nobody can fix him - the problem is NOT solved. Even with all the king's horses and men trying, Humpty stays broken.
Because horses and men ran around
question_json.correct: Because Humpty Dumpty falls and breaks, and nobody can fix him - the problem is NOT solved. Even with all the king's horses and men trying, Humpty stays broken.

feedback.correct: Correct! Humpty Dumpty is a sad story because Humpty falls, breaks, and nobody can fix him. The problem is NOT solved. Even all the king's horses and men cannot put him together again. That unsolved problem is what makes the story sad.
feedback.wrong: Humpty Dumpty is sad because the problem is NOT solved. Humpty falls, he breaks, and nobody can fix him. When a problem cannot be solved at the end, the story is sad.

answer_json.correct: Because Humpty Dumpty falls and breaks, and nobody can fix him - the problem is NOT solved. Even with all the king's horses and men trying, Humpty stays broken.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Look at "The Three Little Pigs" (a happy story where the pigs stay safe in the brick house). How could we change it to make it SAD?
options: The wolf blows down all three houses and the pigs have nowhere to live. The problem (wolf) is NOT solved, and the pigs lose their homes.
question_json.correct: The wolf blows down all three houses and the pigs have nowhere to live. The problem (wolf) is NOT solved, and the pigs lose their homes.

feedback.correct: Correct! To make the story sad, we change the ending so the problem is NOT solved. If the wolf wins and the pigs lose their homes, the reader feels sorry for the pigs. The story becomes sad.
feedback.wrong: To make a happy story sad, change the ending so the problem is NOT solved. The pigs would have to LOSE their houses for the story to become sad.

answer_json.correct: The wolf blows down all three houses and the pigs have nowhere to live. The problem (wolf) is NOT solved, and the pigs lose their homes.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this story of "The Gingerbread Man". Why is this a FUNNY story?

Once upon a time, a little old woman made a gingerbread man. As soon as he was baked, he jumped up and ran away! "Run, run, as fast as you can! You can't catch me, I'm the Gingerbread Man!" he shouted. He ran away from the old woman, the old man, a cow, and a horse. Then he met a clever fox. "Jump on my back and I will help you cross the river," said the fox. The Gingerbread Man jumped on, and the fox ate him in one big gulp!
options: Because the Gingerbread Man is silly and cheeky, and the ending is surprising. We do not expect a piece of gingerbread to run away, and we do not expect the cheeky one to be tricked himself!
Because it has animals in it
Because the old woman bakes things
question_json.correct: Because the Gingerbread Man is silly and cheeky, and the ending is surprising. We do not expect a piece of gingerbread to run away, and we do not expect the cheeky one to be tricked himself!

feedback.correct: Correct! This story is funny because the gingerbread man is silly (gingerbread cannot really run), cheeky (he teases everyone), and the ending is a SURPRISE (the cheeky one is the one who gets tricked). Surprising endings often make stories funny.
feedback.wrong: Funny stories often have silly or cheeky characters and surprising twists. The Gingerbread Man is silly because he runs and talks, and the ending surprises us when the fox tricks him.

answer_json.correct: Because the Gingerbread Man is silly and cheeky, and the ending is surprising. We do not expect a piece of gingerbread to run away, and we do not expect the cheeky one to be tricked himself!

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Which of these things can make a story FUNNY?
options: Characters do silly or unexpected things, the story has surprising or clever twists, and characters say or do things that make us laugh. All three are common features of funny stories.
Characters cry the whole way through
The same thing repeats with no change
question_json.correct: Characters do silly or unexpected things, the story has surprising or clever twists, and characters say or do things that make us laugh. All three are common features of funny stories.

feedback.correct: Correct! Funny stories use silly characters, surprising twists, and laugh-out-loud moments. Sometimes a funny story uses just one of these. The best funny stories use all three.
feedback.wrong: Funny stories make us laugh. They do this through silly characters, surprising twists, and laugh-out-loud moments. Stories that make us cry or are very predictable are usually NOT funny.

answer_json.correct: Characters do silly or unexpected things, the story has surprising or clever twists, and characters say or do things that make us laugh. All three are common features of funny stories.

pairs.right: Happy - the small helper saves the day, then they become friends

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this story. What type of story is "The Boy Who Cried Wolf"?

A shepherd boy looked after sheep on a hill. He was bored, so he shouted "Wolf! Wolf!" The villagers ran to help, but there was no wolf. The boy laughed. He did this again and again. Then one day, a real wolf came! The boy shouted "Wolf! Wolf!" but nobody came to help because they did not believe him. The wolf ate some of the sheep.
options: Sad - the boy loses his sheep because of his lies. The problem is NOT solved at the end - the wolf takes the sheep, and we feel sorry for the boy who has nobody to trust him.
question_json.correct: Sad - the boy loses his sheep because of his lies. The problem is NOT solved at the end - the wolf takes the sheep, and we feel sorry for the boy who has nobody to trust him.

feedback.correct: Correct! This is a SAD story. The boy plays tricks, but in the end the problem is NOT solved - the wolf takes the sheep. We feel sorry for the boy because his lies have made the villagers stop believing him. The unsolved problem makes the story sad.
feedback.wrong: This is a sad story because the problem is NOT solved at the end. The wolf takes the sheep, and the boy has nobody who believes him. Sad stories often have an unsolved problem.

answer_json.correct: Sad - the boy loses his sheep because of his lies. The problem is NOT solved at the end - the wolf takes the sheep, and we feel sorry for the boy who has nobody to trust him.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Which part of a story USUALLY tells us if it will be happy, sad, or funny?
options: The ending - especially how the problem is solved or NOT solved. The ending is what gives the reader the strong feeling that decides the story type.
question_json.correct: The ending - especially how the problem is solved or NOT solved. The ending is what gives the reader the strong feeling that decides the story type.

feedback.correct: Correct! The ENDING decides the story type. A solved problem makes a happy ending. An unsolved problem makes a sad ending. A surprising or silly twist makes a funny ending. The opening just sets the scene; the ending tells you what type the story is.
feedback.wrong: The opening just starts the story. The ending is what tells you the type. Look at how the PROBLEM is solved (or not solved) - that is what gives the reader the strong feeling that decides the type.

answer_json.correct: The ending - especially how the problem is solved or NOT solved. The ending is what gives the reader the strong feeling that decides the story type.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Read this story of "Goldilocks". What type of story is it?

Goldilocks went into the three bears' house. She tried their porridge, chairs, and beds. When the bears came home, they found her sleeping in Baby Bear's bed. Goldilocks woke up, saw the bears, and ran away home safely. The bears fixed what she had broken.
options: Sad - because the bears were cross with Goldilocks
Funny - because Goldilocks is silly to go into the bears' house, and the surprise of waking up with bears watching her is unexpected and a bit silly.
Just an ordinary story with no clear type
question_json.correct: Funny - because Goldilocks is silly to go into the bears' house, and the surprise of waking up with bears watching her is unexpected and a bit silly.

feedback.correct: Correct! Goldilocks is a funny story. It is silly that Goldilocks goes into the bears' house, tries everything, and then falls asleep. The surprise of her waking up with bears looking at her is unexpected and a bit silly. Both make the story funny.
feedback.wrong: Goldilocks is funny because the character does silly things (goes into a stranger's house, tries everything, falls asleep) and the ending has a surprise (she wakes up with bears watching her). Silly characters and surprising twists make stories funny.

answer_json.correct: Funny - because Goldilocks is silly to go into the bears' house, and the surprise of waking up with bears watching her is unexpected and a bit silly.

pairs.right: Sad - the problem is not solved and the rabbit feels left out

pairs.left: The rabbit tries to jump but falls in the water and gets all muddy!
pairs.right: Funny - silly accident makes us laugh

question_json.question:
Write a 3-4 sentence plan for a HAPPY story. Cover: WHO is the character, WHAT problem they have, and HOW the problem is solved (a happy ending).

Remember: Happy stories have a problem that GETS SOLVED so everyone feels safe and good.

Plan a HAPPY story.

Example plan:

"My character is Lily, a small bird. Her problem is that she has lost her nest in a storm. A kind squirrel helps her build a new nest in a safe tree. Lily is happy in her new home."
question_json.prompt:
My character is...
Their problem is...
The happy ending is...
question_json.instruction:
Write a 3-4 sentence plan for a HAPPY story. Cover: WHO is the character, WHAT problem they have, and HOW the problem is solved (a happy ending).

Remember: Happy stories have a problem that GETS SOLVED so everyone feels safe and good.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong happy story plan covers three things:

1. WHO - a character we can imagine and care about (a bird, a child, an animal, a person).

2. WHAT PROBLEM - the trouble or difficulty the character faces. Without a problem there is no story.

3. HAPPY ENDING - HOW the problem is solved. The ending is what makes the story HAPPY. The problem must get solved (often with help from a friend) and everyone feels safe and good at the end.

Example: "Lily the bird lost her nest in a storm (PROBLEM). A kind squirrel helped her build a new nest in a safe tree (SOLUTION). Lily is happy in her new home (HAPPY ENDING)." Notice how the ending is what makes the story happy.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong happy story plan covers three things:

1. WHO - a character we can imagine and care about (a bird, a child, an animal, a person).

2. WHAT PROBLEM - the trouble or difficulty the character faces. Without a problem there is no story.

3. HAPPY ENDING - HOW the problem is solved. The ending is what makes the story HAPPY. The problem must get solved (often with help from a friend) and everyone feels safe and good at the end.

Example: "Lily the bird lost her nest in a storm (PROBLEM). A kind squirrel helped her build a new nest in a safe tree (SOLUTION). Lily is happy in her new home (HAPPY ENDING)." Notice how the ending is what makes the story happy.

question_json.question:
Write a 3-4 sentence plan for a SAD story. Cover: WHO is the character, WHAT happens to them, and the SAD ENDING (where the problem is NOT solved).

Remember: Sad stories have a problem that does NOT get solved, or someone loses something important.

Plan a SAD story.

Example plan:

"My character is Bear, a lonely teddy. He used to belong to a child who has grown up and moved away. Bear sits in an empty room day after day, hoping someone will come back. Nobody comes."
question_json.prompt:
My character is...
What happens to them is...
The sad ending is...
question_json.instruction:
Write a 3-4 sentence plan for a SAD story. Cover: WHO is the character, WHAT happens to them, and the SAD ENDING (where the problem is NOT solved).

Remember: Sad stories have a problem that does NOT get solved, or someone loses something important.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong sad story plan covers three things:

1. WHO - a character we can feel sorry for. Sad stories work best when the character is a bit lonely, lost, or vulnerable.

2. WHAT HAPPENS - the loss, problem, or difficulty. In sad stories, the problem is often something the character cannot fix on their own.

3. SAD ENDING - the problem is NOT solved. Something is lost. The character is left feeling sad. The reader feels sorry for the character.

Example: "Bear the teddy used to belong to a child who has grown up and moved away (WHO). Bear sits in an empty room day after day (WHAT HAPPENS). Nobody comes back, and Bear is left lonely and forgotten (SAD ENDING)." Notice how the problem is not solved - that is what makes the story sad.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong sad story plan covers three things:

1. WHO - a character we can feel sorry for. Sad stories work best when the character is a bit lonely, lost, or vulnerable.

2. WHAT HAPPENS - the loss, problem, or difficulty. In sad stories, the problem is often something the character cannot fix on their own.

3. SAD ENDING - the problem is NOT solved. Something is lost. The character is left feeling sad. The reader feels sorry for the character.

Example: "Bear the teddy used to belong to a child who has grown up and moved away (WHO). Bear sits in an empty room day after day (WHAT HAPPENS). Nobody comes back, and Bear is left lonely and forgotten (SAD ENDING)." Notice how the problem is not solved - that is what makes the story sad.

question_json.question:
Write a 3-4 sentence plan for a FUNNY story. Cover: WHO is your silly character, WHAT silly thing happens, and the SURPRISING or SILLY ending.

Remember: Funny stories have silly characters, surprising twists, or moments that make us laugh.

Plan a FUNNY story.

Example plan:

"My silly character is Bertie, a banana who loves to dance. He puts on dancing shoes that are too big and slides everywhere. He tries to dance for the queen but slips on a banana skin and lands in her tea. The queen laughs and asks for the silly banana to come back tomorrow."
question_json.prompt:
My silly character is...
What silly thing happens is...
The funny ending is...
question_json.instruction:
Write a 3-4 sentence plan for a FUNNY story. Cover: WHO is your silly character, WHAT silly thing happens, and the SURPRISING or SILLY ending.

Remember: Funny stories have silly characters, surprising twists, or moments that make us laugh.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong funny story plan covers three things:

1. WHO - a silly character. Animals doing human things, food that talks, or any unusual character can work. Bertie the dancing banana is silly because bananas do not dance.

2. WHAT SILLY THING - what the character does that makes us laugh. Often this is the character doing the WRONG thing or a SILLY thing that surprises us.

3. SURPRISING/SILLY ENDING - the SURPRISE that makes us laugh. Often the ending has a twist nobody expected. Bertie slipping on a banana skin (a banana slipping on a banana!) is a clever twist.

Example: "Bertie the dancing banana puts on shoes too big (WHO). He dances for the queen but slips on a banana skin (SILLY THING). The queen laughs and asks for him to come back (FUNNY ENDING)." Notice how the silly twist (a banana slipping on a banana skin) makes us smile.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong funny story plan covers three things:

1. WHO - a silly character. Animals doing human things, food that talks, or any unusual character can work. Bertie the dancing banana is silly because bananas do not dance.

2. WHAT SILLY THING - what the character does that makes us laugh. Often this is the character doing the WRONG thing or a SILLY thing that surprises us.

3. SURPRISING/SILLY ENDING - the SURPRISE that makes us laugh. Often the ending has a twist nobody expected. Bertie slipping on a banana skin (a banana slipping on a banana!) is a clever twist.

Example: "Bertie the dancing banana puts on shoes too big (WHO). He dances for the queen but slips on a banana skin (SILLY THING). The queen laughs and asks for him to come back (FUNNY ENDING)." Notice how the silly twist (a banana slipping on a banana skin) makes us smile.

question_json.question:
Choose the best diagnosis.

STORY TYPE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil reads the story below and says, "I think this is a happy story because everyone gets bread at the end."

Is the pupil right? What is the CORRECT diagnosis?

Little Red Hen found some grains of wheat. "Who will help me plant these?" she asked. "Not I," said the cat, dog, and pig. So she planted them herself. When the wheat was ready, she asked, "Who will help me make bread?" "Not I," they all said. So she made the bread herself. When the delicious bread was ready, everyone wanted to eat it. "Who will help me eat this bread?" asked Little Red Hen. "I will!" they all shouted. "No," said Little Red Hen. "I will eat it myself!" And she did.
options: Yes, the pupil is right. Everyone gets bread.

No, the pupil is wrong. This is a HAPPY story because Little Red Hen ENJOYS her bread, but it is happy for HER, not for everyone. The cat, dog, and pig are left out because they did not help. The story is happy for the helpful one, but it teaches a lesson about being lazy.
No, this is a sad story because nobody shares.
No, this is a funny story because hens cannot really bake.
question_json.correct: No, the pupil is wrong. This is a HAPPY story because Little Red Hen ENJOYS her bread, but it is happy for HER, not for everyone. The cat, dog, and pig are left out because they did not help. The story is happy for the helpful one, but it teaches a lesson about being lazy.

feedback.correct: Correct diagnosis! The pupil missed an important detail - the cat, dog, and pig do NOT get bread because they did not help. The story is happy for Little Red Hen (her problem is solved, she has bread). The lesson is about being helpful, but the story type is HAPPY because the hardworking character gets a good outcome.
feedback.wrong: The Story Type Doctor looks at WHO the story ends well for. Little Red Hen DOES enjoy her bread - she planted, made, and ate it herself. The story is happy because the problem (food) is solved for the helpful character. The cat, dog, and pig miss out because they would not help.

answer_json.correct: No, the pupil is wrong. This is a HAPPY story because Little Red Hen ENJOYS her bread, but it is happy for HER, not for everyone. The cat, dog, and pig are left out because they did not help. The story is happy for the helpful one, but it teaches a lesson about being lazy.

question_json.question:
Choose the best advice.

STORY TYPE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil writes a story:
"Tom the kitten wanted to climb the tall tree. He tried and tried but he could not climb very high. He sat at the bottom of the tree and watched the birds fly above him."

The pupil says, "I want this to be a happy story." What advice would the Story Type Doctor give?
options: The story is already happy. Leave it as it is.

This story is currently SAD because the problem is NOT solved - Tom cannot climb the tree and just sits at the bottom watching. To make it HAPPY, the problem must get SOLVED. The Doctor would advise: add an ending where Tom finds a way to climb (with help from a friend) or finds something even better, so the reader feels good for Tom at the end.
Change the kitten to a dog. Dogs make happier stories.
Make the tree shorter so the problem is easier.
question_json.correct: This story is currently SAD because the problem is NOT solved - Tom cannot climb the tree and just sits at the bottom watching. To make it HAPPY, the problem must get SOLVED. The Doctor would advise: add an ending where Tom finds a way to climb (with help from a friend) or finds something even better, so the reader feels good for Tom at the end.

feedback.correct: Correct prescription! The Story Type Doctor knows that the ENDING decides the story type. Right now Tom is left feeling left out (sad). To make the story happy, the problem (cannot climb) must be SOLVED. Adding a helpful friend or finding something even better turns the sad ending into a happy one.
feedback.wrong: The Story Type Doctor diagnoses the ending. Tom watching the birds without joining them is a SAD ending (problem not solved). To make the story happy, the ending must SOLVE the problem - perhaps a friend helps Tom climb, or Tom finds an even better thing to do. The ending makes the story type.

answer_json.correct: This story is currently SAD because the problem is NOT solved - Tom cannot climb the tree and just sits at the bottom watching. To make it HAPPY, the problem must get SOLVED. The Doctor would advise: add an ending where Tom finds a way to climb (with help from a friend) or finds something even better, so the reader feels good for Tom at the end.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on what you have learned about story types.

Three to four sentences. Cover: (1) what the three story types are, (2) what makes each one different, and (3) which type you find most fun to read or write, and why.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 6 Reflection

Example reflection:

"The three story types are happy, sad, and funny. A happy story has a problem that gets solved. A sad story has a problem that does not get solved. A funny story has silly characters or a surprising twist. I like funny stories the best because they make me laugh, and the surprises always make me smile."
question_json.prompt:
The three story types are...
What makes them different is...
My favourite type is... because...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on what you have learned about story types.

Three to four sentences. Cover: (1) what the three story types are, (2) what makes each one different, and (3) which type you find most fun to read or write, and why.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE THREE TYPES - happy, sad, and funny. Each is different from the others.

2. WHAT MAKES EACH ONE DIFFERENT - the ENDING. Happy stories solve the problem so everyone feels good. Sad stories leave the problem unsolved or have someone losing something important. Funny stories have silly characters or surprising twists.

3. WHAT YOU PREFER - your own answer. There is no wrong choice. Some pupils like happy stories because they leave you feeling good. Some like sad stories because they teach us about feelings. Some like funny stories because laughing is fun. Whatever you choose, say WHY it appeals to you.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. THE THREE TYPES - happy, sad, and funny. Each is different from the others.

2. WHAT MAKES EACH ONE DIFFERENT - the ENDING. Happy stories solve the problem so everyone feels good. Sad stories leave the problem unsolved or have someone losing something important. Funny stories have silly characters or surprising twists.

3. WHAT YOU PREFER - your own answer. There is no wrong choice. Some pupils like happy stories because they leave you feeling good. Some like sad stories because they teach us about feelings. Some like funny stories because laughing is fun. Whatever you choose, say WHY it appeals to you.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is a NOUN?
options: A word that NAMES a PERSON, PLACE, or THING. Nouns are naming words. Examples: farmer (person), barn (place), tractor (thing).
A small word that joins sentences
question_json.correct: A word that NAMES a PERSON, PLACE, or THING. Nouns are naming words. Examples: farmer (person), barn (place), tractor (thing).

feedback.correct: Correct! A noun is a NAMING word. It names a person (farmer, Mum), a place (school, barn), or a thing (tractor, hat). Nouns are everywhere - in fact, every sentence has at least one noun.
feedback.wrong: A NOUN is a naming word - it names a person, place, or thing. Examples: dog (thing), London (place), Sam (person). Action words are verbs (we will learn those next lesson).

1. PERSON - someone in your life. Examples: Mum, my best friend Tom, the postman, my baby sister, Mr Smith my teacher.

2. PLACE - somewhere in your life. Examples: my bedroom, the swimming pool, school, Nan's garden, the local park.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) what a NOUN is, (2) what a DETERMINER does, and (3) ONE thing you will check when writing your own sentences.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 7 Reflection

Example reflection:

"A noun is a NAMING word - it names a person, a place, or a thing. A determiner is a word that comes BEFORE a noun and tells us more about it: which one, how many, or whose. When I write my own sentences, I will always check that singular nouns have determiners before them, and that I use 'an' before words that start with a vowel sound."
question_json.prompt:

question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on nouns and determiners.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) what a NOUN is, (2) what a DETERMINER does, and (3) ONE thing you will check when writing your own sentences.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. WHAT A NOUN IS - a NAMING word. Names a person (farmer), place (barn), or thing (tractor). Without nouns we cannot name anything.

2. WHAT A DETERMINER DOES - comes before a noun and tells us more. Articles (a, an, the) tell us which one. Counting words (three, many) tell us how many. Possessives (my, your, his) tell us whose.

3. ONE THING TO CHECK - your own takeaway. Examples: "I will check every singular noun has a determiner" / "I will use 'an' before vowel sounds" / "I will use 'the' when I mean a specific noun". Be specific about what YOU will do.

lessons.title: Common and Proper Nouns / Plural and Singular

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

In Lesson 7 you learned that NOUNS name people, places, and things. Nouns can be split into TWO types. What are they?
options: COMMON nouns and PROPER nouns. A common noun is a GENERAL name (boy, city, day). A proper noun is a SPECIFIC name (Sam, London, Monday). Proper nouns always start with CAPITAL LETTERS.
question_json.correct: COMMON nouns and PROPER nouns. A common noun is a GENERAL name (boy, city, day). A proper noun is a SPECIFIC name (Sam, London, Monday). Proper nouns always start with CAPITAL LETTERS.

feedback.correct: Correct! Nouns are either COMMON (general names like boy, city, day) or PROPER (specific names like Sam, London, Monday). Proper nouns ALWAYS start with capital letters - that is the rule that links the two ideas.
feedback.wrong: Nouns split into COMMON nouns (general names: boy, city, day) and PROPER nouns (specific names: Sam, London, Monday). The key rule: PROPER nouns always start with CAPITAL LETTERS.

answer_json.correct: COMMON nouns and PROPER nouns. A common noun is a GENERAL name (boy, city, day). A proper noun is a SPECIFIC name (Sam, London, Monday). Proper nouns always start with CAPITAL LETTERS.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is a COMMON noun?
options: A general name for any person, place, or thing of that type. Examples: boy (any boy), city (any city), dog (any dog). Common nouns do NOT need capital letters (unless they are at the start of a sentence).
A noun that is hard to spell
A noun used for important things
question_json.correct: A general name for any person, place, or thing of that type. Examples: boy (any boy), city (any city), dog (any dog). Common nouns do NOT need capital letters (unless they are at the start of a sentence).

feedback.correct: Correct! A COMMON noun is a GENERAL name. "Boy" means any boy. "City" means any city. "Dog" means any dog. Common nouns do not need capitals (unless they start a sentence).
feedback.wrong: A COMMON noun is the GENERAL name for something. Examples: girl (any girl), country (any country), book (any book). Common nouns name a TYPE of thing, not one specific thing.

answer_json.correct: A general name for any person, place, or thing of that type. Examples: boy (any boy), city (any city), dog (any dog). Common nouns do NOT need capital letters (unless they are at the start of a sentence).

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is a PROPER noun?
options: A noun that uses the right grammar
A SPECIFIC name for a particular person, place, or thing. Examples: Sam (one specific boy), London (one specific city), Buddy (one specific dog). Proper nouns ALWAYS start with capital letters.
The first noun in a sentence
question_json.correct: A SPECIFIC name for a particular person, place, or thing. Examples: Sam (one specific boy), London (one specific city), Buddy (one specific dog). Proper nouns ALWAYS start with capital letters.

question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on common/proper nouns and plurals.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the difference between COMMON and PROPER nouns, (2) the connection between PROPER nouns and CAPITAL LETTERS, and (3) ONE irregular plural you will remember.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. COMMON vs PROPER - common nouns are GENERAL (boy, city, day). Proper nouns are SPECIFIC (Sam, London, Monday). Common = many of them; proper = one specific one.

2. PROPER NOUNS AND CAPITALS - the connection. PROPER nouns ALWAYS start with capital letters. That is the visible sign. Common nouns stay lowercase (unless they start a sentence). Capital letter = proper noun.

3. ONE IRREGULAR PLURAL - your own takeaway. Pick one to remember. Examples: child → children, foot → feet, mouse → mice, man → men, woman → women, person → people. Picking ONE makes it easier to remember.

3. ONE IRREGULAR PLURAL - your own takeaway. Pick one to remember. Examples: child → children, foot → feet, mouse → mice, man → men, woman → women, person → people. Picking ONE makes it easier to remember.

question_json.instruction: Each sentence should have a clear action (DOING) verb. Underline (or capitalise) your action verbs.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong response has 3 sentences with 3 DIFFERENT action verbs (DOING words):

Example:
1. "Sam KICKED the ball into the goal." (action: kicked)
2. "I READ a brilliant book yesterday." (action: read)
3. "The cat JUMPED over the fence." (action: jumped)

Good action verbs: ran, jumped, kicked, read, wrote, sang, danced, climbed, baked, painted, drew, played, ate, drank, laughed, cried, helped, worked. Pick verbs that show CLEAR actions - what someone is DOING.

Avoid using the same verb twice. Variety makes your writing stronger.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on verbs.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the difference between ACTION verbs and BEING verbs, (2) how HELPING verbs work with MAIN verbs, and (3) ONE thing you will check when writing your own sentences.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 9 Reflection

Example reflection:

"ACTION verbs show what someone DOES (run, jump, eat). BEING verbs show how someone IS or feels (am, is, are, was, were). HELPING verbs work WITH main verbs to add extra meaning (am running, has finished). When I write my own sentences, I will always check that every sentence has a verb - either an action verb or a being verb."
question_json.prompt:
Action verbs show...
Being verbs show...
Helping verbs work with main verbs to...
When I write, I will check...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on verbs.

3-4 sentences. Cover: (1) the difference between ACTION verbs and BEING verbs, (2) how HELPING verbs work with MAIN verbs, and (3) ONE thing you will check when writing your own sentences.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. ACTION vs BEING - action verbs show DOING (run, jump, eat, write). Being verbs show STATE (am, is, are, was, were). Action = doing. Being = how someone is.

2. HELPING + MAIN VERBS - helping verbs work WITH main verbs. The helping verb (am, is, are, was, were, have, has, had, will, can) comes FIRST. The main verb (the action) comes after. Examples: "is running", "have finished", "will go". Together they give extra meaning - usually about WHEN or WHETHER complete.

3. ONE THING TO CHECK - your specific takeaway. Examples: "I will always check every sentence has a verb" / "I will use being verbs when describing how someone IS" / "I will use helping verbs when actions need extra meaning". Be specific about what YOU will do.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers three things:

1. ACTION vs BEING - action verbs show DOING (run, jump, eat, write). Being verbs show STATE (am, is, are, was, were). Action = doing. Being = how someone is.

2. HELPING + MAIN VERBS - helping verbs work WITH main verbs. The helping verb (am, is, are, was, were, have, has, had, will, can) comes FIRST. The main verb (the action) comes after. Examples: "is running", "have finished", "will go". Together they give extra meaning - usually about WHEN or WHETHER complete.

3. ONE THING TO CHECK - your specific takeaway. Examples: "I will always check every sentence has a verb" / "I will use being verbs when describing how someone IS" / "I will use helping verbs when actions need extra meaning". Be specific about what YOU will do.

question_json.correct: TENSE tells us WHEN the action happened. The action verb CHANGES form depending on whether the action is in the past (already happened), present (happening now), or future (will happen later).

answer_json.correct: TENSE tells us WHEN the action happened. The action verb CHANGES form depending on whether the action is in the past (already happened), present (happening now), or future (will happen later).

feedback.correct: Correct! The three basic tenses are PAST, PRESENT, and FUTURE. Past = already happened. Present = happening now or always. Future = will happen later. Every action you write happens at one of these three times.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on tenses.

4-5 sentences. Cover: (1) the THREE basic tenses (past, present, future), (2) why TENSE CONSISTENCY matters in your writing, (3) ONE thing you will check when writing your own sentences. Then SELECT 2-3 nouns and verbs you would use to start your own personal story.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of Lesson 10 Reflection

This is your FIRST PWP entry! From now on, you will use your PWP Booklet at the end of every lesson.

Example reflection:

"The three basic tenses are PAST (already happened, like "walked"), PRESENT (happening now or always, like "walk"), and FUTURE (will happen, like "will walk"). Tense consistency matters because if I switch tenses in the middle of writing, my reader gets confused about WHEN things are happening. When I write my own sentences, I will always check that all my action verbs match the time. For my personal story, I will use these nouns and verbs: NOUNS = my dog Buddy, the park, my best friend; VERBS = ran, played, laughed."
question_json.prompt:
The three tenses are...
Tense consistency matters because...
When I write, I will check...
For my personal story:
  Nouns: ...
  Verbs: ...
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection on tenses.

4-5 sentences. Cover: (1) the THREE basic tenses (past, present, future), (2) why TENSE CONSISTENCY matters in your writing, (3) ONE thing you will check when writing your own sentences. Then SELECT 2-3 nouns and verbs you would use to start your own personal story.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection covers FOUR things:

1. THE THREE TENSES - PAST (already happened: walked, ate, went), PRESENT (happening now or always: walk, eat, go), FUTURE (will happen: will walk, will eat, will go). Tense tells the reader WHEN.

2. WHY TENSE CONSISTENCY MATTERS - because mixing tenses confuses readers. If you start in past ("I went"), readers expect all the action verbs to stay in past ("ate", "saw", "ran"). Switching tenses without reason breaks the flow and confuses the timeline.

3. ONE THING YOU WILL CHECK - your specific takeaway. Examples: "I will always check time words match my verbs" / "I will pick a tense and stick to it through the whole paragraph" / "I will remember irregular past tenses (go → went, eat → ate, see → saw)".

4. NOUNS AND VERBS FOR YOUR PERSONAL STORY (the PWP launch): pick 2-3 nouns (people, places, things from your life) and 2-3 verbs (action verbs you might use). These will be the building blocks of your story when you start writing it.

PWP starts here. From now on, every lesson ends with you using your booklet.

question_json.question:
