question_json.question:
Which grouping is correct?

Sort these rhetorical devices and structural features into two groups: SPEECH FEATURES (used in speeches but not typical essays) and SHARED FEATURES (used in both speeches and essays).

"Good morning everyone" | subheadings | "Have you ever noticed...?" | three-point structure | "Imagine visiting..." | direct address ("you") | formal vocabulary | repetition for emphasis
options:
Speech features: subheadings, three-point structure, formal vocabulary
Shared features: direct address, rhetorical questions, repetition

Speech features: "Good morning everyone", "Have you ever noticed...?", "Imagine visiting...", direct address ("you"), repetition for emphasis
Shared features: subheadings, three-point structure, formal vocabulary

Speech features: formal vocabulary, subheadings, three-point structure
Shared features: "Good morning", rhetorical questions, direct address
All features are shared between speeches and essays.
question_json.correct:
Speech features: "Good morning everyone", "Have you ever noticed...?", "Imagine visiting...", direct address ("you"), repetition for emphasis
Shared features: subheadings, three-point structure, formal vocabulary

feedback.correct: Correct! Speech-specific: greeting, rhetorical questions, imaginative imperatives ("Imagine"), direct personal address ("you"), repetition for spoken emphasis. Shared: three-point structure, formal vocabulary, subheadings (though speeches use spoken signposts like "Firstly" rather than written subheadings). The speech features all assume a live audience.
feedback.wrong: Speech-specific: greeting ("Good morning"), rhetorical questions, "Imagine" imperatives, direct address ("you"), repetition for spoken emphasis. These all assume a live audience who must be engaged differently from a private reader. Structure and vocabulary can be shared.

answer_json.correct:
Speech features: "Good morning everyone", "Have you ever noticed...?", "Imagine visiting...", direct address ("you"), repetition for emphasis
Shared features: subheadings, three-point structure, formal vocabulary

question_json.question:
Choose the best speech opening.

A pupil is writing a persuasive speech about why schools should have a longer lunch break. Which OPENING is most effective?
options: "I am going to talk to you today about why I think schools should have a longer lunch break. I have three reasons for this."

"Good morning everyone. Raise your hand if you have ever felt stressed, rushed, and hungry all at once. What if I told you that 30 extra minutes every day could change all three of those feelings? Today, I want to convince you that a longer lunch break would transform our school day."
"In this speech, the topic of longer lunch breaks in schools will be discussed. There are several arguments in favour of this proposal."
"Lunch breaks are important for pupils. Some schools have longer breaks than others. I will discuss this subject today."
question_json.correct: "Good morning everyone. Raise your hand if you have ever felt stressed, rushed, and hungry all at once. What if I told you that 30 extra minutes every day could change all three of those feelings? Today, I want to convince you that a longer lunch break would transform our school day."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B has: a direct greeting, a show-of-hands activity that immediately involves the audience, a rhetorical question, and a clear purpose statement. It engages the audience physically and emotionally before stating the subject. Options A, C, and D describe the speech rather than delivering it.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It opens with direct greeting, involves the audience physically (raise your hand), uses a rhetorical question, and states the purpose with energy. Options A and D describe the speech. Option C uses passive voice and essays language ("will be discussed").

answer_json.correct: "Good morning everyone. Raise your hand if you have ever felt stressed, rushed, and hungry all at once. What if I told you that 30 extra minutes every day could change all three of those feelings? Today, I want to convince you that a longer lunch break would transform our school day."

question_json.question:
Click a technique on the left, then its definition on the right.

Match each rhetorical technique to its definition.
question_json.instruction: Click a technique on the left, then its definition on the right.

pairs.right: Repeating a word or phrase for emphasis, often at the start of sentences: "Thank you for...Thank you for...Thank you for..."
Comparing an unfamiliar idea to a familiar one to make it easier to understand: "Your mind works like a muscle"
Using three items or ideas together for rhythm and completeness: "dedication, patience, creativity, and care"
A short personal story that makes an abstract idea vivid and relatable for the audience
Repeating a word or phrase for emphasis, often at the start of sentences: "Thank you for...Thank you for...Thank you for..."
Comparing an unfamiliar idea to a familiar one to make it easier to understand: "Your mind works like a muscle"
Using three items or ideas together for rhythm and completeness: "dedication, patience, creativity, and care"
A short personal story that makes an abstract idea vivid and relatable for the audience

question_json.question:
Identify both problems.

A pupil has written this speech opening:

"Today I am going to tell you about why reading is important. Reading has several benefits. These will be discussed in this speech."

What are the TWO main problems?

"Today I am going to tell you about why reading is important. Reading has several benefits. These will be discussed in this speech."
options: The speech is too short and needs more words.

The opening describes the speech rather than delivering it (no hook, no audience engagement), and the language is passive and impersonal ("will be discussed") rather than active and direct for a live audience.
The speech uses present tense when it should use past tense.
The speech needs more technical vocabulary.
question_json.correct: The opening describes the speech rather than delivering it (no hook, no audience engagement), and the language is passive and impersonal ("will be discussed") rather than active and direct for a live audience.

feedback.correct: Correct! Two problems: no hook (the opening gives the audience no reason to listen), and passive essay-style language ("will be discussed") that belongs on a page rather than in a speaker's voice. Compare with Model 1: "Imagine visiting any place in the world without leaving your chair." That creates immediate desire to hear more.
feedback.wrong: Two problems: no hook (no reason for the audience to engage), and passive essay language ("will be discussed") rather than direct spoken language. Compare: "Today I am going to tell you" vs "Imagine visiting any place in the world without leaving your chair." Which makes you want to listen?

answer_json.correct: The opening describes the speech rather than delivering it (no hook, no audience engagement), and the language is passive and impersonal ("will be discussed") rather than active and direct for a live audience.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this signpost sentence from a three-point speech.
question_json.template: ___, reading teaches you about absolutely everything. Want to know how to build a robot? There is a book for that.
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Secondly" is a spoken signpost. Speech signposts tell the audience exactly where they are in the structure without needing to look at a page. Other signposts from the model texts: Firstly, Thirdly, Finally, First, Second, Third. Always use spoken signposts to help the audience track the speech's argument.

question_json.question:
Choose the best improved version.

Read this speech conclusion:

"So, in conclusion, I have told you about reading, pets, and challenges. I hope you found it interesting. That is the end of my speech. Thank you."

How should this be improved?
options: "And that is all I have to say about this topic. There may be other points I have not mentioned. Thank you very much for listening to me today."
"So I challenge you: choose one book this week. Read it. Discover why millions of people love reading. Your next adventure is waiting for you on a library shelf. Thank you."
"In conclusion, the main points of this speech were: reading, pets, and challenges. These three areas are all important. I hope this was useful."
"Thank you for listening to my speech. I have now finished speaking and will sit down."
question_json.correct: "So I challenge you: choose one book this week. Read it. Discover why millions of people love reading. Your next adventure is waiting for you on a library shelf. Thank you."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B ends with a direct challenge (imperative: choose, read, discover), a vivid specific image ("on a library shelf"), and a memorable closing line that makes the abstract feel concrete. It circles back to the main subject without summarising, and the brevity makes it powerful.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct. It ends with: a direct challenge to the audience (imperative verbs: choose, read, discover), a specific memorable image, and a final line that makes the abstract feel real. Compare with Option A (still hedging) and Option C (flat summary). Speech conclusions must move the audience, not summarise at them.

answer_json.correct: "So I challenge you: choose one book this week. Read it. Discover why millions of people love reading. Your next adventure is waiting for you on a library shelf. Thank you."

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this rhetorical question from the motivational speech.
question_json.template: Imagine if she had given___! J.K. Rowling was rejected by twelve publishers before one agreed to publish her book.
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Imagine if she had given up!" is a rhetorical question in the imperative-imagination form. It asks the audience to picture a world where one of the most successful authors never published. The contrast with what actually happened makes the resilience message more powerful.

question_json.question:
Identify all three improvements.

A pupil is writing a persuasive speech about why their school should have a garden. They have written this body paragraph:

"Gardens are good because plants grow in them. You can grow vegetables and flowers. It is nice to be outside. Some schools have gardens already. Our school does not have one. I think we should get one."

What THREE improvements would most strengthen this paragraph?

"Gardens are good because plants grow in them. You can grow vegetables and flowers. It is nice to be outside. Some schools have gardens already. Our school does not have one. I think we should get one."
options: Add more sentences, include a bibliography, and use longer words.

Add a clear signpost (Firstly/Secondly), replace vague claims ("gardens are good", "it is nice") with specific benefits supported by evidence or analogy, and use direct address to make the argument personal to the audience ("When you grow your own vegetables, you understand where food comes from").
Remove all personal opinions and make the paragraph more formal.
Change the tense from present to past throughout.
question_json.correct: Add a clear signpost (Firstly/Secondly), replace vague claims ("gardens are good", "it is nice") with specific benefits supported by evidence or analogy, and use direct address to make the argument personal to the audience ("When you grow your own vegetables, you understand where food comes from").

feedback.correct: Correct! Three improvements: (1) signpost (Firstly/Secondly makes the structure audible), (2) specific benefits instead of vague claims ("good", "nice" – compared to "pupils who work in school gardens reduce stress by 40%" or "growing food teaches you where it comes from"), and (3) direct address transforming the paragraph from description to persuasion ("when YOU grow your own vegetables").
feedback.wrong: Three improvements needed: signpost to signal position in the speech, specific evidence replacing vague claims, and direct address ("you") transforming description into personal persuasion. "Gardens are good" is easy to dismiss. "When you grow your own vegetables, you understand where food comes from" is not.

answer_json.correct: Add a clear signpost (Firstly/Secondly), replace vague claims ("gardens are good", "it is nice") with specific benefits supported by evidence or analogy, and use direct address to make the argument personal to the audience ("When you grow your own vegetables, you understand where food comes from").

question_json.question:
Write 4-6 sentences. Use: a greeting, a hook that engages the audience, and a purpose statement.

Write the OPENING of a short speech on a topic of your choice. Include: a direct greeting, an opening hook (rhetorical question or imaginative imperative), and a clear statement of your speech's purpose.

Your speech opening must include:
1. A direct greeting (Good morning/afternoon, everyone)
2. An opening hook (rhetorical question OR imaginative imperative: Imagine/Picture/Think about)
3. A clear purpose statement: "Today, I want to [persuade/inform/inspire] you that..."
question_json.prompt: Good morning/afternoon everyone. Imagine / Have you ever / Think about...
question_json.instruction: Write 4-6 sentences. Use: a greeting, a hook that engages the audience, and a purpose statement.
question_json.modelAnswer: Good morning, everyone. Have you ever looked at a piece of litter on the ground and thought, "someone else will pick that up"? Every single day, millions of people make that same choice – and every single day, the problem grows. Today, I want to persuade you that each one of us has the power to make our school, our community, and our planet cleaner. It starts with one small decision.

answer_json.modelAnswer: Good morning, everyone. Have you ever looked at a piece of litter on the ground and thought, "someone else will pick that up"? Every single day, millions of people make that same choice – and every single day, the problem grows. Today, I want to persuade you that each one of us has the power to make our school, our community, and our planet cleaner. It starts with one small decision.

question_json.question:
Choose the most powerful closing line.

Which is the BEST closing line for a motivational speech about overcoming challenges?
options: "And so, in conclusion, I have discussed three ways to overcome challenges, including learning from failure, building resilience, and asking for help. Thank you."
"Your challenges today are creating the successful, confident person you will become tomorrow. Thank you."
"I hope my speech about challenges has been useful and that you will think about the ideas I mentioned. That is all. Thank you."
"Challenges are difficult but important. Remember to try hard and ask for help if needed. Thank you very much."
question_json.correct: "Your challenges today are creating the successful, confident person you will become tomorrow. Thank you."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is powerful because it is short, speaks directly to the audience ("you"), connects present struggle to future success, and ends with confident brevity. It does not summarise or hedge. The closing line of a motivational speech must leave the audience feeling capable, not just informed.
feedback.wrong: Option B is the strongest. It is brief, speaks directly to the audience ("your challenges", "you will become"), connects present difficulty to future success, and is memorable. No summary, no hedging. The closing line must leave the audience feeling something.

answer_json.correct: "Your challenges today are creating the successful, confident person you will become tomorrow. Thank you."

question_json.question:
Tick each feature you can find.

Read this extract from the persuasive speech about reading. Use the checklist to evaluate it.

Good morning everyone. Imagine visiting any place in the world without leaving your chair. Picture yourself travelling back in time to ancient Egypt, or forward to distant planets. How? Simply by opening a book.

Today, I want to persuade you that reading is one of the most important skills you will ever develop. Reading changes your life in three powerful ways.

Firstly, reading develops your imagination and creativity. When you read a story, your brain creates pictures of the characters and settings. Have you ever noticed how a book feels different from its film version? That is because your imagination created something unique.
question_json.instruction: Tick each feature you can find.

question_json.question:
Choose the approach that applies all principles.

A pupil is planning a thank-you speech for their class teacher at the end of the year. Which approach correctly applies all speech writing principles?
options: Write five paragraphs summarising all the lessons covered during the year, using formal academic language throughout.

Open with a warm direct greeting, use a short anecdote about a specific memory (making it personal), structure appreciation around 2-3 specific contributions (not just general praise), use repetition ("thank you for...") for rhythmic emphasis, and close with a memorable final sentence expressing ongoing impact.
"Dear Miss Jones, I am writing to express my appreciation for the work you have done this year. You have been a good teacher. Yours sincerely."
Begin with a list of statistics about teaching hours worked, followed by a bibliography of sources confirming that teaching is a demanding profession.
question_json.correct: Open with a warm direct greeting, use a short anecdote about a specific memory (making it personal), structure appreciation around 2-3 specific contributions (not just general praise), use repetition ("thank you for...") for rhythmic emphasis, and close with a memorable final sentence expressing ongoing impact.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B applies all four speech principles to a thank-you speech: specific anecdote (personal, emotional connection), structured appreciation (not just general praise), repetition ("thank you for...") creating spoken rhythm, and a memorable close. Options A and C are written for the eye not the ear. Option D is an argument, not a thank-you.
feedback.wrong: Option B is correct: specific anecdote, structured appreciation, spoken repetition for rhythm, memorable close. This is a thank-you speech, not a letter (no "Dear", no "Yours sincerely"), not an essay (no bibliography), and not an argument (no statistics defending teaching).

answer_json.correct: Open with a warm direct greeting, use a short anecdote about a specific memory (making it personal), structure appreciation around 2-3 specific contributions (not just general praise), use repetition ("thank you for...") for rhythmic emphasis, and close with a memorable final sentence expressing ongoing impact.

question_json.question:
Use the formula to write ONE speech-style sentence in your PWP Booklet. Change the topic – do not copy the example.

Formula: [Direct address + rhetorical hook] + [main point with evidence] + [call to action or memorable closing]

Example: Have you ever felt that one person cannot make a difference? Today I want to prove to you that every significant change in history began with a single person who refused to accept things as they were – and I challenge you to be that person.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

Formula: [Direct address + rhetorical hook] + [main point with evidence] + [call to action or memorable closing]

Example: Have you ever felt that one person cannot make a difference? Today I want to prove to you that every significant change in history began with a single person who refused to accept things as they were – and I challenge you to be that person.
question_json.prompt: Have you ever... / Imagine... / Think about... + main point + closing challenge or memorable image...
question_json.instruction:
Use the formula to write ONE speech-style sentence in your PWP Booklet. Change the topic – do not copy the example.

Formula: [Direct address + rhetorical hook] + [main point with evidence] + [call to action or memorable closing]

Example: Have you ever felt that one person cannot make a difference? Today I want to prove to you that every significant change in history began with a single person who refused to accept things as they were – and I challenge you to be that person.
question_json.modelAnswer: Good morning everyone. Think about the last time you learned something new, something that surprised you and changed the way you see the world – that is the feeling that a school library gives you every single day, which is exactly why I am asking you to sign our petition to save it before Friday.

answer_json.modelAnswer: Good morning everyone. Think about the last time you learned something new, something that surprised you and changed the way you see the world – that is the feeling that a school library gives you every single day, which is exactly why I am asking you to sign our petition to save it before Friday.

question_json.question:
Choose the best explanation.

What does "show don't tell" mean in descriptive writing?
options: Always use long sentences and lots of adjectives to describe everything in detail.

Instead of STATING a feeling or quality directly ("She was nervous"), you CREATE the feeling through actions, sensory details, and behaviour so the reader experiences it rather than being told about it.
Only describe what a character looks like – their hair colour, eye colour, and height.
Use as many similes as possible in every paragraph.
question_json.correct: Instead of STATING a feeling or quality directly ("She was nervous"), you CREATE the feeling through actions, sensory details, and behaviour so the reader experiences it rather than being told about it.

feedback.correct: Correct! Show don't tell means creating the experience for the reader rather than labelling it. Compare: "He was nervous" (telling) with "His hand left a damp print on the door handle" (showing). The reader feels the nervousness rather than being told it exists.
feedback.wrong: "Show don't tell" means creating the experience through details rather than labelling it. "She was angry" tells. "She slammed the drawer so hard the teacups rattled" shows. The reader feels the anger without being told what to call it.

answer_json.correct: Instead of STATING a feeling or quality directly ("She was nervous"), you CREATE the feeling through actions, sensory details, and behaviour so the reader experiences it rather than being told about it.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct explanation.

Read this sentence:

"His fingers – long, brown, precise – moved across the face of a dismantled clock without any hesitation, as if they could read the metal by touch alone."

What does this sentence show about the clockmaker?

"His fingers – long, brown, precise – moved across the face of a dismantled clock without any hesitation, as if they could read the metal by touch alone."
options: He is a clockmaker who has repaired many clocks in his life.
His extreme skill and confidence – the word "precise", the absence of hesitation, and the simile "as if they could read the metal" all show mastery without the writer ever using the word "skilled".
He has long fingers, which is unusual for a clockmaker.
He is currently repairing a clock in his shop.
question_json.correct: His extreme skill and confidence – the word "precise", the absence of hesitation, and the simile "as if they could read the metal" all show mastery without the writer ever using the word "skilled".

feedback.correct: Correct! The writer never says "he was skilled" or "he was experienced." Instead: "precise" describes the fingers themselves; "without any hesitation" shows confidence through absence of doubt; "as if they could read the metal" is a simile that implies the fingers have almost a separate intelligence. The reader concludes mastery without being told it.
feedback.wrong: "Precise" modifies the fingers, not the clockmaker directly. "Without any hesitation" shows confidence through the absence of doubt. "As if they could read the metal by touch alone" is a simile suggesting supernatural familiarity. Together they show mastery without ever naming it.

answer_json.correct: His extreme skill and confidence – the word "precise", the absence of hesitation, and the simile "as if they could read the metal" all show mastery without the writer ever using the word "skilled".

question_json.question:
Identify the setting description.

Which of these is a SETTING description?
options: "She was small, with dark eyes that missed nothing and hands that were always moving."
"He stood up before he had decided to stand up, which was its own kind of answer."

"The mud stretched out from the bank in ridged, dark sheets – not the grey mud of rain puddles but something older, heavier, carrying in its surface the pressed imprint of every tide that had ever drawn back from it."
"I wonder if she saw me. She must have. Everyone always sees."
question_json.correct: "The mud stretched out from the bank in ridged, dark sheets – not the grey mud of rain puddles but something older, heavier, carrying in its surface the pressed imprint of every tide that had ever drawn back from it."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C describes a physical place using sensory detail (sight: ridged dark mud; age: "something older, heavier") and figurative language (the mud carries the "pressed imprint of every tide"). Options A and B describe a character and action. Option D is internal monologue – a character's thoughts.
feedback.wrong: Option C is a setting description: it describes a place (tidal mudflats) using sensory detail and figurative language. Option A describes a character. Option B shows a character's action revealing emotion. Option D is an internal thought – the character's voice inside their own head.

answer_json.correct: "The mud stretched out from the bank in ridged, dark sheets – not the grey mud of rain puddles but something older, heavier, carrying in its surface the pressed imprint of every tide that had ever drawn back from it."

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

Read this extract:

"Somewhere in the reeds, something small moved with a rapid, papery sound. Oystercatchers, at a distance, called to one another in their short, sharp, certain voices. And over all of it – a silence that was not really silence but the absence of human noise, which is different."

Which SENSE is being primarily used here, and what is the most sophisticated observation in the passage?

"Somewhere in the reeds, something small moved with a rapid, papery sound. Oystercatchers, at a distance, called to one another in their short, sharp, certain voices. And over all of it – a silence that was not really silence but the absence of human noise, which is different."
options: Sight. The most sophisticated observation is identifying the oystercatchers by name.

Sound. The most sophisticated observation is the distinction between silence and "the absence of human noise" – noticing that a natural soundscape can feel silent to a human but is full of animal life.
Smell. The most sophisticated observation is describing the birds' voices as "short, sharp, certain."
Touch. The most sophisticated observation is that the reeds move in the wind.
question_json.correct: Sound. The most sophisticated observation is the distinction between silence and "the absence of human noise" – noticing that a natural soundscape can feel silent to a human but is full of animal life.

feedback.correct: Correct! This paragraph is built entirely from sound. The most sophisticated observation is the distinction between silence and "the absence of human noise" – the estuary is not quiet, it is simply empty of the specific sounds that humans make. This is the kind of precise distinction that turns observation into literature.
feedback.wrong: Sound dominates this paragraph: papery movement, oystercatcher calls, and the distinguished definition of silence. The most sophisticated line is "a silence that was not really silence but the absence of human noise" – it creates a category distinction (natural sound vs. human sound) that makes the setting feel observed rather than imagined.

answer_json.correct: Sound. The most sophisticated observation is the distinction between silence and "the absence of human noise" – noticing that a natural soundscape can feel silent to a human but is full of animal life.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

Read these two versions of the same moment:

A: "Jamil was anxious in the waiting room. He felt worried about what was happening inside. Time seemed to pass slowly."

B: "The clock on the wall said 11:47. He was almost certain it had said 11:47 a few minutes ago, which meant either time had stopped or he had lost the ability to tell the difference between four minutes and forty. Both felt plausible."

What makes Version B more effective as internal monologue?

A: "Jamil was anxious in the waiting room. He felt worried about what was happening inside. Time seemed to pass slowly."
B: "The clock on the wall said 11:47. He was almost certain it had said 11:47 a few minutes ago, which meant either time had stopped or he had lost the ability to tell the difference between four minutes and forty. Both felt plausible."
options: Version B is longer and contains more information.

Version A names the feelings directly; Version B never names them. Instead it shows the mind's specific behaviour under stress (clocking the same time twice, generating absurd explanations) and lets the reader name the feeling themselves. The dry self-deprecating tone ("both felt plausible") is itself a form of coping.
Version B uses technical vocabulary about time.
Version A is too short to be effective internal monologue.
question_json.correct: Version A names the feelings directly; Version B never names them. Instead it shows the mind's specific behaviour under stress (clocking the same time twice, generating absurd explanations) and lets the reader name the feeling themselves. The dry self-deprecating tone ("both felt plausible") is itself a form of coping.

feedback.correct: Correct! Version A labels feelings ("anxious", "worried") and states the experience. Version B performs the anxious mind rather than describing it: the repetition of 11:47, the two absurd explanations for the discrepancy, the dry acceptance of both. The reader diagnoses the anxiety themselves, which is far more powerful than being told it.
feedback.wrong: Version A labels feelings and states observations. Version B performs the anxiety through the mind's specific behaviour: noticing the time twice, generating two absurd explanations, accepting them drily. "Both felt plausible" is not a logical response – it is a stressed mind's response. The reader recognises the feeling because the behaviour is so precise.

answer_json.correct: Version A names the feelings directly; Version B never names them. Instead it shows the mind's specific behaviour under stress (clocking the same time twice, generating absurd explanations) and lets the reader name the feeling themselves. The dry self-deprecating tone ("both felt plausible") is itself a form of coping.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

Read this sentence from the garden passage:

"She had learned, across more years than she cared to count, that a garden responds to attention the same way a person does: not to urgency, but to steadiness."

What technique is being used, and what does it reveal?

"She had learned, across more years than she cared to count, that a garden responds to attention the same way a person does: not to urgency, but to steadiness."
options: A simile comparing garden and person. It reveals that the character is patient.

An analogy between garden and person. It reveals her age ("more years than she cared to count"), her philosophy of care, and – because a person uses this insight when dealing with both gardens AND people – implies she has tended both with the same patience over a long life. The character is built in one sentence without a single physical description.
A metaphor. It reveals that she prefers gardening to dealing with people.
Personification, giving the garden human qualities of preference.
question_json.correct: An analogy between garden and person. It reveals her age ("more years than she cared to count"), her philosophy of care, and – because a person uses this insight when dealing with both gardens AND people – implies she has tended both with the same patience over a long life. The character is built in one sentence without a single physical description.

feedback.correct: Correct! One sentence does multiple things: "more years than she cared to count" implies age without stating it; the garden/person analogy reveals her philosophy; the specific contrast (not urgency, but steadiness) tells us how she has lived, not just how she gardens. This is how skilled fiction writers build character – through implication, not description.
feedback.wrong: The analogy builds character in one sentence: age is implied ("more years than she cared to count"), philosophy is stated (steadiness not urgency), and the comparison between garden and person suggests she has applied this wisdom to both throughout a long life. Physical description of the character is unnecessary – this single sentence shows who she is.

answer_json.correct: An analogy between garden and person. It reveals her age ("more years than she cared to count"), her philosophy of care, and – because a person uses this insight when dealing with both gardens AND people – implies she has tended both with the same patience over a long life. The character is built in one sentence without a single physical description.

question_json.question:
Click a type on the left, then its feature on the right.

Match each description type to its defining feature.
question_json.instruction: Click a type on the left, then its feature on the right.

pairs.right: Reveals personality through physical detail, actions, dialogue, and objects – not through stated qualities
The five senses used deliberately to create the reader's experience of a place rather than listing its features
A character's thoughts rendered as they actually occur: interrupted, contradictory, wandering
A technique applied across all three types: instead of naming feelings or qualities, behaviour and detail create them
Reveals personality through physical detail, actions, dialogue, and objects – not through stated qualities
The five senses used deliberately to create the reader's experience of a place rather than listing its features
A character's thoughts rendered as they actually occur: interrupted, contradictory, wandering
A technique applied across all three types: instead of naming feelings or qualities, behaviour and detail create them

question_json.question:
Identify the taste/near-taste detail.

Five senses appear in the estuary passage. Which sentence uses TASTE (or the sense closest to taste)?
options: "The mud stretched out from the bank in ridged, dark sheets."
"Somewhere in the reeds, something small moved with a rapid, papery sound."
"In winter it sharpened to something that felt almost like a flavour – the sort that settles at the back of your throat and stays there for hours."
"The creak of a single moored boat, rope taut and complaining against the post."
question_json.correct: "In winter it sharpened to something that felt almost like a flavour – the sort that settles at the back of your throat and stays there for hours."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C describes how the cold mineral smell of the estuary in winter crosses into almost-taste territory: a sensation felt at the back of the throat. This is sophisticated sensory writing – noting where one sense bleeds into another. The estuary passage demonstrates all five senses: sight (ridged mud), smell (salt and mineral), sound (birds and boat), touch (cold in layers), and this near-taste.
feedback.wrong: Option C: "something that felt almost like a flavour – the sort that settles at the back of your throat" crosses from smell into the sensation closest to taste. This is an advanced technique: noticing where one sense bleeds into another rather than treating the five senses as entirely separate categories.

answer_json.correct: "In winter it sharpened to something that felt almost like a flavour – the sort that settles at the back of your throat and stays there for hours."

question_json.question:
Choose the most effective example.

Which of these demonstrates the MOST advanced use of "show don't tell"?
options: "She was very sad after the news."
"She felt overwhelmed and could not stop crying."
"She stood at the kitchen window for a long time after the call ended. The tap was dripping. She had been meaning to fix that for months."
"Her eyes were red from crying and she looked very upset."
question_json.correct: "She stood at the kitchen window for a long time after the call ended. The tap was dripping. She had been meaning to fix that for months."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C never mentions sadness or emotion at all. Yet it is the most emotionally powerful: the long silence at the window, and then the mind fixing on a small domestic fact that has nothing to do with the crisis – this is exactly how shock and grief actually work. The tap dripping is a form of dissociation. The reader feels the emotion without a single emotional word.
feedback.wrong: Option C is most advanced: no emotional word appears. The long stillness, and then the mind noticing the dripping tap (which has nothing to do with the crisis), shows how shock or grief actually works – the mind detaches to something small and manageable. This is more accurate to experience than "she felt overwhelmed," and therefore more powerful.

answer_json.correct: "She stood at the kitchen window for a long time after the call ended. The tap was dripping. She had been meaning to fix that for months."

question_json.question:
Click a technique on the left, then its example on the right.

Match each figurative technique to the example from the model texts.
question_json.instruction: Click a technique on the left, then its example on the right.

pairs.right: His voice was "dry, unhurried, like old paper unfolding"
The small channels in the mud were "silver strips so that the whole flat expanse looked like a cracked mirror"
The estuary "belonged entirely to itself" in the early mornings
The apple tree "had gone to bones, each branch showing against the white sky with a clarity it hid all summer"
His voice was "dry, unhurried, like old paper unfolding"
The small channels in the mud were "silver strips so that the whole flat expanse looked like a cracked mirror"
The estuary "belonged entirely to itself" in the early mornings

question_json.question:
Choose the most effective rewrite.

A pupil has written:

"The old man was very experienced at his job and knew exactly what he was doing. He was also kind and thoughtful."

Which rewrite is the BEST example of showing rather than telling?

"The old man was very experienced at his job and knew exactly what he was doing. He was also kind and thoughtful."
options: "The old man was an extremely experienced professional with many years of practice in his field."

"His fingers moved across the mechanism without hesitation. When she told him the watch had belonged to her grandfather, he set down his tools and held it to the light before speaking – not in a hurry to give an answer, but as if the watch deserved consideration first."
"He was old and had worked there for a very long time. He was nice to customers."
"The experienced and kind man worked carefully and thoughtfully on the broken clock mechanism."
question_json.correct: "His fingers moved across the mechanism without hesitation. When she told him the watch had belonged to her grandfather, he set down his tools and held it to the light before speaking – not in a hurry to give an answer, but as if the watch deserved consideration first."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B shows experience through the hands moving without hesitation and kindness through the specific action of setting down tools, holding the watch to the light, taking time – giving the watch consideration before answering. Not a single quality is named. Every quality is demonstrated through behaviour.
feedback.wrong: Option B shows both qualities through specific behaviour: experience shown by hands moving without hesitation; kindness shown by setting tools down, holding the watch to the light, not rushing the answer. "Kind" and "experienced" never appear. The behaviour creates them.

answer_json.correct: "His fingers moved across the mechanism without hesitation. When she told him the watch had belonged to her grandfather, he set down his tools and held it to the light before speaking – not in a hurry to give an answer, but as if the watch deserved consideration first."

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this sentence from the estuary description with the correct word.
question_json.template: "And over all of it – a___that was not really silence but the absence of human noise, which is different."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Silence" is the word that makes the distinction powerful: the writer names it, then immediately qualifies and redefines it. This is a common technique in literary description: introduce a word, then challenge your own use of it to be more precise.

question_json.question:
Choose the best analysis.

Read this opening:

"The garden in November was a particular kind of beautiful, the kind that requires some patience to see."

What does this sentence do that a simple description would not?

"The garden in November was a particular kind of beautiful, the kind that requires some patience to see."
options: It describes the garden in November, which is a cold month.

It immediately establishes the beekeeper's relationship to beauty and time – not every kind of beauty is immediately obvious; some requires patience. This tells us who is seeing the garden before we have met the seer. It also positions the reader: you will need to look carefully at this passage.
It uses the word "particular" which is a precise adjective.
It sets the scene before describing the characters.
question_json.correct: It immediately establishes the beekeeper's relationship to beauty and time – not every kind of beauty is immediately obvious; some requires patience. This tells us who is seeing the garden before we have met the seer. It also positions the reader: you will need to look carefully at this passage.

feedback.correct: Correct! This sentence does three things: it characterises November beauty (not obvious, requiring patience); it characterises the observer (someone who has the patience to see it); and it sets an expectation for the reader (look carefully at what follows). All in one sentence, before the beekeeper has even appeared.
feedback.wrong: "A particular kind of beautiful, the kind that requires some patience to see" characterises November beauty (not immediate), the observer (patient, attentive), and the reader's task (look carefully). This is an efficient opening sentence that does more work than it appears to.

answer_json.correct: It immediately establishes the beekeeper's relationship to beauty and time – not every kind of beauty is immediately obvious; some requires patience. This tells us who is seeing the garden before we have met the seer. It also positions the reader: you will need to look carefully at this passage.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word. Think about how a stressed mind actually behaves.

Complete this internal monologue sentence with the most effective word.
question_json.template: "He found himself thinking about the garden at home – not the garden as it was now, which was overgrown and needed dealing with, but the garden as it___been when he was small."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Had" – the past perfect tense – signals a shift further back in time (a memory inside a present moment). The movement from "now" (overgrown garden, present situation) to "had been" (childhood memory) is the mind doing what stressed minds do: retreating to a safer past. The tense marks the retreat.

question_json.question:
Identify all three problems.

A pupil is writing a character description and has written:

"The teacher was strict but fair. She had grey hair and wore glasses. She was good at her job and the pupils liked her even though they were a bit scared of her sometimes."

What are the THREE main problems with this description?

"The teacher was strict but fair. She had grey hair and wore glasses. She was good at her job and the pupils liked her even though they were a bit scared of her sometimes."
options: The sentences are too short, the vocabulary is too simple, and there is no punctuation.

Physical details are minimal and generic (grey hair, glasses tell us nothing specific), all qualities are stated rather than shown ("strict but fair", "good at her job", "liked her"), and there are no actions, objects, or dialogue to build an individual person rather than a category.
The description is too short and needs to be longer.
The character has not been named, which makes it impossible to describe her.
question_json.correct: Physical details are minimal and generic (grey hair, glasses tell us nothing specific), all qualities are stated rather than shown ("strict but fair", "good at her job", "liked her"), and there are no actions, objects, or dialogue to build an individual person rather than a category.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three problems: (1) generic physical details (grey hair and glasses describe thousands of teachers), (2) all qualities stated not shown ("strict but fair", "liked her", "good at her job"), (3) no specific actions, objects, or moments that make this teacher distinct rather than a generic type. "She marked every piece of work the same day it was handed in, always in purple ink" would tell us more than "she was good at her job."
feedback.wrong: Three problems: generic physical detail (grey hair/glasses = no individual), all qualities stated (strict, fair, liked, good at job = no showing), no specific actions or objects (a character without specific behaviour is a type, not a person). The fix: find the detail that belongs only to this teacher, not all teachers.

answer_json.correct: Physical details are minimal and generic (grey hair, glasses tell us nothing specific), all qualities are stated rather than shown ("strict but fair", "good at her job", "liked her"), and there are no actions, objects, or dialogue to build an individual person rather than a category.

question_json.question:
Choose a place: a library just before closing, a playground in winter, an empty kitchen at dawn, a bus in heavy rain. Use at least TWO different senses. Do not write the words "quiet", "peaceful", "eerie", "lonely", or "cosy" – show those feelings instead.

Write a SHORT setting description (4-6 sentences) of a place that creates a specific atmosphere WITHOUT naming what the atmosphere is.

Requirements:
1. A specific real place (not fantasy)
2. At least two senses used
3. No atmosphere word named (no quiet, eerie, lonely, etc.)
4. At least one figurative image (simile, metaphor, or personification)
question_json.prompt: Write your setting description here...
question_json.instruction: Choose a place: a library just before closing, a playground in winter, an empty kitchen at dawn, a bus in heavy rain. Use at least TWO different senses. Do not write the words "quiet", "peaceful", "eerie", "lonely", or "cosy" – show those feelings instead.
question_json.modelAnswer: The library's central heating ticked and settled, the kind of sound a building makes when it is slowly exhaling. The last few readers sat at separate tables, their pages turning at different intervals, each in their own silence. Near the fire door, a trolley of returned books waited to be reshelved, their spines dusty and patient. Through the tall windows, the car park lights were coming on one by one in the early dark.

answer_json.modelAnswer: The library's central heating ticked and settled, the kind of sound a building makes when it is slowly exhaling. The last few readers sat at separate tables, their pages turning at different intervals, each in their own silence. Near the fire door, a trolley of returned books waited to be reshelved, their spines dusty and patient. Through the tall windows, the car park lights were coming on one by one in the early dark.

question_json.question:
Choose the most effective internal monologue.

Which of these is the BEST example of internal monologue?
options: "Amara thought about how nervous she was feeling. She wondered what would happen next. She hoped everything would be all right."
"She imagined many different scenarios and her mind was working overtime as she tried to process all the possibilities."

"It would probably be fine. Almost certainly fine. There was no good reason for it to be anything other than completely fine. She told herself this three times, which she recognised was probably one repetition too many."
"Amara was an anxious person who always worried about things before they happened."
question_json.correct: "It would probably be fine. Almost certainly fine. There was no good reason for it to be anything other than completely fine. She told herself this three times, which she recognised was probably one repetition too many."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C performs anxiety without naming it. The repetition of "fine" three times, and then the dry observation ("one repetition too many") shows a mind trying to reassure itself while also observing its own attempt. This is the double-consciousness of internal monologue: the self, and the self watching the self.
feedback.wrong: Option C performs anxiety through the repetition of "fine" three times – the very repetition reveals the anxiety it is trying to suppress. Then "which she recognised was probably one repetition too many" shows the mind watching itself. This double-consciousness (self + self watching self) is the hallmark of effective internal monologue.

answer_json.correct: "It would probably be fine. Almost certainly fine. There was no good reason for it to be anything other than completely fine. She told herself this three times, which she recognised was probably one repetition too many."

question_json.question:
Choose the most complete analysis.

Read the final lines of the garden passage:

"The sky was the colour of pewter. The bare apple tree moved slightly in the wind. Somewhere down the lane a dog barked twice and fell silent.

She straightened, pulled her coat closed, and went back inside to make tea."

Why is this ending effective?

"The sky was the colour of pewter. The bare apple tree moved slightly in the wind. Somewhere down the lane a dog barked twice and fell silent.

She straightened, pulled her coat closed, and went back inside to make tea."
options: It describes the weather and the character's actions, which gives the passage a natural ending.

The three short sentences (sky, tree, dog) create a visual stillness. Then the ending enacts the character's return to ordinary life through three small sequential actions (straightened, pulled coat, went inside). The action of making tea does not resolve the emotion of the passage – instead it returns the character to daily life while the reader still holds the emotion. This is the literary technique of the "open close": the scene ends, but the feeling does not.
It ends with a simple domestic action to show that the character is practical.
The dog barking twice adds an unexpected sound to the silence.
question_json.correct: The three short sentences (sky, tree, dog) create a visual stillness. Then the ending enacts the character's return to ordinary life through three small sequential actions (straightened, pulled coat, went inside). The action of making tea does not resolve the emotion of the passage – instead it returns the character to daily life while the reader still holds the emotion. This is the literary technique of the "open close": the scene ends, but the feeling does not.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three short observational sentences create stillness. Then three sequential actions return the character to ordinary life. "Making tea" is a domestic act so specific and British that it carries cultural weight about how people continue after quiet revelation. The passage does not explain what the beekeeper has felt. It simply ends. The emotion the reader is carrying does not resolve – it is handed to the reader to hold.
feedback.wrong: The stillness of three short observation sentences, then three small sequential actions. "Making tea" is both ordinary and weighted – it is what people do when they need to do something small after something large. The emotion is not resolved or named. The scene ends; the feeling continues. This is the "open close" technique.

answer_json.correct: The three short sentences (sky, tree, dog) create a visual stillness. Then the ending enacts the character's return to ordinary life through three small sequential actions (straightened, pulled coat, went inside). The action of making tea does not resolve the emotion of the passage – instead it returns the character to daily life while the reader still holds the emotion. This is the literary technique of the "open close": the scene ends, but the feeling does not.

question_json.question:
Choose the approach that combines all three most effectively.

A pupil wants to write a paragraph that combines character, setting, and internal thought. They have planned:

- Character: an elderly man waiting for his granddaughter to visit
- Setting: a small front room in winter
- Thought: he is worried she might not come

Which approach is correct?
options: Write three separate paragraphs: one describing the man, one describing the room, one describing his thoughts.

Open with the setting detail (the room telling us about him), weave his action through it (checking the clock, adjusting a photograph, moving to the window), let one brief internal thought appear mid-passage without explanation, and close on a sensory detail that carries the emotion without naming it.
Describe the man first, then the room, then write "He thought to himself:" and explain his worries.
Begin with a statement of the emotion ("He was worried she would not come") and then explain why he was worried using setting details.
question_json.correct: Open with the setting detail (the room telling us about him), weave his action through it (checking the clock, adjusting a photograph, moving to the window), let one brief internal thought appear mid-passage without explanation, and close on a sensory detail that carries the emotion without naming it.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B weaves all three together rather than separating them into blocks. The setting details reveal character (a room tells us about the person who lives in it). The actions (checking clock, adjusting photograph) show anxiety without naming it. One brief thought appears and passes – not a block of explanation but a flash. The closing sensory detail holds the emotion for the reader.
feedback.wrong: Option B: setting reveals character (objects and arrangement of a room show the person who created it), actions show anxiety (checking clock, adjusting photograph), one brief thought appears and passes without explanation, closing sensory detail carries the emotion. Weaving is always more effective than separating into blocks.

answer_json.correct: Open with the setting detail (the room telling us about him), weave his action through it (checking the clock, adjusting a photograph, moving to the window), let one brief internal thought appear mid-passage without explanation, and close on a sensory detail that carries the emotion without naming it.

question_json.question:
Use the formula to write ONE descriptive sentence in your PWP Booklet. Change the subject – do not copy the example.

Formula: [Sensory detail or figurative image] + [character action or reaction] + [internal thought or emotion]

Example: The rain fell in silver curtains against the window as Mara pressed her forehead to the cold glass, watching her breath fog and clear, fog and clear, thinking that some things are easier to understand when you cannot be seen.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

Formula: [Sensory detail or figurative image] + [character action or reaction] + [internal thought or emotion]

Example: The rain fell in silver curtains against the window as Mara pressed her forehead to the cold glass, watching her breath fog and clear, fog and clear, thinking that some things are easier to understand when you cannot be seen.
question_json.prompt: [Sensory detail / figurative image] + [character action] + [internal thought]...
question_json.instruction:
Use the formula to write ONE descriptive sentence in your PWP Booklet. Change the subject – do not copy the example.

Formula: [Sensory detail or figurative image] + [character action or reaction] + [internal thought or emotion]

Example: The rain fell in silver curtains against the window as Mara pressed her forehead to the cold glass, watching her breath fog and clear, fog and clear, thinking that some things are easier to understand when you cannot be seen.
question_json.modelAnswer: The last candle guttered and spat as Joel pulled his coat tighter around his shoulders, listening to the house settle into its nighttime sounds, wondering whether silence was something a building made or something it simply allowed.

answer_json.modelAnswer: The last candle guttered and spat as Joel pulled his coat tighter around his shoulders, listening to the house settle into its nighttime sounds, wondering whether silence was something a building made or something it simply allowed.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is the most important difference between poetry and prose?
options: Poetry must always rhyme; prose never does.

Poetry uses line breaks and compression to make every word carry more weight than it would in a sentence. Prose uses sentences and paragraphs to develop ideas at length. The principle: poetry asks each word to do more.
Poetry is shorter than prose, and prose is longer than poetry.
Poetry is about feelings and prose is about facts.
question_json.correct: Poetry uses line breaks and compression to make every word carry more weight than it would in a sentence. Prose uses sentences and paragraphs to develop ideas at length. The principle: poetry asks each word to do more.

feedback.correct: Correct! Poetry compresses meaning – every word does more because there are fewer of them. Line breaks create pauses that prose punctuation cannot. Stanza breaks create silences. Prose can rhyme, prose can be short, prose can be about feelings – the difference is compression and the use of the line as a unit of meaning.
feedback.wrong: Poetry compresses meaning through line breaks and word economy. The defining feature is not rhyme, length, or subject matter (poetry can be unrhymed, long, and factual). It is that every word carries more weight, and the line break itself becomes a unit of meaning that sentences cannot replicate.

answer_json.correct: Poetry uses line breaks and compression to make every word carry more weight than it would in a sentence. Prose uses sentences and paragraphs to develop ideas at length. The principle: poetry asks each word to do more.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct technique.

Read this opening from Model 1 (free verse):

"The last reader left at quarter past five,
and the library exhaled."

What technique is being used in line 2?
options: Simile – the library is being compared to a person.
Personification – the library is given a human action (exhaling). The short line and the period at the end make the breath physical for the reader.
Onomatopoeia – the word "exhaled" sounds like the action.
Rhyme – the lines rhyme with each other.
question_json.correct: Personification – the library is given a human action (exhaling). The short line and the period at the end make the breath physical for the reader.

feedback.correct: Correct! Personification gives the library the human action of exhaling. Notice how the line itself enacts the breath: it is short, ends with a period, and makes the reader pause. The form (short line) and the meaning (a breath being released) work together. This is what poetry does that prose cannot easily do.
feedback.wrong: Personification: the library is given a human action (exhaling). A simile would say "the library was like someone exhaling"; here the library simply exhales. The technique is sharper and more compressed than simile because the line itself acts out the breath.

answer_json.correct: Personification – the library is given a human action (exhaling). The short line and the period at the end make the breath physical for the reader.

question_json.question:
Identify the haiku.

Which of these is a HAIKU?
options:
"Before the street wakes up, before the cars,
the blackbird finds the highest branch to own."

"On the bench – two leaves.
Someone sat here recently.
The dent, still warm."

"A river knows how to go around things.
It does not argue with the rock."

"Roses are red, violets are blue,
The garden in autumn is missing you too."
question_json.correct:
"On the bench – two leaves.
Someone sat here recently.
The dent, still warm."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is a haiku: three lines, approximately 5-7-5 syllables ("On the bench – two leaves" / "Someone sat here recently" / "The dent, still warm"), one observation followed by an unexpected turn. The most powerful haiku detail – "The dent, still warm" – shows recent presence through absence. Option A is rhyming verse; C is free verse; D is doggerel rhyme.
feedback.wrong: Option B is the haiku: three lines, 5-7-5 syllable pattern, a single moment captured with an unexpected turn. Notice "The dent, still warm" – the recently departed person is felt through what they left behind. This is the haiku discipline: presence through absence.

answer_json.correct:
"On the bench – two leaves.
Someone sat here recently.
The dent, still warm."

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

Read this stanza from Model 1:

"Dust moved through the window light
in slow trajectories,
unhurried as the pages
of books that have not been opened
for a season."

Which poetic device dominates this stanza, and what is its effect?
options: Simile – "unhurried as the pages of books that have not been opened" compares dust's movement to unread books. Both share slow, patient stillness. The comparison turns dust into something with a kind of dignity.
Alliteration – the repeated "s" sound throughout the stanza.
question_json.correct: Simile – "unhurried as the pages of books that have not been opened" compares dust's movement to unread books. Both share slow, patient stillness. The comparison turns dust into something with a kind of dignity.

feedback.correct: Correct! Simile: "unhurried as the pages of books that have not been opened for a season." The comparison links two unremarkable things (drifting dust, unopened books) and reveals what they share: a kind of patient stillness. The dust is no longer just dust – it has the same presence as books waiting to be read.
feedback.wrong: Simile is the dominant device: "unhurried as the pages of books that have not been opened for a season." This compares the unhurried movement of dust to the patient stillness of unread books. The comparison reveals what they share – a kind of waiting. The dust acquires dignity through the comparison.

answer_json.correct: Simile – "unhurried as the pages of books that have not been opened" compares dust's movement to unread books. Both share slow, patient stillness. The comparison turns dust into something with a kind of dignity.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

Read the closing couplet of Model 2 (Blackbird):

"Some mornings I am the street, the noise, the car.
Some mornings I am listening. That is far."

What is happening at this point in the poem, and why is it called a "volta" or "turn"?
options: The poem ends with a rhyme between "car" and "far".

A "volta" is a turn – the moment when a poem shifts direction. Here, the poem has been describing the blackbird and the city. In the final two lines it suddenly turns inward: the speaker admits they are sometimes the noise that drowns the bird out, and sometimes the listener. The poem stops being about the bird and becomes about the reader.
The two lines repeat each other to create emphasis.
The poem ends with a question to the audience.
question_json.correct: A "volta" is a turn – the moment when a poem shifts direction. Here, the poem has been describing the blackbird and the city. In the final two lines it suddenly turns inward: the speaker admits they are sometimes the noise that drowns the bird out, and sometimes the listener. The poem stops being about the bird and becomes about the reader.

feedback.correct: Correct! The volta is the turn from observation to confession. For twelve lines the poem describes the blackbird and the traffic. Then suddenly: "I am the street, the noise, the car" – the speaker admits to being the very thing that erases the bird's song. "That is far" carries two meanings: a long distance to travel between those two states, and that is much / that is everything.
feedback.wrong: The volta is poetry's "turn" – the moment a poem changes direction. This poem observes the blackbird for three quatrains, then in the closing couplet pivots inward: the speaker confesses that they are sometimes the traffic and sometimes the listener. The poem stops being about the bird and becomes about the reader's attention.

answer_json.correct: A "volta" is a turn – the moment when a poem shifts direction. Here, the poem has been describing the blackbird and the city. In the final two lines it suddenly turns inward: the speaker admits they are sometimes the noise that drowns the bird out, and sometimes the listener. The poem stops being about the bird and becomes about the reader.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

Read the AUTUMN haiku from Model 3:

"On the bench – two leaves.
Someone sat here recently.
The dent, still warm."

What is the most sophisticated technique in this haiku?
options: The haiku follows the 5-7-5 syllable pattern exactly.

Presence through absence: the human is never named, never seen, but their recent presence is felt through three pieces of evidence (two leaves, the recently sat-in feeling, the warmth in the cushion). The reader experiences the person without ever meeting them.
The em-dash creates a pause between "the bench" and "two leaves".
The poem describes the bench in detail using sensory language.
question_json.correct: Presence through absence: the human is never named, never seen, but their recent presence is felt through three pieces of evidence (two leaves, the recently sat-in feeling, the warmth in the cushion). The reader experiences the person without ever meeting them.

feedback.correct: Correct! The most sophisticated technique is presence through absence. Three pieces of evidence (two leaves, the recently sat-in feeling, the still-warm dent) tell the reader that someone was here a moment ago. We never see them. We never know who. But we know they exist, and we know they have just gone. This is what haiku does best – it shows what is present by what is absent.
feedback.wrong: Presence through absence: the haiku contains a person who never appears. Two leaves on the bench, the bench shows recent occupation, the cushion is still warm – the person is everywhere implied and nowhere named. The reader meets someone who has just left. This is the haiku discipline at its most refined.

answer_json.correct: Presence through absence: the human is never named, never seen, but their recent presence is felt through three pieces of evidence (two leaves, the recently sat-in feeling, the warmth in the cushion). The reader experiences the person without ever meeting them.

Click a form on the left, then its feature on the right.
