question_json.question:
Choose the more successful haiku.

Read these two haiku candidates. Which one is a more SUCCESSFUL haiku, and why?

A: "The flowers are bright /
In the sunshine they look nice /
I like flowers a lot."

B: "Three petals, fallen. /
The path keeps the shape of feet /
that are no longer there."
options: A is better because it describes flowers and sunshine clearly and uses simple language.

B is more successful: it offers a single specific observation (three petals on a path), uses concrete sensory imagery, and contains a turn (the shape of absent feet). It performs the haiku discipline of compression and unexpected reversal. A simply states feelings ("look nice", "I like") without showing.
Both are equally successful because they both have three lines and contain the right number of syllables.
A is better because it does not contain difficult vocabulary.
question_json.correct: B is more successful: it offers a single specific observation (three petals on a path), uses concrete sensory imagery, and contains a turn (the shape of absent feet). It performs the haiku discipline of compression and unexpected reversal. A simply states feelings ("look nice", "I like") without showing.

feedback.correct: Correct! B succeeds because it does what haiku must do: a specific observation, a turn or surprise, and presence through absence (the path holds shape after feet have left). A merely states feelings ("look nice", "I like") without showing what the flowers actually look like or why they matter. The 5-7-5 form is necessary but not sufficient – the haiku must also do the work of compression.
feedback.wrong: B succeeds because it contains the haiku essentials: a specific observation, a turn (the shape of feet that have left), and an image that resonates beyond itself. A meets the syllable count but not the haiku discipline – it states feelings rather than showing observed details. The form alone does not make a haiku; the compression and turn do.

answer_json.correct: B is more successful: it offers a single specific observation (three petals on a path), uses concrete sensory imagery, and contains a turn (the shape of absent feet). It performs the haiku discipline of compression and unexpected reversal. A simply states feelings ("look nice", "I like") without showing.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct analysis.

In Model 4 ("What Rivers Know"), the structural device that powers the entire poem is anaphora – the repetition of "A river knows" at the start of stanzas. What does this technique achieve that another structure could not?
options: It makes the poem easier to memorise because of the repetition.

The anaphora creates the rhythm of an argument or list. Each "A river knows" reopens the same statement and extends it. The reader expects the next "A river knows" – and when it arrives, the poem feels like accumulated wisdom rather than scattered observations. The repetition is structural, not sonic.
It allows the poet to use the same words over and over and not have to think of new ones.
It is the only way to write a poem about a river.
question_json.correct: The anaphora creates the rhythm of an argument or list. Each "A river knows" reopens the same statement and extends it. The reader expects the next "A river knows" – and when it arrives, the poem feels like accumulated wisdom rather than scattered observations. The repetition is structural, not sonic.

feedback.correct: Correct! Anaphora creates the structure of a sustained argument. Each "A river knows" returns the reader to the central claim and extends it with new evidence. The poem feels like a list-argument that builds. By the time the closing question arrives ("What do you know, the way a river knows?") the reader has been prepared for it. The repetition is structural – it is what holds the poem together.
feedback.wrong: Anaphora structures the poem as a sustained argument: each "A river knows" reopens the central claim and adds another aspect. By the closing question, the reader has been prepared for the turn. Without anaphora, the stanzas would feel like separate observations. With it, they feel like one continuous insight.

answer_json.correct: The anaphora creates the rhythm of an argument or list. Each "A river knows" reopens the same statement and extends it. The reader expects the next "A river knows" – and when it arrives, the poem feels like accumulated wisdom rather than scattered observations. The repetition is structural, not sonic.

question_json.question:
Identify all three problems.

A pupil has written this "haiku":

"The garden is so bright /
The flowers smell really nice /
I feel happy too."

What are the THREE main problems?
options: It does not rhyme, the lines are too short, and there is no title.

The vocabulary is generic ("bright", "really nice", "happy"), the speaker states feelings rather than showing them, and there is no turn or surprise – the three lines move in a straight line with no compression or twist.
The poem is not long enough and does not have enough characters.
The poem uses too many verbs and not enough nouns.
question_json.correct: The vocabulary is generic ("bright", "really nice", "happy"), the speaker states feelings rather than showing them, and there is no turn or surprise – the three lines move in a straight line with no compression or twist.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three problems: (1) generic words ("bright", "nice", "happy") name qualities without showing them; (2) the speaker tells us their feelings rather than offering an image that produces those feelings in the reader; (3) the haiku has no turn – it moves directly from observation to stated emotion. Compare with the Spring haiku from Model 3: "Under last year's leaves – something bright has decided it is time enough." Specific image, agency, surprise.
feedback.wrong: Three problems: generic vocabulary that tells rather than shows; stated feelings ("I feel happy") instead of images that produce feelings; no turn or surprise – the three lines move in a straight line. The haiku discipline requires concrete observation and one unexpected pivot.

answer_json.correct: The vocabulary is generic ("bright", "really nice", "happy"), the speaker states feelings rather than showing them, and there is no turn or surprise – the three lines move in a straight line with no compression or twist.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word. Think about what kind of "sun" the librarian's lamp is.

Complete this line from Model 1 (Old Library) with the most effective word.
question_json.template: "...switching off row by row, until only her desk lamp remained – a small,___sun in the darkening room."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Ordinary" is the word that turns a metaphor into an observation. "A small, ordinary sun" admits that this is not a real sun, not a special sun, just the everyday lamp that keeps a librarian working after hours. The word "ordinary" is what gives the metaphor its quiet dignity – it does not pretend to be more than it is.

question_json.question:
Type the rhyming word.

Complete this rhyming line from Model 2 (Blackbird).
question_json.template: "He sings as if the night has left him scars / worth turning into music, not a___."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Groan" rhymes with "scars" – wait, no. "Groan" rhymes with "own" (the previous line's end word in the ABAB scheme). This is the contrast: scars could become groans (sounds of pain) but the blackbird turns them into music instead. The pairing of "music" and "groan" makes the choice visible. The rhyme word arrives where the meaning arrives.

question_json.question:
Choose the most effective line-broken version.

Read this prose sentence:

"The bell rang and everyone went home and I was left standing alone in the empty playground."

Which version uses LINE BREAKS to make the same content into more powerful poetry?
options:
"The bell rang and everyone went home /
and I was left standing alone in the empty playground."

"The bell rang. /
Everyone went home. /
I was left /
in the empty playground, /
standing."

"The /
bell /
rang /
and /
everyone /
went /
home."

"The bell rang and everyone went home /
and I was alone."
question_json.correct:
"The bell rang. /
Everyone went home. /
I was left /
in the empty playground, /
standing."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B uses line breaks to do work. The short opening lines ("The bell rang. / Everyone went home.") are matter-of-fact and abrupt. Then the longer line ("I was left in the empty playground") slows down. The closing isolated word "standing" turns into the whole emotional content of the poem – the person is left in stillness. The form enacts the meaning.
feedback.wrong: Option B uses line breaks deliberately: short statements at the start, a longer line that slows the pace, and the isolated final word "standing" that turns into the poem's emotional centre. Option A barely changes the prose. Option C breaks every word arbitrarily, which destroys rhythm rather than building it.

answer_json.correct:
"The bell rang. /
Everyone went home. /
I was left /
in the empty playground, /
standing."

question_json.question:
Choose the best improvement.

A pupil has written this poem ending:

"The sky was blue
The day was nice
I felt happy when I saw the playground.
It was a really good day."

What changes would most improve this ending?
options: Make the lines longer so the poem has more detail.

Replace the stated feelings ("nice", "happy", "good") with concrete images that show the feelings: replace "I felt happy" with a specific action (e.g. "I held my arms out to the wind"); replace "it was a really good day" with a small final image (e.g. "the swings still moving from someone who had just got off"). End on the image, not the conclusion.
Add more rhyming words so it sounds like proper poetry.
Add more adjectives to describe the sky and the day in more detail.
question_json.correct: Replace the stated feelings ("nice", "happy", "good") with concrete images that show the feelings: replace "I felt happy" with a specific action (e.g. "I held my arms out to the wind"); replace "it was a really good day" with a small final image (e.g. "the swings still moving from someone who had just got off"). End on the image, not the conclusion.

feedback.correct: Correct! The poem ends with three flat statements of feeling. The improvement: replace the stated feelings with concrete images that produce feelings. End on the image, not the conclusion. "The swings still moving from someone who had just got off" closes the poem on a specific physical detail that holds the emotion. This is the principle from L61 (show don't tell) applied to poetry.
feedback.wrong: The poem ends with three stated feelings ("nice", "happy", "good") that label rather than show. The fix: replace each stated feeling with a concrete image. End on the image (the swings still moving), not the conclusion ("it was a really good day"). Show don't tell from L61 applies fully to poetry.

answer_json.correct: Replace the stated feelings ("nice", "happy", "good") with concrete images that show the feelings: replace "I felt happy" with a specific action (e.g. "I held my arms out to the wind"); replace "it was a really good day" with a small final image (e.g. "the swings still moving from someone who had just got off"). End on the image, not the conclusion.

question_json.question:
Three lines, approximately 5-7-5 syllables. Include: ONE concrete observation (no stated feelings), ONE specific physical detail, and ONE turn or surprise at the end.

Write your own HAIKU. Choose ONE subject: an empty classroom at the end of the day; the moment just before rain begins; a window seat on a long journey; an animal you have watched closely.

Haiku requirements:
1. Three lines (approximately 5-7-5 syllables – flexibility is allowed)
2. ONE concrete observation; no stated feelings ("happy", "sad", "nice")
3. ONE specific physical detail (one leaf, one shadow, one sound)
4. ONE turn or surprise – the third line shifts the perspective
question_json.prompt: Three lines, 5-7-5...
question_json.instruction: Three lines, approximately 5-7-5 syllables. Include: ONE concrete observation (no stated feelings), ONE specific physical detail, and ONE turn or surprise at the end.

The chair pushed in tight –
a single jacket, hanging.

answer_json.modelAnswer:

question_json.question:
Choose the best improvement.

A pupil is writing an extended metaphor poem. They have started:

"A book is like a door.
It opens to a different place.
You go through it and come out somewhere new."

Which improvement is most powerful?
options: Add more lines so the poem is longer.

Develop the metaphor with specific concrete details that extend the comparison: WHAT kind of door (heavy, light, easy to open, locked at first)? Who passes through it? What lies on either side? Is it a door you can come back through, or only one way? Extend the comparison until the metaphor reveals something the literal description cannot. The poem should ARGUE the metaphor, not just state it.
Add rhyming words at the end of each line.
Replace the word "door" with a more interesting word like "gateway" or "portal".
question_json.correct: Develop the metaphor with specific concrete details that extend the comparison: WHAT kind of door (heavy, light, easy to open, locked at first)? Who passes through it? What lies on either side? Is it a door you can come back through, or only one way? Extend the comparison until the metaphor reveals something the literal description cannot. The poem should ARGUE the metaphor, not just state it.

feedback.correct: Correct! An extended metaphor poem develops the comparison across the whole poem. "A book is like a door" is the first line – good. But then the poem must argue the metaphor: what kind of door? Heavy or light? Locked at first? One way or two ways? Each new detail must extend the comparison and reveal something. Compare with Model 4: "A river knows" returns over and over, each time adding a new aspect. The metaphor accumulates meaning.
feedback.wrong: An extended metaphor poem must develop the comparison through specific details that extend it. "A book is like a door" is only the start. Ask: what kind of door? Heavy? Locked? One-way? Each detail must add something the literal description cannot. The poem should ARGUE the metaphor across multiple stanzas, like Model 4 argues the river.

answer_json.correct: Develop the metaphor with specific concrete details that extend the comparison: WHAT kind of door (heavy, light, easy to open, locked at first)? Who passes through it? What lies on either side? Is it a door you can come back through, or only one way? Extend the comparison until the metaphor reveals something the literal description cannot. The poem should ARGUE the metaphor, not just state it.

question_json.question:
Choose the most complete analysis.

Read the closing of Model 4 (What Rivers Know):

"And a river knows
the sea is not the end of it –
it is simply the next form.

What do you know,
the way a river knows?"

Why is this ending effective?
options: It ends with a question, which is a common technique in poetry.

The penultimate stanza extends the metaphor (the sea is not the end – it is simply the next form). Then the final stanza turns the entire poem outward: the poem has been describing the river for nine stanzas, but now it reveals that the river has been a model all along. The reader is invited to compare themselves. The closing question makes the whole poem retroactively address the reader.
It uses two short stanzas to balance the longer ones earlier in the poem.
It explains what the river knows so the reader understands the message.
question_json.correct: The penultimate stanza extends the metaphor (the sea is not the end – it is simply the next form). Then the final stanza turns the entire poem outward: the poem has been describing the river for nine stanzas, but now it reveals that the river has been a model all along. The reader is invited to compare themselves. The closing question makes the whole poem retroactively address the reader.

feedback.correct: Correct! The ending does two things in two stanzas: first, the penultimate stanza extends the metaphor one final time (the sea as "the next form" – not death, but transformation); second, the final question turns the poem outward. The whole poem has been about the river. The final two lines reveal the poem was always about the reader, and the river was the way of teaching it. This is the highest level of extended metaphor.
feedback.wrong: The ending does two things: extends the metaphor once more (the sea as "the next form" rather than an end), and turns the entire poem outward. The reader realises the poem was always addressing them. The river was the teacher, not the subject. This kind of turn at the end of an extended metaphor poem is what gives the form its power.

answer_json.correct: The penultimate stanza extends the metaphor (the sea is not the end – it is simply the next form). Then the final stanza turns the entire poem outward: the poem has been describing the river for nine stanzas, but now it reveals that the river has been a model all along. The reader is invited to compare themselves. The closing question makes the whole poem retroactively address the reader.

question_json.question:
Choose the best form and rationale.

A pupil wants to write a poem about how their grandmother always smelled of lavender. Which form would suit this subject MOST powerfully – and why?
options: A 14-line rhyming sonnet, because sonnets are the most respected form of poetry.

A short free verse poem with one specific image (the grandmother's sleeve, a folded handkerchief, a closed cupboard) and one turn at the end. The compression of free verse suits a subject built around one specific scent and one specific person. A haiku might also work – the shape of memory often fits the haiku discipline.
A shape poem in the shape of a flower, because grandmothers like flowers.
An acrostic poem spelling the word LAVENDER, because that is the subject.
question_json.correct: A short free verse poem with one specific image (the grandmother's sleeve, a folded handkerchief, a closed cupboard) and one turn at the end. The compression of free verse suits a subject built around one specific scent and one specific person. A haiku might also work – the shape of memory often fits the haiku discipline.

feedback.correct: Correct! Free verse (or possibly haiku) suits this subject best because the subject is built around one specific scent and one specific person – the form must do compression, not decoration. A sonnet would force fourteen lines onto a subject that may need only six or seven. A shape poem would distract from the genuine memory. An acrostic risks mechanical writing. The form should serve the subject, not the other way round.
feedback.wrong: Free verse or haiku suits the subject best because the memory is built around one specific scent. The form should be a vessel for the compression. A sonnet (14 lines, regular metre) would force the subject to fit the form rather than the other way round. A shape poem or acrostic would impose mechanical constraints that distract from the genuine observation.

answer_json.correct: A short free verse poem with one specific image (the grandmother's sleeve, a folded handkerchief, a closed cupboard) and one turn at the end. The compression of free verse suits a subject built around one specific scent and one specific person. A haiku might also work – the shape of memory often fits the haiku discipline.

question_json.question:
Use the formula to write THREE LINES of poetry in your PWP Booklet. Choose your own subject – do not copy the example.

Formula: [One compressed image or concrete observation] + [a turn or unexpected comparison] + [a resonant or surprising final detail]

Example:
The last light left the classroom window
like someone giving up an argument –
outside, the playground held its shapes in the dark.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

Formula: [One compressed image or concrete observation] + [a turn or unexpected comparison] + [a resonant or surprising final detail]

Example:
The last light left the classroom window
like someone giving up an argument –
outside, the playground held its shapes in the dark.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is the main difference between a "lesson task" and a "writing project"?
options: A lesson task is harder and a writing project is easier.
A lesson task is something you finish in one or two lessons. A writing project is something extended that you plan, develop, and refine over several weeks, applying everything you have learned.
A lesson task uses pen and paper. A writing project must be typed.
A lesson task is for younger pupils. A writing project is only for older pupils.
question_json.correct: A lesson task is something you finish in one or two lessons. A writing project is something extended that you plan, develop, and refine over several weeks, applying everything you have learned.

feedback.correct: Correct! A writing project is extended work over several weeks. It draws on everything you have learned across all 62 lessons. You plan it, develop it, refine it, and produce something you are proud of. Tasks build skills; projects apply them all together.
feedback.wrong: A writing project is extended work that draws on all the skills you have built. The difference is not difficulty or format – it is scope. A project has a beginning, a planned middle, and a deliberate end, and produces something whole.

answer_json.correct: A lesson task is something you finish in one or two lessons. A writing project is something extended that you plan, develop, and refine over several weeks, applying everything you have learned.

question_json.question:
Identify the writing project.

Which of these is a WRITING PROJECT (not a single lesson task)?
options: Writing one paragraph using show don't tell.
Practising five sentences using a PWP formula.
Writing a collection of eight poems on the theme of "things my grandmother taught me", planning the sequence, drafting each poem, and revising over five weeks.
Identifying figurative language in a single passage.
question_json.correct: Writing a collection of eight poems on the theme of "things my grandmother taught me", planning the sequence, drafting each poem, and revising over five weeks.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C is a project: extended work over several weeks, with planning, drafting, revising, and a deliberate whole. The other options are single lesson tasks – useful, but completed in one sitting.
feedback.wrong: Option C is the project: a themed sequence of poems, planned and drafted over weeks. The other options are single tasks completed in one or two lessons. Projects have scope; tasks have lessons.

answer_json.correct: Writing a collection of eight poems on the theme of "things my grandmother taught me", planning the sequence, drafting each poem, and revising over five weeks.

question_json.question:
Click a pathway on the left, then its description on the right.

Match each of the five WriFe project pathways to its description.
question_json.instruction: Click a pathway on the left, then its description on the right.

pairs.left: Pathway A: Short Story / Picture Book

question_json.question:
Choose the correct pathway.

A pupil says: "I want to write a guidebook for new pupils about how to find their way around the school, what each room is for, and where to go if they need help."

Which pathway is this?
options: Pathway A: Short Story / Picture Book
question_json.correct: Pathway E: Information Book

feedback.correct: Correct! This is Pathway E (Information Book). The purpose is to inform and explain. The audience is new pupils who need to know things. The skills come from L57 (instructions/explanations), L41 (factual writing), and L23 (sentence variety).
feedback.wrong: This is Pathway E (Information Book). The clue is the purpose: explaining and informing. New pupils need to know things; the project tells them. Information books include guidebooks, recipe collections, how-to manuals, and explanatory blogs.

answer_json.correct: Pathway E: Information Book

question_json.question:
Choose the most likely primary audience.

A pupil is planning a series of news articles about the wildlife living near the school – squirrels, foxes, birds, the changes through the seasons – published as a small magazine.

What is the PRIMARY AUDIENCE for this project?
options: Future university researchers studying urban wildlife.
Pupils, teachers, and families in the school community who want to know more about wildlife in their area.
Government wildlife officials who make policy decisions.
International readers interested in British wildlife.
question_json.correct: Pupils, teachers, and families in the school community who want to know more about wildlife in their area.

feedback.correct: Correct! The primary audience is the school community – the people closest to the wildlife being reported on. Identifying audience clearly is one of the most important parts of choosing a project. The audience determines voice, vocabulary, and format.
feedback.wrong: The primary audience is the school community – people who see the same wildlife. Audience determines everything else: vocabulary level, photograph style, article length, where it is published. Always choose your primary audience first.

answer_json.correct: Pupils, teachers, and families in the school community who want to know more about wildlife in their area.

question_json.question:
Choose the primary purpose.

A pupil wants to design a campaign – posters, a speech, and a class assembly – to convince fellow pupils to use less single-use plastic.

What is the PRIMARY PURPOSE?
options: To entertain pupils with creative writing about plastic.
To persuade pupils to change their behaviour.
To describe what single-use plastic looks like.
question_json.correct: To persuade pupils to change their behaviour.

feedback.correct: Correct! The primary purpose is to PERSUADE. The pupil wants behaviour change. This is Pathway D (Campaign), drawing on L55 (argument), L59 (posters), and L60 (speech writing). The whole campaign is designed around persuasive techniques.
feedback.wrong: The primary purpose is to persuade. Information may be included, but it is in service of changing behaviour. This is Pathway D (Campaign). Always identify whether your project is informing, entertaining, persuading, or expressing – the purpose shapes every choice.

answer_json.correct: To persuade pupils to change their behaviour.

question_json.question:
Choose the most achievable scope.

A pupil is planning a project but they have only FOUR WEEKS to complete it (drafting, editing, sharing). Which of these is the MOST REALISTIC scope?
options: A 200-page novel with twelve characters and three major plot arcs.
A poetry collection of 50 poems, each in a different traditional form.
A picture book of 8-12 pages with a single story arc and matching illustrations.
A complete monthly magazine with 30 articles by different writers.
question_json.correct: A picture book of 8-12 pages with a single story arc and matching illustrations.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C is realistic: 8-12 pages is a manageable size, a single story arc keeps the project focused, illustrations can be simple. The others are too ambitious for four weeks – the project would be abandoned half-finished. Realistic scope is what makes projects actually finish.
feedback.wrong: Option C is realistic. The other options would be impossible in four weeks – they are not bad ideas, just too big. Realistic scope is the difference between projects that finish and projects that get abandoned. Always cut your idea down until you can imagine finishing it.

answer_json.correct: A picture book of 8-12 pages with a single story arc and matching illustrations.

question_json.question:
Choose the most research-intensive project.

Which of these projects is MOST AMBITIOUS – meaning it requires substantial research and consultation with real people?
options: A short story about an imagined character solving a mystery.
A poetry collection on the changing seasons in your garden.
A magazine of biographies of three local people who do interesting work, including interviews and photographs.
A guidebook to your favourite books and games.
question_json.correct: A magazine of biographies of three local people who do interesting work, including interviews and photographs.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C requires interviews with real people, photographs, fact-checking, and background research. This is what professional journalism involves. The other projects can draw mainly on imagination, observation, and existing knowledge.
feedback.wrong: Option C requires real-world research: interviews, photographs, fact-checking. This is the most research-intensive. Stories and poetry draw mainly on imagination and observation; guidebooks draw on existing knowledge. Journalism uniquely requires going out to find information.

answer_json.correct: A magazine of biographies of three local people who do interesting work, including interviews and photographs.

Click a pathway on the left, then the planning tool on the right.

Match each pathway to the planning tool that suits it best.
question_json.instruction: Click a pathway on the left, then the planning tool on the right.

pairs.right: Connect Grid (5-part) – Opening, Build-up, Problem, Resolution, Ending
Themed sequence map – list of poem ideas with form and length for each
Editorial calendar – articles with angles, word counts, and structure
Outline / Table of Contents – section breakdown with sub-topics
Connect Grid (5-part) – Opening, Build-up, Problem, Resolution, Ending
Themed sequence map – list of poem ideas with form and length for each
Editorial calendar – articles with angles, word counts, and structure
Outline / Table of Contents – section breakdown with sub-topics

question_json.question:
Identify all three problems.

A pupil says: "I want to write something about football because I like football."

What are the THREE main problems with this as a project idea?
options: It does not specify what kind of writing (story? journalism? poetry? guidebook?), it does not name an audience, and it does not say what specifically about football.
Football is too popular as a topic and would be hard to research.
The pupil should choose a different sport because football has too many books written about it already.
Personal interest is not a good reason to choose a project.
question_json.correct: It does not specify what kind of writing (story? journalism? poetry? guidebook?), it does not name an audience, and it does not say what specifically about football.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three missing pieces: WHAT KIND of writing? FOR WHO? ABOUT WHAT specifically? "I like football" is a starting point, not a project. The improvement: "I want to write a magazine of player profiles for pupils in my school who love football, focusing on local academy players whose stories aren't well known." Now it is a project.
feedback.wrong: Three missing pieces: what kind of writing (pathway), who for (audience), what specifically about football (focus). "I like football" is a starting point, not a project. Personal interest is the BEST reason to choose – but the interest must be sharpened into a specific project before work can begin.

answer_json.correct: It does not specify what kind of writing (story? journalism? poetry? guidebook?), it does not name an audience, and it does not say what specifically about football.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct three elements.

A strong project proposal needs THREE core elements. Which option lists them correctly?
options: A title, a length, and a deadline.
A subject (what the project is about), an audience (who it is for), and a purpose (what change you want in the reader).
A theme, a colour, and an illustration.
A name, a date, and a teacher's signature.
question_json.correct: A subject (what the project is about), an audience (who it is for), and a purpose (what change you want in the reader).

feedback.correct: Correct! Subject + Audience + Purpose. These three answer the most important questions: WHAT, WHO FOR, and WHY. A project missing any of the three is incomplete. Title, length, and deadline are useful too, but they come after the core three.
feedback.wrong: Subject + Audience + Purpose. Without these three, no project has a foundation. The subject answers "what is this about?" The audience answers "who is this for?" The purpose answers "why am I making this and what should it do?"

answer_json.correct: A subject (what the project is about), an audience (who it is for), and a purpose (what change you want in the reader).

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this project pitch sentence with the missing word.
question_json.template: "I want to write a poetry collection about my grandmother's garden, for my mother and sisters,___the garden is part of our family history and I want to capture it before it changes."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Because" – the word that gives the project its REASON. Every strong project pitch contains a "because" clause: why this matters to YOU. The "because" is what carries you through the difficult middle weeks when projects nearly get abandoned.

question_json.question:
Choose the most important question.

Which of these is the MOST USEFUL question to ask BEFORE starting a project?
options: "How long does it have to be?"
"Who will read this when it is finished, and what do I want them to feel or do?"
"Is this the easiest project I can do?"
question_json.correct: "Who will read this when it is finished, and what do I want them to feel or do?"

feedback.correct: Correct! Audience + intended effect = the most useful pre-project question. Until you know who will read it and what you want them to feel/do, you cannot make any other decision well. The other questions are administrative; this one is structural.
feedback.wrong: "Who will read this and what do I want them to feel?" Without this question, you cannot choose voice, vocabulary, length, or any other element well. Audience + purpose come first; everything else flows from them.

answer_json.correct: "Who will read this when it is finished, and what do I want them to feel or do?"

question_json.question:
Choose the best improvement.

A pupil has written this project pitch:

"My project will be a thing about animals. Animals are interesting. I will write about them."

Which rewrite is the BEST improvement?

"My project will be a thing about animals. Animals are interesting. I will write about them."
options: "My project will be a long thing about many different animals. I think animals are very interesting and there is a lot to say about them."

"My project will be a magazine of three feature articles about urban foxes – their feeding habits, how families adapt to city life, and what residents notice about them. The audience is families in our area, and I want them to notice the foxes around them rather than seeing only pests."
"Animals are good and bad. I will explore both sides of the question."
question_json.correct: "My project will be a magazine of three feature articles about urban foxes – their feeding habits, how families adapt to city life, and what residents notice about them. The audience is families in our area, and I want them to notice the foxes around them rather than seeing only pests."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option C is specific in every way: pathway (magazine = Pathway C journalism), subject (urban foxes), structure (three feature articles), audience (local families), and purpose (notice rather than dismiss). Each element is named. The pupil could start research tomorrow with this proposal.
feedback.wrong: Option C names the pathway, the specific subject, the structure, the audience, and the purpose. A strong proposal answers all five at once. The other options are still vague – they could not be started without further questions.

answer_json.correct: "My project will be a magazine of three feature articles about urban foxes – their feeding habits, how families adapt to city life, and what residents notice about them. The audience is families in our area, and I want them to notice the foxes around them rather than seeing only pests."

question_json.question:
Which pupil has chosen better based on their strengths?

Two pupils are choosing projects. Which one has chosen WELL based on their strengths?

Pupil 1: "My strongest WriFe lessons were figurative language (L45) and poetry (L62). I am going to write a 100-page detective novel because I have always wanted to."

Pupil 2: "My strongest WriFe lessons were figurative language (L45) and poetry (L62). I am going to write a poetry collection of eight poems on the theme of 'small things people overlook' for the school anthology."
options: Pupil 1, because writing what you have always wanted to is more important than your strengths.

Pupil 2, because they have chosen a project that builds on their actual strongest skills. The detective novel is admirable, but it is a stretch project not a strengths project. With four weeks, Pupil 2 will produce something they can be proud of.
Both have chosen well because there are no wrong choices in project work.
Neither – both should choose journalism instead.
question_json.correct: Pupil 2, because they have chosen a project that builds on their actual strongest skills. The detective novel is admirable, but it is a stretch project not a strengths project. With four weeks, Pupil 2 will produce something they can be proud of.

feedback.correct: Correct! Pupil 2 has played to their strengths: poetry skills + figurative language fit a poetry collection perfectly. Pupil 1 may eventually write the detective novel, but starting it as their first major project is a stretch that often does not finish. The advice: USE this project to build on your strengths. Save the stretch projects for after you have one finished project under your belt.
feedback.wrong: Pupil 2 has chosen well. Project work is the moment to apply your strongest skills, not test your weakest ones. Pupil 1 may write the novel one day – but this four-week project is the wrong time to start it. Strengths first; stretches later.

answer_json.correct: Pupil 2, because they have chosen a project that builds on their actual strongest skills. The detective novel is admirable, but it is a stretch project not a strengths project. With four weeks, Pupil 2 will produce something they can be proud of.

question_json.question:
Use the formula: I want to make [type of project] about [subject] for [audience], because [reason]. This is a draft – your real proposal will come on the worksheet.

Write your own three-line project pitch.

Formula: I want to make [type of project] about [subject] for [audience], because [reason].

Example: I want to make a poetry collection about the trees in my street for my neighbours and teachers, because the oldest tree was cut down last summer and I want to remember it before the others go too.
question_json.prompt: I want to make...
question_json.instruction: Use the formula: I want to make [type of project] about [subject] for [audience], because [reason]. This is a draft – your real proposal will come on the worksheet.
question_json.modelAnswer: I want to make a magazine of three feature articles about local volunteers (the lollipop lady, the community library helper, the youth football coach) for families in our area, because these people make our lives better and most of us never know their names.

answer_json.modelAnswer: I want to make a magazine of three feature articles about local volunteers (the lollipop lady, the community library helper, the youth football coach) for families in our area, because these people make our lives better and most of us never know their names.

question_json.question:
Choose the most accurate analysis.

A pupil shows you this project proposal:

"PROJECT: A picture book called 'When Granny Was Small' for my five-year-old cousin, who has just started school. Eight pages with simple sentences, illustrations of my granny's 1960s school. I want him to feel less worried about starting school by seeing that grannies were small once too."

What are the THREE strongest features of this proposal?
options: It uses lots of adjectives and includes a title with quotation marks.
It is short, has a clear title, and is about family.

The pathway is clear (picture book = Pathway A); the audience is specific (a five-year-old cousin starting school); and the purpose is precise and emotional (to ease his worry through a particular insight). All five elements – subject, pathway, audience, purpose, scope – are present in three sentences.
It uses the word "want" which shows enthusiasm.
question_json.correct: The pathway is clear (picture book = Pathway A); the audience is specific (a five-year-old cousin starting school); and the purpose is precise and emotional (to ease his worry through a particular insight). All five elements – subject, pathway, audience, purpose, scope – are present in three sentences.

feedback.correct: Correct! All five elements are present: subject (Granny's 1960s childhood), pathway (picture book = A), audience (specific five-year-old cousin), purpose (ease worry about school), scope (eight pages, simple sentences). The proposal also has a "because" buried in it – the cousin's situation is the reason. This is what a strong proposal looks like.
feedback.wrong: The proposal has all five elements: subject, pathway, audience, purpose, and scope. The audience is one specific person, which is more powerful than "children". The purpose is precise (ease school worry). And the "because" is implied (the cousin is starting school). This is a model proposal.

answer_json.correct: The pathway is clear (picture book = Pathway A); the audience is specific (a five-year-old cousin starting school); and the purpose is precise and emotional (to ease his worry through a particular insight). All five elements – subject, pathway, audience, purpose, scope – are present in three sentences.

question_json.question:
Use the formula to write your project pitch in your PWP Booklet. This pitch goes into your booklet as the bridge between your PWP composition and your Chapter 6 project.

Formula: I want to make [project type] about [subject] for [audience], because [reason that matters to me].

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

Formula: I want to make [project type] about [subject] for [audience], because [reason that matters to me].

Example: I want to make a guidebook to good places to read in our school – the library window seat, the bench by the science pond, the corner of the hall stage – for new pupils who don't yet know where the calm places are, because moving school is harder than people remember.
question_json.prompt: I want to make...
question_json.instruction:
Use the formula to write your project pitch in your PWP Booklet. This pitch goes into your booklet as the bridge between your PWP composition and your Chapter 6 project.

Formula: I want to make [project type] about [subject] for [audience], because [reason that matters to me].
question_json.modelAnswer: I want to make a poetry collection about the small jobs my mother does in the kitchen at the end of every day for the family, because she has done these things for fifteen years and no one has ever written them down.

answer_json.modelAnswer: I want to make a poetry collection about the small jobs my mother does in the kitchen at the end of every day for the family, because she has done these things for fifteen years and no one has ever written them down.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is the difference between a project PROPOSAL and a project CONCEPT?
options: A proposal is shorter than a concept.

A proposal answers "what am I making?" - it names the project. A concept answers "what will make this WORK?" - it adds the audience research, scope realism, model deconstruction, and key features that turn an idea into a workable plan.
A proposal is for the teacher; a concept is for the pupil.
A proposal is written first and a concept is written second, but they have the same elements.
question_json.correct: A proposal answers "what am I making?" - it names the project. A concept answers "what will make this WORK?" - it adds the audience research, scope realism, model deconstruction, and key features that turn an idea into a workable plan.

feedback.correct: Correct! A proposal names the project (subject + audience + because + scope). A concept develops the proposal into something that will actually work: it adds research about the audience, deconstruction of a real model, refinement of theme, and identification of the key text features. The concept is the bridge between idea and plan.
feedback.wrong: A proposal answers "what am I making?" A concept answers "what will make this WORK?" The concept adds audience research, model deconstruction, theme refinement, and key features. Without these, the proposal cannot become a finished project.

answer_json.correct: A proposal answers "what am I making?" - it names the project. A concept answers "what will make this WORK?" - it adds the audience research, scope realism, model deconstruction, and key features that turn an idea into a workable plan.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct six elements.

A strong CONCEPT DOCUMENT contains six elements. Which option lists them correctly?
options: Title, length, deadline, format, font, layout.
Theme (refined subject), Audience (researched), Purpose (intended effect), Scope (realistic), Working Title, Key Features (the text characteristics).
Subject, structure, sentence-types, spelling, sources, summary.
Pathway, plot, problem, place, people, point.
question_json.correct: Theme (refined subject), Audience (researched), Purpose (intended effect), Scope (realistic), Working Title, Key Features (the text characteristics).

feedback.correct: Correct! Theme + Audience + Purpose + Scope + Working Title + Key Features. The six elements together turn a proposal into a workable concept. The Theme is the proposal's subject after research and refinement; the Audience now includes what you have learned about real readers; Key Features are the specific text qualities (e.g. "third-person narration", "ABAB rhyme scheme", "interview-based").
feedback.wrong: Theme + Audience + Purpose + Scope + Working Title + Key Features. The Theme is more refined than the proposal's subject. The Audience now reflects research, not just guesses. Key Features list the specific text characteristics. Together these six elements describe how the project will work.

answer_json.correct: Theme (refined subject), Audience (researched), Purpose (intended effect), Scope (realistic), Working Title, Key Features (the text characteristics).

question_json.question:
Click an element on the left, then its question on the right.

Match each concept element to what it answers.
question_json.instruction: Click an element on the left, then its question on the right.

question_json.question:
Choose the concept-level addition.

A pupil submitted this proposal in L63: "I will make a magazine of three feature articles about local volunteers for families in our area, because these people make our lives better."

Which of these is a CONCEPT-LEVEL refinement (going beyond proposal to working plan)?
options: "My project will be a thing about volunteers."

"I have spoken with two families in our street and learned that they would value short articles (300-400 words each) with a portrait photograph. I will profile the lollipop lady, the library helper, and the youth football coach. Each article will follow the structure: opening hook, three paragraphs of biography and impact, one quote from a service user, closing line returning to the hook."
"I want my magazine to be really good."
"My audience will be everyone who reads."
question_json.correct: "I have spoken with two families in our street and learned that they would value short articles (300-400 words each) with a portrait photograph. I will profile the lollipop lady, the library helper, and the youth football coach. Each article will follow the structure: opening hook, three paragraphs of biography and impact, one quote from a service user, closing line returning to the hook."

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is concept-level: it includes audience research (spoke with two families), scope realism (300-400 words each, three specific volunteers named), and key features (specific structure for each article). The proposal becomes workable. The other options are vague restatements - no advance toward planning.
feedback.wrong: Option B does the concept work: audience research, scope realism, named subjects, defined key features. The other options simply restate or vagueify. Concept-level work makes the proposal workable - it tells the pupil exactly what to do tomorrow.

answer_json.correct: "I have spoken with two families in our street and learned that they would value short articles (300-400 words each) with a portrait photograph. I will profile the lollipop lady, the library helper, and the youth football coach. Each article will follow the structure: opening hook, three paragraphs of biography and impact, one quote from a service user, closing line returning to the hook."

question_json.question:
Choose the most useful insight.

A pupil planning a poetry collection on "things my mother does in the kitchen" has just researched the audience (her mother and aunts). What audience research insight is MOST USEFUL for the concept?
options: My mother prefers the colour blue.

My aunts read poetry occasionally and prefer poems with concrete images over abstract themes. They have said they want to recognise specific moments in the poems, not just "a kitchen". They will read it as a printed gift.
Poetry collections are popular at the moment.
My mother and aunts are all women.
question_json.correct: My aunts read poetry occasionally and prefer poems with concrete images over abstract themes. They have said they want to recognise specific moments in the poems, not just "a kitchen". They will read it as a printed gift.

feedback.correct: Correct! The useful insight is what the audience prefers and what they want from the work. "Concrete images" tells the pupil to use specific details (the kettle, the tea towel, the locking of doors). "Recognise specific moments" tells the pupil to write about actual remembered scenes. "Printed gift" tells the pupil about the format. Audience research that changes design choices is useful research.
feedback.wrong: Useful audience research changes design choices. The pupil now knows to use concrete imagery (not abstract themes), to write about specific remembered moments, and to plan a printed format. Generic facts (likes blue, are women) do not change the project. The test for useful research: does it tell the pupil what to do differently?

answer_json.correct: My aunts read poetry occasionally and prefer poems with concrete images over abstract themes. They have said they want to recognise specific moments in the poems, not just "a kitchen". They will read it as a printed gift.

question_json.question:
Choose the strongest example.

Look at this concept extract for a Pathway A picture book project:

"Theme: Granny in school, 1960s, for my five-year-old cousin Ezra who has just started school.
Audience research: I have read three picture books with Ezra. He likes books with the same character on every page, simple sentences, and one or two surprises. He found one book with too many words boring.
Key features: 8 pages, 1-2 sentences per page, the same character (Granny aged 5) on every page, one surprise per page, illustrations of 1960s school items."

Which element BEST shows that audience research has shaped concept decisions?
options: The decision to keep ONE main character on every page (because Ezra likes books that way) and the choice of 1-2 sentences per page (because Ezra found a wordy book boring). The audience research became design choices.
question_json.correct: The decision to keep ONE main character on every page (because Ezra likes books that way) and the choice of 1-2 sentences per page (because Ezra found a wordy book boring). The audience research became design choices.

feedback.correct: Correct! The audience research changed the design. The pupil decided to keep one character per page and use short sentences SPECIFICALLY because of what they observed when reading with Ezra. This is what concept-level audience research does - it shapes concrete design decisions. Without research, the pupil might have used multiple characters or longer sentences.
feedback.wrong: The audience research became design decisions: one character per page (because Ezra likes that), short sentences (because longer ones bored him). The other features are reasonable but not visibly research-driven. The mark of strong concept research is when you can trace a design decision back to an observation about the audience.

answer_json.correct: The decision to keep ONE main character on every page (because Ezra likes books that way) and the choice of 1-2 sentences per page (because Ezra found a wordy book boring). The audience research became design choices.
