question_json.question:
Choose the most useful two observations.

A pupil is doing model deconstruction on a published picture book for their Pathway A project. Which TWO observations are most useful for THEIR concept?
options: The book has a green cover and the author has a French name.

(1) The protagonist appears on every page in the first half, then disappears for two pages before returning - creating a "missing" feeling that the ending resolves. (2) The illustrator uses warm colours for safe scenes and cool blues for moments of worry, training the reader without telling them.
The book is 32 pages long and was published in 2019.
The book has dedications at the front and an "About the Author" page at the back.
question_json.correct: (1) The protagonist appears on every page in the first half, then disappears for two pages before returning - creating a "missing" feeling that the ending resolves. (2) The illustrator uses warm colours for safe scenes and cool blues for moments of worry, training the reader without telling them.

feedback.correct: Correct! Useful deconstruction observations identify TECHNIQUES that the pupil could borrow or adapt. The protagonist disappearing to create longing - a teachable technique. The colour-coding of emotional states - a teachable technique. The pupil could now consider: should my protagonist disappear at some point? Should my colour palette match my emotional moments? Useful research changes design decisions.
feedback.wrong: Useful deconstruction identifies teachable techniques - things the pupil could adapt for their own project. Cover colour and publication year are facts, not techniques. The disappearing protagonist and emotion-coded colours ARE techniques the pupil could adapt. The test: does this observation give me an idea I could use?

answer_json.correct: (1) The protagonist appears on every page in the first half, then disappears for two pages before returning - creating a "missing" feeling that the ending resolves. (2) The illustrator uses warm colours for safe scenes and cool blues for moments of worry, training the reader without telling them.

question_json.question:
Choose the most concept-changing insight.

A pupil planning a Pathway D campaign about reducing single-use plastic in the school canteen has been deconstructing a real charity poster. Which deconstruction insight will MOST IMPROVE their concept?
options: The poster uses three colours: red, white, and black.

The poster uses ONE clear statistic ("8 million tonnes of plastic enter the ocean each year") and ONE simple call to action ("Refuse the straw"). The pupil has decided their campaign will follow the same structure: ONE statistic, ONE action.
The poster is A3 sized and printed on glossy paper.
The poster has a website address at the bottom in small text.
question_json.correct: The poster uses ONE clear statistic ("8 million tonnes of plastic enter the ocean each year") and ONE simple call to action ("Refuse the straw"). The pupil has decided their campaign will follow the same structure: ONE statistic, ONE action.

feedback.correct: Correct! The most useful insight is the structural decision: ONE statistic + ONE call to action. Many beginner campaigns fail by including too many statistics and too many requested actions. Adopting the model's discipline (one of each) will substantially improve the concept. This is concept-changing research.
feedback.wrong: The structural insight (ONE statistic, ONE call to action) is what will most change the campaign. Beginner campaigns fail by trying to do everything; expert campaigns make one clear point and ask for one specific thing. Adopting that discipline transforms the concept.

answer_json.correct: The poster uses ONE clear statistic ("8 million tonnes of plastic enter the ocean each year") and ONE simple call to action ("Refuse the straw"). The pupil has decided their campaign will follow the same structure: ONE statistic, ONE action.

question_json.question:
Identify all three weaknesses.

A pupil has produced this draft concept:

"Theme: Football. Audience: People who like football. Purpose: To be interesting. Scope: A magazine. Key features: Some articles about football."

What are the THREE main weaknesses?
options: The font is too small and there are not enough adjectives.

Theme is too broad ("Football" - what specific aspect?), Audience is generic ("people who like football" - which people, where?), and Key Features are vague ("some articles" - how many, what about, in what order?). The concept needs the same SPECIFICITY required of the proposal, plus the depth that comes from research.
The pupil should choose a different sport instead of football.
The concept is too short and needs to be longer.
question_json.correct: Theme is too broad ("Football" - what specific aspect?), Audience is generic ("people who like football" - which people, where?), and Key Features are vague ("some articles" - how many, what about, in what order?). The concept needs the same SPECIFICITY required of the proposal, plus the depth that comes from research.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three weaknesses: vague theme, generic audience, vague key features. A concept must add SPECIFICITY plus DEPTH. Compare with: "Theme: local academy football players (Years 7-9) at our city club. Audience: pupils in Years 5-6 who follow the senior team but don't know about the academy. Purpose: to introduce these players as people, not statistics. Scope: three feature articles, 400 words each, with one photograph. Key features: opening hook from a personal moment, three biography paragraphs, two quotes per article, closing line returning to the opening." Now it works.
feedback.wrong: Three weaknesses: vague theme, generic audience, vague key features. A concept needs MORE specificity than a proposal, not less. The pupil has gone backwards from L63. The fix: name the specific aspect, the specific audience, the specific structure. Each element should answer the design question concretely.

answer_json.correct: Theme is too broad ("Football" - what specific aspect?), Audience is generic ("people who like football" - which people, where?), and Key Features are vague ("some articles" - how many, what about, in what order?). The concept needs the same SPECIFICITY required of the proposal, plus the depth that comes from research.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this audience research insight with the missing word.
question_json.template: "My audience research is useful when it changes a___decision in my project, not just when it tells me a fact about my reader."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Design" - audience research must change DESIGN decisions. Knowing that your reader prefers shorter sentences = useful (changes your sentence length). Knowing that your reader was born in March = not useful (changes nothing). The test for every research insight: does it change what I will actually do?

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this scope reality test.
question_json.template: "If I cannot imagine FINISHING my project in the time available, the scope is too___and I must reduce it now, before planning begins."
question_json.feedback: Correct! Too "large" or "big" or "ambitious" - scope creep is the biggest single cause of unfinished projects. The reality test: can I actually IMAGINE the moment I finish this? If not, reduce the scope until you can. A finished short project is better than an abandoned long one.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct diagnosis.

A pupil has written this scope statement: "I will write a 30-poem collection across 12 different traditional forms, in 4 weeks."

Which diagnosis is correct?
options: This is a strong scope because it is specific.

The scope is wildly unrealistic. 30 poems in 12 forms in 4 weeks = average 7.5 poems per week, learning a new form roughly every 4 days. The pupil will produce many weak poems instead of a few strong ones. Reduce to 6-8 poems in 1 or 2 forms - this is achievable and produces quality work.
The scope is fine but the pupil should choose better forms.
The scope cannot be evaluated without more information.
question_json.correct: The scope is wildly unrealistic. 30 poems in 12 forms in 4 weeks = average 7.5 poems per week, learning a new form roughly every 4 days. The pupil will produce many weak poems instead of a few strong ones. Reduce to 6-8 poems in 1 or 2 forms - this is achievable and produces quality work.

feedback.correct: Correct! 30 poems in 12 forms in 4 weeks is impossible to do well. The maths shows it: roughly 7.5 poems per week, with a new form every 4 days. Beginner poets need time to find each form's voice. The realistic scope is 6-8 poems in 1 or 2 forms - this lets the pupil go deep rather than thin. The principle: depth beats breadth in beginner project work.
feedback.wrong: The scope is unrealistic. 30 poems + 12 forms + 4 weeks = quantity over quality. Reduce to 6-8 poems in 1 or 2 forms. Beginner project work should go DEEP into a small space, not THIN across a large one. A finished collection of 6 strong poems is better than an abandoned attempt at 30.

answer_json.correct: The scope is wildly unrealistic. 30 poems in 12 forms in 4 weeks = average 7.5 poems per week, learning a new form roughly every 4 days. The pupil will produce many weak poems instead of a few strong ones. Reduce to 6-8 poems in 1 or 2 forms - this is achievable and produces quality work.

question_json.question:
Choose the best refined theme.

A pupil with this L63 proposal: "I will make a how-to guide about caring for a first pet for pupils thinking about a first pet."

What is the BEST refined CONCEPT theme statement?
options: A 12-page guide called "Your First Pet" specifically for Years 5-6 pupils thinking about a small first pet (hamster, fish, or rabbit). After interviewing two families with first-pet experience, I have learned that pupils most need help with: choosing the right pet for their family, understanding actual costs, and knowing what could go wrong. The guide will have three sections matching these needs.
A guide for everyone interested in pets.
A pet guide that is informative and useful.
question_json.correct: A 12-page guide called "Your First Pet" specifically for Years 5-6 pupils thinking about a small first pet (hamster, fish, or rabbit). After interviewing two families with first-pet experience, I have learned that pupils most need help with: choosing the right pet for their family, understanding actual costs, and knowing what could go wrong. The guide will have three sections matching these needs.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is a refined theme: it adds the specific page count, the named pets (hamster, fish, rabbit), the specific year groups, the audience research findings, and the structure that emerges from those findings. Most importantly, the audience research has SHAPED the structure - the three sections match the three things pupils actually need help with. This is what a concept theme looks like.
feedback.wrong: Option B is the refined theme. It adds specific details (12 pages, three named pets, Years 5-6) and shows the audience research changing the design (three sections matching the three things pupils most need help with). The other options are vague restatements. A refined theme is concrete and reflects research.

answer_json.correct: A 12-page guide called "Your First Pet" specifically for Years 5-6 pupils thinking about a small first pet (hamster, fish, or rabbit). After interviewing two families with first-pet experience, I have learned that pupils most need help with: choosing the right pet for their family, understanding actual costs, and knowing what could go wrong. The guide will have three sections matching these needs.

question_json.question:
Choose the strongest key features list.

A pupil has this proposal: "I will write a campaign with a poster, a speech, and an open letter to encourage break-time games."

Which KEY FEATURES list shows the strongest concept-level work?
options: The poster will be colourful and the speech will be loud.

POSTER: A3, ONE statistic + ONE call to action, primary colour scheme, image hierarchy with the action centred. SPEECH: 90 seconds maximum, opens with a question, uses the rule of three, ends with the same question answered. OPEN LETTER: Formal letter format, three paragraphs (problem, evidence, request), specific addressee (Head Teacher by name), one specific request supported by one piece of evidence.
The campaign will have multiple parts and will be persuasive.
I will use lots of techniques to convince people.
question_json.correct: POSTER: A3, ONE statistic + ONE call to action, primary colour scheme, image hierarchy with the action centred. SPEECH: 90 seconds maximum, opens with a question, uses the rule of three, ends with the same question answered. OPEN LETTER: Formal letter format, three paragraphs (problem, evidence, request), specific addressee (Head Teacher by name), one specific request supported by one piece of evidence.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B specifies the structural decisions for each piece. ONE statistic + ONE call to action on the poster (drawn from L59). 90 seconds and rule of three for the speech (drawn from L60). Three paragraphs with specific structure for the letter (drawn from L56). Each piece has a defined architecture. The pupil can begin drafting tomorrow with this level of specificity.
feedback.wrong: Option B has the right level of detail: each campaign piece has its specific architecture defined. The other options are vague (colourful, loud, persuasive) and would not help anyone draft anything. Strong key features lists tell the pupil exactly what to do for each piece.

answer_json.correct: POSTER: A3, ONE statistic + ONE call to action, primary colour scheme, image hierarchy with the action centred. SPEECH: 90 seconds maximum, opens with a question, uses the rule of three, ends with the same question answered. OPEN LETTER: Formal letter format, three paragraphs (problem, evidence, request), specific addressee (Head Teacher by name), one specific request supported by one piece of evidence.

question_json.question:
Use the formula: "[Project type] called [working title] for [specific audience], focused on [the focused subject after research]. Scope: [realistic length and structure]."

Write a refined THEME statement for your own project.

Formula:
"[Project type] called [working title] for [specific audience], focused on [focused subject]. Scope: [realistic length and structure]."

Example (Pathway B Poetry):
"A poetry collection called 'Things My Mother Does' for my mother and aunts, focused on the small daily kitchen actions that no one has acknowledged. Scope: eight free-verse poems of 8-15 lines each, presented as a printed booklet."
question_json.prompt: A [project type] called...
question_json.instruction: Use the formula: "[Project type] called [working title] for [specific audience], focused on [the focused subject after research]. Scope: [realistic length and structure]."
question_json.modelAnswer: A picture book called 'When Granny Was Small' for my five-year-old cousin Ezra, focused on the surprises Granny experienced in her 1960s school. Scope: 8 pages, 1-2 sentences per page, the same character (Granny aged 5) on every page with one visible surprise per page.

answer_json.modelAnswer: A picture book called 'When Granny Was Small' for my five-year-old cousin Ezra, focused on the surprises Granny experienced in her 1960s school. Scope: 8 pages, 1-2 sentences per page, the same character (Granny aged 5) on every page with one visible surprise per page.

question_json.question:
Choose the most useful prescription.

CONCEPT DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil has written: "Theme: A poetry collection. Audience: My family. Purpose: They will like it."

Which prescription will MOST strengthen this concept?
options: Add more poems to make the collection longer.

Three prescriptions: (1) Refine the theme - WHAT specifically about your family will the poems be about? (2) Research the audience - speak with two family members about what kind of poetry they would actually want to read; what specific moments or themes would resonate. (3) Sharpen the purpose - replace "they will like it" with the specific feeling or recognition you want them to have when they read.
Choose a different family member as the audience.
Use more rhyming forms instead of free verse.
question_json.correct: Three prescriptions: (1) Refine the theme - WHAT specifically about your family will the poems be about? (2) Research the audience - speak with two family members about what kind of poetry they would actually want to read; what specific moments or themes would resonate. (3) Sharpen the purpose - replace "they will like it" with the specific feeling or recognition you want them to have when they read.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three prescriptions address the three weaknesses: vague theme (specific subject needed), unsearched audience (research needed), and generic purpose (specific intended effect needed). This is the Concept Doctor approach - identify each weak element and prescribe what will fix it.
feedback.wrong: Three prescriptions for three weaknesses: refine theme (specifically about what?), research audience (what do they actually want?), sharpen purpose (what specific feeling?). Concept Doctor diagnoses one weakness per element and prescribes the fix.

answer_json.correct: Three prescriptions: (1) Refine the theme - WHAT specifically about your family will the poems be about? (2) Research the audience - speak with two family members about what kind of poetry they would actually want to read; what specific moments or themes would resonate. (3) Sharpen the purpose - replace "they will like it" with the specific feeling or recognition you want them to have when they read.

question_json.question:
Choose the best prescription.

CONCEPT DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil planning a Pathway A short story has written: "I will write a 15,000 word fantasy novel with seven main characters, three different worlds, and a magic system based on emotions, in four weeks."

What is the BEST prescription?
options: Reduce to a 30-page novel and add more characters.

Honour the ambition while making the project finishable. Reduce drastically to: ONE protagonist, ONE world, ONE problem rooted in emotion. Aim for a 1500-2000 word short story OR a single chapter of the larger novel that establishes the protagonist and their problem. The full novel can become a long-term project after Chapter 6 - this is the start of it.
Tell the pupil they cannot do fantasy because it is too difficult.
Add more worlds and characters to make it more interesting.
question_json.correct: Honour the ambition while making the project finishable. Reduce drastically to: ONE protagonist, ONE world, ONE problem rooted in emotion. Aim for a 1500-2000 word short story OR a single chapter of the larger novel that establishes the protagonist and their problem. The full novel can become a long-term project after Chapter 6 - this is the start of it.

feedback.correct: Correct! Honour the ambition (do not dismiss the dream) while making the project finishable. The fix is drastic reduction: ONE protagonist, ONE world, ONE problem. The pupil produces a strong short story OR a published opening chapter, and can continue the larger novel after Chapter 6. This way the dream survives without sinking the project.
feedback.wrong: The right prescription honours the ambition without accepting the scope. ONE protagonist + ONE world + ONE problem = an achievable short story or opening chapter. The full novel can come later. Many published novelists worked this way - chapter by chapter, year by year. Today's project is just the start.

answer_json.correct: Honour the ambition while making the project finishable. Reduce drastically to: ONE protagonist, ONE world, ONE problem rooted in emotion. Aim for a 1500-2000 word short story OR a single chapter of the larger novel that establishes the protagonist and their problem. The full novel can become a long-term project after Chapter 6 - this is the start of it.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write the SIX-ELEMENT CONCEPT for your project. Each element on its own line.

1. THEME (refined subject): 
2. AUDIENCE (with research insight): 
3. PURPOSE (specific intended effect): 
4. SCOPE (realistic length and structure): 
5. WORKING TITLE: 
6. KEY FEATURES (specific text characteristics):

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

Six elements:
1. THEME: focused subject after research
2. AUDIENCE: with what you have learned about them
3. PURPOSE: specific intended effect
4. SCOPE: realistic length and structure
5. WORKING TITLE: a working name (can change)
6. KEY FEATURES: specific text characteristics
question_json.prompt:
1. THEME:
2. AUDIENCE:
3. PURPOSE:
4. SCOPE:
5. WORKING TITLE:
6. KEY FEATURES:
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write the SIX-ELEMENT CONCEPT for your project. Each element on its own line.

1. THEME (refined subject): 
2. AUDIENCE (with research insight): 
3. PURPOSE (specific intended effect): 
4. SCOPE (realistic length and structure): 
5. WORKING TITLE: 
6. KEY FEATURES (specific text characteristics):
question_json.modelAnswer:
1. THEME: the small daily kitchen actions of my mother that no one has noticed - the way she chooses fruit, the order of locking doors, the sound of the kettle she uses
2. AUDIENCE: my mother and aunts. I have spoken with my eldest aunt who said she would value poems where she could "recognise her sister" - so concrete moments, not abstract themes
3. PURPOSE: I want them to read it and have to put it down for a moment because they recognise something they thought no one had seen
4. SCOPE: 8 free-verse poems, 8-15 lines each, presented as a printed and bound A5 booklet given to my mother on her birthday
5. WORKING TITLE: "Things My Mother Does"
6. KEY FEATURES: free verse, present tense, one specific kitchen moment per poem, no abstract emotional language, a recurring small image (light through the kitchen window) appearing in three poems

answer_json.modelAnswer:
1. THEME: the small daily kitchen actions of my mother that no one has noticed - the way she chooses fruit, the order of locking doors, the sound of the kettle she uses
2. AUDIENCE: my mother and aunts. I have spoken with my eldest aunt who said she would value poems where she could "recognise her sister" - so concrete moments, not abstract themes
3. PURPOSE: I want them to read it and have to put it down for a moment because they recognise something they thought no one had seen
4. SCOPE: 8 free-verse poems, 8-15 lines each, presented as a printed and bound A5 booklet given to my mother on her birthday
5. WORKING TITLE: "Things My Mother Does"
6. KEY FEATURES: free verse, present tense, one specific kitchen moment per poem, no abstract emotional language, a recurring small image (light through the kitchen window) appearing in three poems

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is the difference between a project CONCEPT and a project PLAN?
options: A concept and a plan are the same thing in different words.

A concept describes WHAT will work and WHY (the six elements). A plan describes HOW the work will be made step by step (the structure, sequence, word counts, and details that allow drafting to begin tomorrow).
A concept is for younger pupils; a plan is for older pupils.
A concept must be one page; a plan must be ten pages.
question_json.correct: A concept describes WHAT will work and WHY (the six elements). A plan describes HOW the work will be made step by step (the structure, sequence, word counts, and details that allow drafting to begin tomorrow).

feedback.correct: Correct! A concept describes WHAT and WHY. A plan describes HOW step by step. The plan takes the concept's six elements and turns them into a structure detailed enough that drafting can begin tomorrow without needing further decisions about scope, sequence, or content.
feedback.wrong: A concept answers WHAT and WHY (theme, audience, purpose, scope, title, key features). A plan answers HOW step by step (specific structure, sequence, word counts, content per section). Both are needed - the concept tells you what you are making; the plan tells you what to write first.

answer_json.correct: A concept describes WHAT will work and WHY (the six elements). A plan describes HOW the work will be made step by step (the structure, sequence, word counts, and details that allow drafting to begin tomorrow).

question_json.question:
Choose the correct next step.

A pupil shows you their L64 concept document and says "I am ready to start drafting." What should they do FIRST?
options: Begin drafting the opening sentence immediately.

Build a complete plan using their pathway-specific planning tool. The concept tells them what to make; the plan tells them what to write first, second, third. Drafting without a plan produces stalled drafts and abandoned projects.
Start by typing the title in fancy fonts.
Email their teacher for permission to begin.
question_json.correct: Build a complete plan using their pathway-specific planning tool. The concept tells them what to make; the plan tells them what to write first, second, third. Drafting without a plan produces stalled drafts and abandoned projects.

feedback.correct: Correct! The plan must come BEFORE drafting. Pupils who skip planning often write a strong opening, then stall when they reach the middle of the project because they have not decided what comes next. A plan tells them, paragraph by paragraph or poem by poem or section by section, what to write and in what order.
feedback.wrong: The pupil must build a complete plan using their pathway-specific planning tool BEFORE drafting begins. Concept clarity is not the same as plan clarity. The concept says "I am making a magazine of three articles about local volunteers." The plan says "Article 1 will be 350 words about the lollipop lady, opening with the Tuesday-morning detail, including ONE direct quote from a parent..."

answer_json.correct: Build a complete plan using their pathway-specific planning tool. The concept tells them what to make; the plan tells them what to write first, second, third. Drafting without a plan produces stalled drafts and abandoned projects.

question_json.question:
Click a pathway on the left, then its planning tool on the right.

Match each pathway to its appropriate planning tool.
question_json.instruction: Click a pathway on the left, then its planning tool on the right.

pairs.right: Connect Grid (5-part: Opening, Build-up, Problem, Resolution, Ending)
Themed Sequence Map (poem-by-poem grid with form, length, theme connection)
Editorial Calendar (article angles, word counts, sources, deadlines)
AMC Grid (Audience-Message-Channel for each campaign element)
Connect Grid (5-part: Opening, Build-up, Problem, Resolution, Ending)
Themed Sequence Map (poem-by-poem grid with form, length, theme connection)
Editorial Calendar (article angles, word counts, sources, deadlines)
AMC Grid (Audience-Message-Channel for each campaign element)

question_json.question:
Identify the biggest weakness.

A pupil planning a Pathway A short story has a Connect Grid with these entries:

OPENING: A girl arrives at a new school.
BUILD-UP: She meets people.
PROBLEM: (left blank)
RESOLUTION: Things get better.
ENDING: She is happy.

What is the BIGGEST problem with this plan?
options: The PROBLEM section is empty - and without a clear problem, the story has nothing for the protagonist to face. The other sections are also too vague to draft from. A plan must be specific enough that someone else could begin writing tomorrow.
The pupil should have used a different plot.
The pupil chose the wrong age for their protagonist.
question_json.correct: The PROBLEM section is empty - and without a clear problem, the story has nothing for the protagonist to face. The other sections are also too vague to draft from. A plan must be specific enough that someone else could begin writing tomorrow.

feedback.correct: Correct! The empty Problem section is the biggest issue - the central element of any story is missing. The other sections are also too vague ("she meets people" / "things get better"). A plan must be specific. A reader of the plan should know exactly what happens, in what order, with which characters. This plan tells us almost nothing.
feedback.wrong: The empty Problem section is the biggest issue. Without a problem, there is no story. The other sections are also too vague to draft from. The diagnostic test for any plan: could a stranger read it and begin drafting tomorrow? If not, the plan needs more detail.

answer_json.correct: The PROBLEM section is empty - and without a clear problem, the story has nothing for the protagonist to face. The other sections are also too vague to draft from. A plan must be specific enough that someone else could begin writing tomorrow.

question_json.question:
Identify the weakest planning element.

A pupil planning a Pathway D campaign has an AMC Grid for THREE campaign pieces:

Piece 1 (Poster): Audience = Year 5-6 pupils. Message = "Reduce plastic". Channel = School noticeboard.
Piece 2 (Speech): Audience = Year 5-6 pupils. Message = "Reduce plastic". Channel = Year assembly.
Piece 3 (Letter): Audience = Year 5-6 pupils. Message = "Reduce plastic". Channel = Year newsletter.

Which element of this plan is WEAKEST?
options: The campaign should have more pieces.

All three pieces have IDENTICAL audience and IDENTICAL message. Each campaign piece should do DIFFERENT work - reaching slightly different audiences with slightly different angles, even if the goal is the same. As planned, the three pieces would feel repetitive rather than reinforcing.
The campaign should not include a letter.
The pupil chose the wrong cause.
question_json.correct: All three pieces have IDENTICAL audience and IDENTICAL message. Each campaign piece should do DIFFERENT work - reaching slightly different audiences with slightly different angles, even if the goal is the same. As planned, the three pieces would feel repetitive rather than reinforcing.

feedback.correct: Correct! Each piece in a campaign should do different work. The poster catches attention quickly. The speech creates emotional commitment. The letter persuades a specific decision-maker. As planned (identical audience and message for all three pieces), the campaign is repetitive. A revised plan: Poster catches attention with one statistic; Speech to the year group makes emotional appeal; Letter to the catering manager makes a specific request with evidence. Now each piece does different work.
feedback.wrong: Each campaign piece should do different work - different audiences, different angles, different channels. As planned, the three pieces are identical except for format, which means the campaign is repetitive rather than reinforcing. A strong campaign plan distinguishes what each piece uniquely does.

answer_json.correct: All three pieces have IDENTICAL audience and IDENTICAL message. Each campaign piece should do DIFFERENT work - reaching slightly different audiences with slightly different angles, even if the goal is the same. As planned, the three pieces would feel repetitive rather than reinforcing.

question_json.question:
Identify the missing planning question.

A pupil planning a Pathway B poetry collection has a Themed Sequence Map with 7 poems. Looking at the sequence column:

Poem 1: free verse, kitchen action 1
Poem 2: free verse, kitchen action 2
Poem 3: free verse, kitchen action 3
Poem 4: free verse, kitchen action 4
Poem 5: free verse, kitchen action 5
Poem 6: free verse, kitchen action 6
Poem 7: free verse, kitchen action 7

What is one important question this plan does NOT yet answer?
options: What the SEQUENCE LOGIC is - WHY are the poems in this particular order? Are they ordered chronologically (morning to night)? By emotional intensity (calmest to most charged)? By theme depth (simplest action to most surprising)? A sequence without sequencing logic is just a list.
Whether the pupil should write a different number of poems.
Whether the poems should be about a different subject.
question_json.correct: What the SEQUENCE LOGIC is - WHY are the poems in this particular order? Are they ordered chronologically (morning to night)? By emotional intensity (calmest to most charged)? By theme depth (simplest action to most surprising)? A sequence without sequencing logic is just a list.

feedback.correct: Correct! Sequence is not the same as order. The plan lists 7 poems, but it does not say WHY they are in this order. A reader moving through a collection has an experience that depends on sequencing logic - the same poems in a different order would create a different effect. The plan should answer: what is the principle that orders these poems? Time? Emotional intensity? Surprise? Without that principle, the collection is a list.
feedback.wrong: The plan does not name the sequencing logic - the principle that determines the order. A poetry collection is more than a list of poems; the order creates an experience. The plan should answer: WHY this poem first? Why this one last? What principle organises the sequence?

answer_json.correct: What the SEQUENCE LOGIC is - WHY are the poems in this particular order? Are they ordered chronologically (morning to night)? By emotional intensity (calmest to most charged)? By theme depth (simplest action to most surprising)? A sequence without sequencing logic is just a list.

question_json.question:
Click a tool on the left, then its essential question.

Match each planning tool to ONE essential question it must answer.
question_json.instruction: Click a tool on the left, then its essential question.

pairs.right: What is the central PROBLEM, and where in the structure does it appear?
Who am I interviewing, when, and what is the angle of each article?
What does each piece UNIQUELY do, and through which channel does it reach its audience?
What does my reader NEED to know first, second, third?
What is the central PROBLEM, and where in the structure does it appear?
Who am I interviewing, when, and what is the angle of each article?
What does each piece UNIQUELY do, and through which channel does it reach its audience?
What does my reader NEED to know first, second, third?

question_json.question:
Identify the biggest planning issue.

A pupil planning a Pathway E information book on "Your First Pet" has this Outline:

1. Choosing your pet (1000 words)
2. Costs and equipment (200 words)
3. Daily care (150 words)
4. What could go wrong (100 words)

Total: 1450 words across 4 sections.

Which is the BIGGEST issue with this plan?
options: The WORD COUNT DISTRIBUTION is wrong. "Choosing your pet" gets 1000 words but "What could go wrong" - which the model deconstruction in L64 identified as essential - gets only 100. The proportions do not match the importance of each section. A balanced plan would distribute words according to what the reader most needs.
The pet topic is not interesting enough.
The book should be shorter overall.
question_json.correct: The WORD COUNT DISTRIBUTION is wrong. "Choosing your pet" gets 1000 words but "What could go wrong" - which the model deconstruction in L64 identified as essential - gets only 100. The proportions do not match the importance of each section. A balanced plan would distribute words according to what the reader most needs.

feedback.correct: Correct! Word count distribution is a planning decision, not just a length choice. If the audience research told the pupil that pupils most need help with "what could go wrong", that section deserves substantial space - perhaps 400 words, not 100. A balanced plan: Choosing 400, Costs 350, Daily care 350, What could go wrong 350. Now the proportions match the importance.
feedback.wrong: Word count distribution should match what the reader most needs. As planned, the proportions are unbalanced - the most reader-needed section has the fewest words. A planning revision: rebalance the word counts to match the audience research findings about what pupils actually need.

answer_json.correct: The WORD COUNT DISTRIBUTION is wrong. "Choosing your pet" gets 1000 words but "What could go wrong" - which the model deconstruction in L64 identified as essential - gets only 100. The proportions do not match the importance of each section. A balanced plan would distribute words according to what the reader most needs.

question_json.question:
Choose the most draft-ready element.

Which of these plan elements is MOST DETAILED enough that drafting could begin tomorrow?
options: OPENING (2 sentences, picture book page 1): "On the first day of school, Granny was very small. She was so small that her shoes had laces and her bag had her name on it." Illustration: Granny in 1962 school uniform, shoes prominent, looking up at a doorway. Colour: warm yellow.
question_json.correct: OPENING (2 sentences, picture book page 1): "On the first day of school, Granny was very small. She was so small that her shoes had laces and her bag had her name on it." Illustration: Granny in 1962 school uniform, shoes prominent, looking up at a doorway. Colour: warm yellow.

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B is draft-ready: it specifies the sentence count, the actual words for the opening, the illustration content, and the colour palette. A stranger could begin drafting page 1 tomorrow with this level of detail. The other options are vague labels that would still require all the planning decisions to be made before drafting begins.
feedback.wrong: Option B is draft-ready. It specifies sentences, words, illustration, and colour. The other options are labels, not plans. A draft-ready plan element answers: how many sentences? What words? What images? What style? Once these are answered, drafting becomes execution rather than further planning.

answer_json.correct: OPENING (2 sentences, picture book page 1): "On the first day of school, Granny was very small. She was so small that her shoes had laces and her bag had her name on it." Illustration: Granny in 1962 school uniform, shoes prominent, looking up at a doorway. Colour: warm yellow.

question_json.question:
Identify the three missing elements.

A pupil planning a Pathway C journalism project has this Editorial Calendar:

Article 1: 400 words about the lollipop lady. Source: ?
Article 2: 400 words about the library volunteer. Source: ?
Article 3: 400 words about the football coach. Source: ?

Diagnose the THREE missing planning elements.
options: (1) Sources are not named or confirmed - "?" means the pupil has not approached the people. (2) No interview dates or arrangements - sources need contact and consent. (3) No angle for each article - WHAT specifically about each volunteer? Their backstory? A specific moment? The community impact? Three sources × three angles = nine planning decisions still to make.
The pupil should choose different volunteers.
question_json.correct: (1) Sources are not named or confirmed - "?" means the pupil has not approached the people. (2) No interview dates or arrangements - sources need contact and consent. (3) No angle for each article - WHAT specifically about each volunteer? Their backstory? A specific moment? The community impact? Three sources × three angles = nine planning decisions still to make.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three missing elements: confirmed sources, interview dates, and specific angles. "Source: ?" means the pupil has not yet approached the people - so the article cannot be drafted because the interview has not happened. The plan needs to become an actual schedule: contact source X by date Y, conduct interview by date Z, then draft begins.
feedback.wrong: Three missing elements: source confirmation (have they been approached and consented?), interview dates (when will the conversation happen?), and specific angle for each article (WHAT about this person?). Without these, the plan is aspiration not action. Journalism plans must be operational schedules.

answer_json.correct: (1) Sources are not named or confirmed - "?" means the pupil has not approached the people. (2) No interview dates or arrangements - sources need contact and consent. (3) No angle for each article - WHAT specifically about each volunteer? Their backstory? A specific moment? The community impact? Three sources × three angles = nine planning decisions still to make.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this principle of strong project planning.
question_json.template: "A plan is strong enough to begin drafting when a___could read it and start writing tomorrow without needing to ask further questions about structure, content, or sequence."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Stranger" - the diagnostic test for plan readiness. If a stranger could read your plan and start drafting tomorrow, your plan is ready. If they would need to ask you questions about what you actually meant, the plan needs more specificity. This is sometimes called the "stranger test" - it removes the temptation to keep planning details only in your head.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this sequencing principle.
question_json.template: "In a strong plan, each element should___naturally from the previous one - giving the reader a journey, not a list of disconnected parts."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Flow" (or follow, lead, connect, build) - the principle of sequencing logic. A reader moving through your finished project should feel guided from one element to the next, with each part building on or responding to what came before. A list of items in any order is not a plan - it is just a collection.

question_json.question:
Diagnose the issue.

A pupil tells you: "My plan is finished. I have written the working titles of all six poems in my collection." 

What is the issue?
options: Working titles alone are NOT a plan. The plan needs: sequencing logic (why this order?), form for each poem (free verse / haiku / rhyming?), length for each poem (number of lines), the specific subject moment for each poem, the recurring image plan, and the relationship of each poem to the overall theme. Working titles are just labels - the plan must specify what is actually inside each poem.
The pupil should choose different working titles.
Six poems is the right number but in wrong order.
question_json.correct: Working titles alone are NOT a plan. The plan needs: sequencing logic (why this order?), form for each poem (free verse / haiku / rhyming?), length for each poem (number of lines), the specific subject moment for each poem, the recurring image plan, and the relationship of each poem to the overall theme. Working titles are just labels - the plan must specify what is actually inside each poem.

feedback.correct: Correct! Working titles are labels, not plans. A complete poetry plan includes: form per poem, length per poem, specific subject moment per poem, the sequence logic, and the recurring image plan. Without these, the pupil has six working titles and no actual plan to draft from. The Themed Sequence Map provides all these planning fields.
feedback.wrong: Working titles are labels, not plans. A complete poetry plan answers: form for each? length for each? specific moment for each? sequence logic? recurring image use? Once these are answered for each poem, drafting can begin. Without these, six titles are just six labels.

answer_json.correct: Working titles alone are NOT a plan. The plan needs: sequencing logic (why this order?), form for each poem (free verse / haiku / rhyming?), length for each poem (number of lines), the specific subject moment for each poem, the recurring image plan, and the relationship of each poem to the overall theme. Working titles are just labels - the plan must specify what is actually inside each poem.

question_json.question:
Choose the strongest plan distribution.

A pupil planning a Pathway A picture book has 8 pages. Their concept Key Features include "the same character on every page, one surprise per page, warm colours for safe scenes, cool blues for moments of worry."

Which Connect Grid distribution best uses these key features?
options: Opening: page 1. Build-up: pages 2-7. Problem: pages 7-8. Resolution: (none). Ending: (none). Just write the whole story without resolution.

Opening: page 1 (introduce Granny, warm colours). Build-up: pages 2-3 (school items, surprises). Problem: pages 4-5 (Granny's worry, cool blue colours). Resolution: pages 6-7 (something happens, colours warm again). Ending: page 8 (Granny smiles, full warm colours, returning surprise from page 1).
All 8 pages should be the same scene shown from different angles.
Pages 1-4 should be Granny worrying, pages 5-8 should be a different character.
question_json.correct: Opening: page 1 (introduce Granny, warm colours). Build-up: pages 2-3 (school items, surprises). Problem: pages 4-5 (Granny's worry, cool blue colours). Resolution: pages 6-7 (something happens, colours warm again). Ending: page 8 (Granny smiles, full warm colours, returning surprise from page 1).

feedback.correct: Correct! Option B distributes the 8 pages across the 5 Connect Grid sections AND uses the concept Key Features deliberately - the warm/cool colour scheme matches the emotional shift; the surprise per page is built in; the same character appears throughout; and the ending returns to the opening detail (creating a circular structure). This is what plan + concept integration looks like.
feedback.wrong: Option B distributes the pages across the 5 Connect Grid sections and uses each Key Feature deliberately. The warm/cool colour scheme tracks the emotional arc. The surprises happen progressively. The ending returns to the opening detail. A plan should USE the concept's Key Features, not ignore them.

answer_json.correct: Opening: page 1 (introduce Granny, warm colours). Build-up: pages 2-3 (school items, surprises). Problem: pages 4-5 (Granny's worry, cool blue colours). Resolution: pages 6-7 (something happens, colours warm again). Ending: page 8 (Granny smiles, full warm colours, returning surprise from page 1).

question_json.question:
Identify the strongest planning aspect.

A pupil planning a Pathway C journalism project on "Three Quiet Heroes" has this revised Editorial Calendar:

Week 1: Contact lollipop lady, library helper, football coach. Confirm interview times.
Week 2: Conduct three interviews. Take photographs.
Week 3: Draft three articles (350 words each, common structure: personal-moment hook, three biography paragraphs, ONE direct quote, returning detail at close).
Week 4: Edit, lay out, share with subjects for accuracy check.

Which aspect of this plan is STRONGEST?
options: The plan is OPERATIONAL, not just descriptive. It has dates (week 1, 2, 3, 4), specific actions (contact, interview, draft, edit, accuracy-check), and a common article structure (so all three articles have the same backbone). A stranger could begin executing this plan tomorrow.
The plan should include more articles.
The plan needs more famous subjects.
question_json.correct: The plan is OPERATIONAL, not just descriptive. It has dates (week 1, 2, 3, 4), specific actions (contact, interview, draft, edit, accuracy-check), and a common article structure (so all three articles have the same backbone). A stranger could begin executing this plan tomorrow.

feedback.correct: Correct! The plan is operational - it has dates, specific actions, and a structure for each article. This is what journalism plans should look like. Notice the "accuracy check with subjects" - real journalism includes letting subjects review what is written about them. The plan honours the real-world model and is genuinely actionable.
feedback.wrong: The plan is operational rather than just descriptive. Dates are named, actions are specified, the article structure is defined. The accuracy-check step honours the real-world model. Journalism plans are schedules, not just lists.

answer_json.correct: The plan is OPERATIONAL, not just descriptive. It has dates (week 1, 2, 3, 4), specific actions (contact, interview, draft, edit, accuracy-check), and a common article structure (so all three articles have the same backbone). A stranger could begin executing this plan tomorrow.

question_json.question:
Choose the most useful three prescriptions.

PLAN DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil planning a Pathway D campaign has this AMC Grid:

Piece 1 (Poster): Audience = pupils. Message = "use less plastic". Channel = somewhere in school.
Piece 2 (Speech): Audience = pupils. Message = "use less plastic". Channel = some assembly.

What THREE prescriptions will MOST strengthen this plan?
options: Add more pieces to the campaign.

Three prescriptions: (1) Specify which pupils for each piece - "Year 5-6 pupils at break time" vs "all pupils at year assembly" - because audience precision changes message. (2) Differentiate the messages - poster could be "ONE statistic + ONE action" while speech could be "personal story + emotional appeal". (3) Specify channels - which noticeboard? which assembly? when? Each piece should now do different work.
Choose a different campaign topic instead of plastic.
question_json.correct: Three prescriptions: (1) Specify which pupils for each piece - "Year 5-6 pupils at break time" vs "all pupils at year assembly" - because audience precision changes message. (2) Differentiate the messages - poster could be "ONE statistic + ONE action" while speech could be "personal story + emotional appeal". (3) Specify channels - which noticeboard? which assembly? when? Each piece should now do different work.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three prescriptions address the three weaknesses: vague audience (specify which pupils), repeated message (differentiate so each piece does unique work), unclear channel (specify which noticeboard, which assembly, when). After the prescriptions, each piece has a different audience-message-channel combination - making the campaign reinforcing rather than repetitive.
feedback.wrong: Three prescriptions: specify audience for each piece (so they differ), differentiate messages (so each piece does unique work), specify channels (so deployment is operational). Each AMC row should be different from the others, otherwise the campaign just repeats itself in different formats.

answer_json.correct: Three prescriptions: (1) Specify which pupils for each piece - "Year 5-6 pupils at break time" vs "all pupils at year assembly" - because audience precision changes message. (2) Differentiate the messages - poster could be "ONE statistic + ONE action" while speech could be "personal story + emotional appeal". (3) Specify channels - which noticeboard? which assembly? when? Each piece should now do different work.

question_json.question:
Choose the best prescription.

PLAN DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil planning a Pathway A short story has this Connect Grid:

OPENING: A girl finds something.
BUILD-UP: She tells her friends.
PROBLEM: There is a problem.
RESOLUTION: They solve it.
ENDING: They are happy.

What is the BEST prescription?
options: Tell the pupil to choose a different plot.

The plan is a series of vague labels rather than a specific story. Walk through one section at a time: WHAT does she find? Where? When? WHO are her friends, by name? WHAT specifically is the problem? WHAT specific action solves it? Each label needs to become a specific scene with specific actions, characters, and detail. The work is not to write a different story but to plan THIS story properly.
Tell the pupil their story is too short.
Tell the pupil to add more characters.
question_json.correct: The plan is a series of vague labels rather than a specific story. Walk through one section at a time: WHAT does she find? Where? When? WHO are her friends, by name? WHAT specifically is the problem? WHAT specific action solves it? Each label needs to become a specific scene with specific actions, characters, and detail. The work is not to write a different story but to plan THIS story properly.

feedback.correct: Correct! The plan needs each label converted into a specific scene. "A girl finds something" → "Maya, walking home from school, notices a small wooden box wedged under the railway bridge wall." Now we have a who, where, what, and when. Each Connect Grid section needs the same conversion. The pupil is not making the wrong story - they have not yet planned the story they want to make.
feedback.wrong: The plan is vague labels. The fix is to convert each label into a specific scene with named characters, specific objects, specific places, and specific actions. The pupil is not failing to invent a story - they are failing to plan the story they have already chosen. Specificity is the cure.

answer_json.correct: The plan is a series of vague labels rather than a specific story. Walk through one section at a time: WHAT does she find? Where? When? WHO are her friends, by name? WHAT specifically is the problem? WHAT specific action solves it? Each label needs to become a specific scene with specific actions, characters, and detail. The work is not to write a different story but to plan THIS story properly.

In your PWP Booklet, complete YOUR full pathway-specific plan using the appropriate planning tool from your worksheet.

STORY: Connect Grid (5 sections, each specific)
POETRY: Themed Sequence Map (each poem with form, length, subject, sequence position)
JOURNALISM: Editorial Calendar (each article with subject, source, angle, deadline)

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

See the worked examples on Slides 7-11 of the lesson presentation for your specific pathway.

question_json.modelAnswer:
Example complete Story plan for "When Granny Was Small" (8 pages):

OPENING (page 1): "On the first day of school, Granny was very small. She was so small that her shoes had laces and her bag had her name on it." Illustration: Granny in 1962 uniform looking up at a doorway. Warm yellow palette.

BUILD-UP (pages 2-3): Page 2: Granny meets the school dinner - "She had never seen so much food on one plate." Page 3: Granny meets the bell - "It was loud enough to make her dog jump if her dog had been there." One surprise per page. Warm colours continue.

PROBLEM (pages 4-5): Page 4: The other children went to a classroom Granny did not know. Page 5: Granny stood alone in the corridor, holding her bag. Cool blue palette enters.

RESOLUTION (pages 6-7): Page 6: A teacher named Miss Ada noticed her. Page 7: Miss Ada walked Granny to the right classroom, talking about Granny's bag. Colours warm gradually.

ENDING (page 8): "By home time, Granny had laces, a name on her bag, and a teacher who knew her." Returning detail (laces and name from page 1). Full warm yellow palette restored.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

What is the difference between PLANNING (L65) and DRAFTING (L66)?
options: PLANNING decides what will go where (the blueprint). DRAFTING produces the actual words on the page (the building). The plan tells you WHAT to write next; drafting is when you actually write it. Strong drafting follows from strong planning - the plan keeps you moving forward when drafting gets hard.
Drafting is for fast writing; planning is for slow writing.
Drafting is harder than planning so it comes second.
question_json.correct: PLANNING decides what will go where (the blueprint). DRAFTING produces the actual words on the page (the building). The plan tells you WHAT to write next; drafting is when you actually write it. Strong drafting follows from strong planning - the plan keeps you moving forward when drafting gets hard.

feedback.correct: Correct! Planning produces the structure; drafting produces the words. Throughout L66, your L65 plan is your guide. When you do not know what to write next, the plan tells you. When you finish a section, the plan tells you what comes next. The plan keeps you moving forward.
feedback.wrong: Planning decides WHAT will go where. Drafting produces the actual words. The L65 plan is your guide throughout L66 - it tells you what to write next when you are unsure.

answer_json.correct: PLANNING decides what will go where (the blueprint). DRAFTING produces the actual words on the page (the building). The plan tells you WHAT to write next; drafting is when you actually write it. Strong drafting follows from strong planning - the plan keeps you moving forward when drafting gets hard.

question_json.question:
Choose the correct answer.

L66 runs across MULTIPLE sessions (typically 4). Why is drafting structured this way instead of in one long session?
options: Drafting requires SUSTAINED effort that one session cannot deliver. A complete first draft typically needs several hours of work. Multi-session structure also lets the writer step back between sessions, which improves the writing - returning to a draft after a day brings fresh eyes.
Because the school timetable demands it.
Because drafting is boring and needs to be split up.
question_json.correct: Drafting requires SUSTAINED effort that one session cannot deliver. A complete first draft typically needs several hours of work. Multi-session structure also lets the writer step back between sessions, which improves the writing - returning to a draft after a day brings fresh eyes.

feedback.correct: Correct! Drafting needs sustained effort - typically several hours total - and multi-session structure also gives writers fresh eyes between sessions. You can return to a draft on day 2 and notice things you missed on day 1. Across 4 sessions, you build the draft in stages, each session focused on different parts of the project.
feedback.wrong: Drafting needs sustained effort - more than one session can provide. Multi-session structure also gives the writer fresh eyes between sessions. Returning to your draft after a day will let you notice things you missed.

answer_json.correct: Drafting requires SUSTAINED effort that one session cannot deliver. A complete first draft typically needs several hours of work. Multi-session structure also lets the writer step back between sessions, which improves the writing - returning to a draft after a day brings fresh eyes.

question_json.question:
Click a session on the left, then its focus.

Match each L66 SESSION to its typical FOCUS.
question_json.instruction: Click a session on the left, then its focus.

pairs.right: Drafting begins - openings + early build-up sections
Drafting continues - middle sections of the project
Drafting continues - approaching the ending; some pupils nearing complete draft
Self-editing pass + completing remaining sections; first draft complete
Drafting begins - openings + early build-up sections
Drafting continues - middle sections of the project
Drafting continues - approaching the ending; some pupils nearing complete draft
Self-editing pass + completing remaining sections; first draft complete

question_json.question:
Identify the issue.

A pupil opens their drafting session by sitting down with no plan visible. They have no draft from last session in front of them. They start writing whatever comes to mind. 

What is wrong with this approach?
options: WITHOUT THE PLAN AND PREVIOUS DRAFT VISIBLE, the pupil has no continuity. They will write something disconnected from their project, restart sections they already drafted, or stall after the first paragraph. Strong drafting begins by getting plan + previous draft + Tutorial Cards out and reading the last paragraph aloud to re-enter the project's voice.
They should be using a computer.
question_json.correct: WITHOUT THE PLAN AND PREVIOUS DRAFT VISIBLE, the pupil has no continuity. They will write something disconnected from their project, restart sections they already drafted, or stall after the first paragraph. Strong drafting begins by getting plan + previous draft + Tutorial Cards out and reading the last paragraph aloud to re-enter the project's voice.

feedback.correct: Correct! The Self-Direction Guide gives a 4-step routine for opening every session: (1) Get the plan out. (2) Find where you left off (read last paragraph aloud quietly). (3) Check the plan for what comes next. (4) Draft forwards. Skipping step 1 produces drift; skipping step 2 produces voice changes between sessions; skipping step 3 produces stalls.
feedback.wrong: Strong drafting needs the plan visible (the guide) and the previous draft visible (continuity). Without these, the pupil has no anchor and will drift, restart, or stall. The Self-Direction Guide gives the 4-step routine: plan out, last paragraph reread, check what comes next, draft forwards.

answer_json.correct: WITHOUT THE PLAN AND PREVIOUS DRAFT VISIBLE, the pupil has no continuity. They will write something disconnected from their project, restart sections they already drafted, or stall after the first paragraph. Strong drafting begins by getting plan + previous draft + Tutorial Cards out and reading the last paragraph aloud to re-enter the project's voice.

question_json.question:
Identify the pattern.

A pupil writes 3 sentences and then says "this is rubbish" and starts over. They write 2 sentences and say "this is rubbish" and start over again. After 20 minutes they have one paragraph that they are about to delete. 

What is happening?
options: PERFECTIONISM IS BLOCKING DRAFTING. The pupil is editing while drafting - judging each sentence as soon as they write it. This prevents forward momentum. The fix from Card 6 / Mini-Lesson 4: draft forwards before fixing. Save what you have written; do not delete; move to the next section. Editing comes after drafting, not during.
question_json.correct: PERFECTIONISM IS BLOCKING DRAFTING. The pupil is editing while drafting - judging each sentence as soon as they write it. This prevents forward momentum. The fix from Card 6 / Mini-Lesson 4: draft forwards before fixing. Save what you have written; do not delete; move to the next section. Editing comes after drafting, not during.

feedback.correct: Correct! This pattern is one of the four named stalls (Stall 3: "I want to start over"). The fix is the Golden Rule: do not start over. Save what you have written. Open the next section and draft forwards from there. You can revise later. The pupil who keeps starting over produces nothing; the pupil who drafts forwards produces a complete (if imperfect) first draft.
feedback.wrong: This is Stall 3 (I want to start over) - perfectionism blocking drafting. The fix: do not delete. Save what you have written. Move to the next section and draft forwards. Editing comes after drafting, not during. Forward momentum matters more than perfection.

The fix from Card 6 / Mini-Lesson 4: draft forwards before fixing. Save what you have written; do not delete; move to the next section. Editing comes after drafting, not during.

Run a brief SELF-REVISION pass on what they just wrote. Card 7 outlines four passes: Specificity / Flow / Voice / Purpose. Even a 2-minute specificity pass (replacing 3 general words with specific ones) measurably improves the writing. Then mark the section complete on the plan and move on.

question_json.question:

options: "Show me your plan. What does your plan say comes next?" If the plan answers the question, the pupil can begin. If the plan does not answer the question, the plan needs more detail - the conference becomes a brief plan-strengthening session. This routes the pupil to the resource (plan) that actually answers their question.
Tell them to take a break.
Tell them their project is too hard.

This builds independence: pupils learn to consult the plan first, not the teacher first. The Self-Direction Guide makes this explicit: "If the plan does not tell you, ask for a 3-minute conference."

The fix: specificity. Replace general words with specific details. One sensory detail per paragraph.

Type the missing word.

Complete the L66 drafting principle.
question_json.template: "When stuck, change the smallest___first. New sentence. New paragraph. New section. Do not restart the project."

question_json.question:

Complete the L66 forward-momentum principle.

question_json.feedback: Correct! "Perfection" - forward momentum matters more than perfection. Draft first; edit second. Switching back and forth slows both. The pupil who drafts a complete imperfect first draft has SOMETHING to revise. The pupil who keeps editing the first paragraph perfectly has nothing else to revise. Forward, every session.

question_json.question:
Diagnose the feedback issue.

A pupil shares their draft with a partner for peer feedback. The partner reads silently and then says: "It was really good. Well done." 

What is wrong with this feedback?
options: Nothing - the partner is being kind.

GENERIC PRAISE IS NOT USEFUL FEEDBACK. "Well done" tells the writer nothing they can act on. Card 8 (Peer Feedback) gives the structure: name ONE specific thing that worked, ask ONE genuine question, offer ONE precise suggestion. Specifics are what writers can use. "The detail about the kettle landed for me" is feedback. "It was good" is politeness.
The partner should say it was bad.
The partner should not give feedback.
question_json.correct: GENERIC PRAISE IS NOT USEFUL FEEDBACK. "Well done" tells the writer nothing they can act on. Card 8 (Peer Feedback) gives the structure: name ONE specific thing that worked, ask ONE genuine question, offer ONE precise suggestion. Specifics are what writers can use. "The detail about the kettle landed for me" is feedback. "It was good" is politeness.

feedback.correct: Correct! "Well done" cannot improve the writing. Card 8 (Peer Feedback) structures useful feedback: ONE specific strength named ("the detail about X landed for me"), ONE genuine question ("what does Maya do next?"), ONE precise suggestion. The questions reveal what the reader needs more of. The named strength shows the writer what is working. Specifics are what writers can use.
feedback.wrong: Generic praise is not useful feedback. Card 8 structures it: ONE specific strength named, ONE genuine question, ONE precise suggestion. "The detail about X landed for me" is feedback. "It was good" is politeness.

question_json.question:
Write the 4-step routine you will use at the START of each L66 drafting session, in your own words.

This is the routine that gets you back into your project quickly and prevents drift, restart, or stall.

Write your DRAFTING SESSION OPENING ROUTINE.

Example routine:

"At the start of each session, I will: (1) Get my L65 plan out and place it visibly on the desk. (2) Find where I left off last session and read the last paragraph aloud quietly to put me back in my project's voice. (3) Check my plan for what comes next - the next section, the next event, the next argument. (4) Begin drafting forward. I will not edit yet. I will not restart. I will draft forwards until I finish a section, then run a brief revision pass."
question_json.prompt:
Write your routine here. 4 steps minimum.

Step 1...
Step 2...
Step 3...
Step 4...
question_json.instruction:
Write the 4-step routine you will use at the START of each L66 drafting session, in your own words.

This is the routine that gets you back into your project quickly and prevents drift, restart, or stall.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong drafting session opening routine includes:

1. PLAN OUT - place your L65 plan visibly on your desk. The plan is your guide.

2. FIND LAST POINT - look at what you wrote last session. Read the last paragraph aloud quietly. This restores the voice.

3. CHECK PLAN - your plan tells you what comes next. Identify the specific section / event / argument / question you are about to draft.

4. DRAFT FORWARDS - begin writing. Do not edit. Do not restart. Get words on the page. When you finish a section, run a quick revision pass, then move to the next section.

This routine takes 2 minutes at session start and saves 20 minutes of drift, restart, or stall later in the session.

answer_json.modelAnswer:

2. FIND LAST POINT - look at what you wrote last session. Read the last paragraph aloud quietly. This restores the voice.

3. CHECK PLAN - your plan tells you what comes next. Identify the specific section / event / argument / question you are about to draft.

4. DRAFT FORWARDS - begin writing. Do not edit. Do not restart. Get words on the page. When you finish a section, run a quick revision pass, then move to the next section.

This routine takes 2 minutes at session start and saves 20 minutes of drift, restart, or stall later in the session.

question_json.question:
Choose the most useful three interventions.

DRAFTING DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

It is Session 2 of L66. A Pathway A (Story) pupil has drafted their opening, build-up, and most of their resolution. The Problem section is empty. They keep skipping past it because they "don't know what to write."

What are the THREE most useful interventions?
options: Tell them to delete the project.

Three interventions: (1) The Problem is the central element of a story - this gap explains why drafting feels disconnected. Address it first, not last. (2) Conference using Card 6 / Mini-Lesson 4 prompts: "What specifically goes wrong? Who does it happen to? Why does it matter to your protagonist?" Push for specificity. (3) If the plan was vague on the Problem (likely), spend 5 minutes strengthening the plan first - then draft from the strengthened plan.
Tell them to skip the Problem section permanently.
Tell them to copy a problem from another story.
question_json.correct: Three interventions: (1) The Problem is the central element of a story - this gap explains why drafting feels disconnected. Address it first, not last. (2) Conference using Card 6 / Mini-Lesson 4 prompts: "What specifically goes wrong? Who does it happen to? Why does it matter to your protagonist?" Push for specificity. (3) If the plan was vague on the Problem (likely), spend 5 minutes strengthening the plan first - then draft from the strengthened plan.

feedback.correct: Correct! The Pathway A drafting risk is exactly this - stalling at the Problem section. The interventions: (1) name why the Problem matters - it is the story's central element. (2) Use diagnostic questions to push for specificity. (3) Strengthen the plan if needed before drafting. The pupil cannot draft what the plan does not specify - so plan-strengthening is often the first move.
feedback.wrong: Three interventions: name why the Problem matters (central story element); use diagnostic questions for specificity (what / who / why); strengthen the plan first if needed. The pupil cannot draft what the plan does not specify.

answer_json.correct: Three interventions: (1) The Problem is the central element of a story - this gap explains why drafting feels disconnected. Address it first, not last. (2) Conference using Card 6 / Mini-Lesson 4 prompts: "What specifically goes wrong? Who does it happen to? Why does it matter to your protagonist?" Push for specificity. (3) If the plan was vague on the Problem (likely), spend 5 minutes strengthening the plan first - then draft from the strengthened plan.

question_json.question:
Choose the most important next step.

DRAFTING DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

It is Session 4 of L66 (final session). A pupil has a complete draft but has done no self-editing. They want to share their work tomorrow in L67. They are about to declare the draft "done."

What is the SINGLE most important next step?
options: Apply Mini-Lesson 5: run the four self-editing passes. Specificity (replace general words). Flow (read aloud, fix stumbles). Voice (cut anything borrowed). Purpose (does each section advance the project?). Even at 3 minutes per pass, the 12 minutes transform the draft. Pupils who skip self-editing share work that has avoidable weaknesses.
Tell them to add another whole section.
Tell them to start the project over.
question_json.correct: Apply Mini-Lesson 5: run the four self-editing passes. Specificity (replace general words). Flow (read aloud, fix stumbles). Voice (cut anything borrowed). Purpose (does each section advance the project?). Even at 3 minutes per pass, the 12 minutes transform the draft. Pupils who skip self-editing share work that has avoidable weaknesses.

feedback.correct: Correct! Mini-Lesson 5 (Self-Editing Pass) is exactly the Session 4 mini-lesson. The four passes (Specificity / Flow / Voice / Purpose) at 3 minutes each = 12 minutes. The transformation is significant - what felt like a finished draft becomes a stronger version. Pupils who skip self-editing share work with avoidable weaknesses; pupils who run the passes share work that does its job.
feedback.wrong: Apply Mini-Lesson 5: run the four self-editing passes. Specificity, Flow, Voice, Purpose. 3 minutes each. The 12 minutes transform the draft. Pupils who skip self-editing share avoidable weaknesses; pupils who run the passes share work that does its job.

answer_json.correct: Apply Mini-Lesson 5: run the four self-editing passes. Specificity (replace general words). Flow (read aloud, fix stumbles). Voice (cut anything borrowed). Purpose (does each section advance the project?). Even at 3 minutes per pass, the 12 minutes transform the draft. Pupils who skip self-editing share work that has avoidable weaknesses.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection across the 4 drafting sessions.

Four sentences minimum, six sentences maximum.

Cover: (1) which session was hardest and why, (2) which mini-lesson helped you most, (3) what you discovered about your own drafting process, (4) what you will carry into L67 (Celebrating).

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP) - End of L66 Reflection

Example reflection:

"Session 2 was the hardest because that is when my middle started to sag. I kept writing general statements like 'they were happy' instead of specific ones. Mini-Lesson 2 (Drafting the Middle) helped me most - the technique of replacing one general word with a specific detail per paragraph turned my writing around. What I discovered: I work best when I follow the 4-step opening routine - plan out, last paragraph reread, check plan, draft forwards. When I skipped step 2, my voice changed between sessions. What I carry into L67: my draft is ready for an audience because I ran self-editing in Session 4. I will share it confidently."
question_json.prompt:
Write your reflection here. Four to six sentences.

Which session was hardest:
Which mini-lesson helped most:
What I discovered about my drafting:
What I carry into L67:
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your reflection across the 4 drafting sessions.

Four sentences minimum, six sentences maximum.

Cover: (1) which session was hardest and why, (2) which mini-lesson helped you most, (3) what you discovered about your own drafting process, (4) what you will carry into L67 (Celebrating).
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong L66 reflection covers four specifics:

1. HARDEST SESSION - name which session and what made it hard (was it the middle sag in Session 2? a stall in Session 3? running out of time in Session 4?). Specific naming reveals what you learned about drafting under different conditions.

2. MOST HELPFUL MINI-LESSON - name which of the 5 mini-lessons gave you a technique you actually used. The mini-lesson you can name as helpful is one whose technique you have integrated into your drafting practice.

3. WHAT YOU DISCOVERED ABOUT YOUR DRAFTING - specific. "I work best when..." or "I struggle when..." Name a condition under which your drafting works well or poorly. This carries to every future drafting task.

4. WHAT YOU CARRY INTO L67 - your draft. State its readiness. Say one specific thing you will do in L67 (the final section, the celebration) that builds on what you completed in L66.

answer_json.modelAnswer:

2. MOST HELPFUL MINI-LESSON - name which of the 5 mini-lessons gave you a technique you actually used. The mini-lesson you can name as helpful is one whose technique you have integrated into your drafting practice.

3. WHAT YOU DISCOVERED ABOUT YOUR DRAFTING - specific. "I work best when..." or "I struggle when..." Name a condition under which your drafting works well or poorly. This carries to every future drafting task.

4. WHAT YOU CARRY INTO L67 - your draft. State its readiness. Say one specific thing you will do in L67 (the final section, the celebration) that builds on what you completed in L66.

question_json.question:
Choose the best answer.

Why does a writing project need an AUDIENCE in real life?
options: So pupils can be marked by the teacher.

A real audience completes the writing - writing only fully exists when it reaches readers. The audience experience tells the writer what their words actually do in the world. Without an audience, the project is finished as a draft but unfinished as communication.
So pupils can show off to their friends.
Because audiences give writers ratings and reviews.
question_json.correct: A real audience completes the writing - writing only fully exists when it reaches readers. The audience experience tells the writer what their words actually do in the world. Without an audience, the project is finished as a draft but unfinished as communication.

feedback.correct: Correct! A real audience completes the writing. Writing exists for someone to read - the writer's work is only fully realised when it reaches readers. Throughout Chapter 6, your concept named your audience, your plan tried to serve them, your draft tried to communicate with them. L67 is where they actually arrive.
feedback.wrong: Writing only fully exists when it reaches readers. The audience completes the writing. The whole point of Chapter 6 - choosing a project, developing the concept, planning, drafting - was to produce something that would actually reach a real reader. L67 is where that reader arrives.

answer_json.correct: A real audience completes the writing - writing only fully exists when it reaches readers. The audience experience tells the writer what their words actually do in the world. Without an audience, the project is finished as a draft but unfinished as communication.

question_json.question:
