What does L67 (Celebrating the End Results) ADD to the project work you have already done?
options: L67 teaches pupils new writing skills.

L67 takes the completed draft from L66 and brings it to a real audience - through reading aloud, displaying, or distributing. The audience experience is what L67 adds. Pupils have already done the writing work; L67 lets that work meet readers.
L67 starts a new project that builds on the L63-L66 project.
L67 is just a fun lesson with no real purpose.
question_json.correct: L67 takes the completed draft from L66 and brings it to a real audience - through reading aloud, displaying, or distributing. The audience experience is what L67 adds. Pupils have already done the writing work; L67 lets that work meet readers.

feedback.correct: Correct! L67 takes the completed work and brings it to readers. There is no new writing skill to learn - the writing work was done in L63-L66. What L67 adds is the audience moment. The lesson is structured around three celebration modes (reading aloud, displaying, distributing) and a final reflection on the whole journey.
feedback.wrong: L67 brings the completed work to a real audience. There is no new writing skill to learn in L67 - that work was done in L63-L66. The lesson is structured around how to share the work effectively and how to reflect on the whole project journey.

answer_json.correct: L67 takes the completed draft from L66 and brings it to a real audience - through reading aloud, displaying, or distributing. The audience experience is what L67 adds. Pupils have already done the writing work; L67 lets that work meet readers.

The book should have more pages.
question_json.correct: A REAL AUDIENCE. The book was made for the pupil's five-year-old cousin Ezra. A celebration must reach the actual intended audience - in this case, Ezra reading the book with the pupil's aunt. Saving a PDF for yourself is project storage; it is not celebration.

feedback.correct: Correct! Saving a copy is not celebrating. The book was MADE FOR Ezra - so the celebration is Ezra meeting the book. The pupil's actual celebration would be: print the book, deliver it to Ezra, watch Ezra read it with their aunt, capture what Ezra says about it. Audience reach is the first criterion of Form 6 (Audience Response) - has the work actually reached its intended reader?
feedback.wrong: A celebration must reach the intended audience. The book was made for Ezra, so Ezra is the audience. Saving a PDF is storage, not celebration. Form 6 Criterion 1 (Audience Reach) requires the work to actually arrive at its intended reader.

answer_json.correct: A REAL AUDIENCE. The book was made for the pupil's five-year-old cousin Ezra. A celebration must reach the actual intended audience - in this case, Ezra reading the book with the pupil's aunt. Saving a PDF for yourself is project storage; it is not celebration.

question_json.question:
Identify the issue.

A pupil completed a Pathway D campaign ("Less Plastic"). They want to display the poster in their bedroom at home, give the speech in the school hallway when no one is around, and put the letter in their school bag without sending it. 

What is wrong with this celebration?
options: NONE OF THESE PIECES REACH THEIR INTENDED AUDIENCE. The poster needs to be on the school noticeboard where Year 5-6 pupils see it at break. The speech needs to be delivered at year assembly. The letter needs to be sent to the catering manager. As planned, the campaign celebrations are private acts that do no campaigning work.
The pupil should choose a different cause.
Three pieces is too few for a campaign.
question_json.correct: NONE OF THESE PIECES REACH THEIR INTENDED AUDIENCE. The poster needs to be on the school noticeboard where Year 5-6 pupils see it at break. The speech needs to be delivered at year assembly. The letter needs to be sent to the catering manager. As planned, the campaign celebrations are private acts that do no campaigning work.

feedback.correct: Correct! The pupil planned the campaign carefully (audience-message-channel for each piece) but the celebration plan does not deploy any of them. The poster goes to a bedroom (wrong location). The speech happens with no audience (wrong situation). The letter sits in a bag (no recipient). The campaign cannot do its work without reaching its intended targets. Real celebration: poster on the noticeboard; speech at assembly; letter delivered to catering manager.
feedback.wrong: A campaign celebrates by being deployed - reaching its intended audiences through its intended channels. As planned, none of the three pieces reach their targets. A campaign in a bedroom is not a campaign; it is a paper. The celebration must match the deployment plan from L65 (the AMC Grid).

answer_json.correct: NONE OF THESE PIECES REACH THEIR INTENDED AUDIENCE. The poster needs to be on the school noticeboard where Year 5-6 pupils see it at break. The speech needs to be delivered at year assembly. The letter needs to be sent to the catering manager. As planned, the campaign celebrations are private acts that do no campaigning work.

question_json.question:
Identify the audience experience.

A pupil completed a Pathway B poetry collection ("Things My Mother Does"). They plan to read all 8 poems aloud to their class, very fast, with no pauses, looking at the floor, hoping to finish quickly. 

What will the audience experience?
options: A rushed, awkward delivery that prevents the poetry from working. Poems need PAUSES to land. The reader looking at the floor blocks audience connection. Reading too fast turns careful poems into a stream. The audience will hear words but not experience the poetry.
A clever and modern reading style.
A reading that helps shy listeners.
question_json.correct: A rushed, awkward delivery that prevents the poetry from working. Poems need PAUSES to land. The reader looking at the floor blocks audience connection. Reading too fast turns careful poems into a stream. The audience will hear words but not experience the poetry.

feedback.correct: Correct! Poetry depends on delivery. Pauses let images land. Eye contact (or at least looking up) creates the connection between reader and listener. Reading speed lets the listener hear the rhythm. The pupil has worked carefully on the poems for weeks - the celebration deserves matching care. A practice run before the main reading helps. Reading 1-2 favourite poems instead of all 8 may also work better - poetry rewards depth over breadth.
feedback.wrong: Poetry needs pauses to work. The audience needs time between poems to absorb each one. Looking at the floor blocks connection. Rushing the reading turns it into a list. The pupil should slow down, look up at least sometimes, and consider reading fewer poems with proper pauses rather than all 8 quickly.

answer_json.correct: A rushed, awkward delivery that prevents the poetry from working. Poems need PAUSES to land. The reader looking at the floor blocks audience connection. Reading too fast turns careful poems into a stream. The audience will hear words but not experience the poetry.

question_json.question:
Click a mode on the left, then an example.

Match each celebration MODE to ONE example of how it works in practice.
question_json.instruction: Click a mode on the left, then an example.

pairs.right: A pupil sits in front of younger children with their picture book open, turning pages and reading.
Campaign posters are mounted on the school noticeboard for two weeks where pupils see them daily.
Pupils give the magazine to school families on a designated day; people pick up copies to read at home.
A pupil delivers their speech at year assembly, using pauses, voice variation, and direct eye contact.
A pupil sits in front of younger children with their picture book open, turning pages and reading.
Campaign posters are mounted on the school noticeboard for two weeks where pupils see them daily.
Pupils give the magazine to school families on a designated day; people pick up copies to read at home.
A pupil delivers their speech at year assembly, using pauses, voice variation, and direct eye contact.

question_json.question:
Choose the principle.

A pupil reads their finished story aloud to the class. They look up between sentences. They pause at the end of each section. They speak loud enough that the back of the room can hear. 

Which audience-etiquette principle is the pupil applying?
options: They are SERVING THE AUDIENCE - reading at a pace and volume that lets the audience experience the work properly. Looking up creates connection. Pausing lets the audience absorb each section. Volume ensures everyone is included. The reader's job is to deliver the words so the audience can receive them.
They are showing they don't care about the audience.
question_json.correct: They are SERVING THE AUDIENCE - reading at a pace and volume that lets the audience experience the work properly. Looking up creates connection. Pausing lets the audience absorb each section. Volume ensures everyone is included. The reader's job is to deliver the words so the audience can receive them.

feedback.correct: Correct! Reading aloud well is a service to the audience. Pace, volume, eye contact, and pauses are all ways the reader makes it possible for the audience to receive the work. A talented writer who mumbles into their script with no eye contact prevents the audience from experiencing the writing. The reader's job is to deliver the words so the audience can receive them.
feedback.wrong: Reading aloud is a service to the audience. Pace, volume, eye contact, and pauses make it possible for the audience to receive the work. Without these, even excellent writing cannot reach the listener. Practice before the celebration helps - read your project aloud at home first.

answer_json.correct: They are SERVING THE AUDIENCE - reading at a pace and volume that lets the audience experience the work properly. Looking up creates connection. Pausing lets the audience absorb each section. Volume ensures everyone is included. The reader's job is to deliver the words so the audience can receive them.

Match each AUDIENCE BEHAVIOUR to whether it shows ENGAGEMENT or DISENGAGEMENT.

question_json.question:
Identify what the data tells them.

A pupil completed a Pathway E information guide ("Your First Pet"). They print 5 copies and put them in the school library. After two weeks, they ask the librarian which copies were borrowed. The librarian says all 5 copies are still on the shelf, untouched. 

What does this tell the pupil?
options: The guide is bad and should be thrown away.

The DELIVERY MODE did not reach the intended audience. The guide was made for pupils who are CONSIDERING getting their first pet - those pupils are not browsing the library section where the guide ended up. The pupil could try a different delivery: a flyer in the lunch queue, a copy in each classroom, distribution at a parents' evening. The work itself may be fine; the path to the reader was wrong.
Libraries are bad places for guides.
question_json.correct: The DELIVERY MODE did not reach the intended audience. The guide was made for pupils who are CONSIDERING getting their first pet - those pupils are not browsing the library section where the guide ended up. The pupil could try a different delivery: a flyer in the lunch queue, a copy in each classroom, distribution at a parents' evening. The work itself may be fine; the path to the reader was wrong.

feedback.correct: Correct! Five untouched copies tell us about the path to the reader, not the quality of the writing. Guides for pupils considering pets need to find pupils considering pets - not be quietly available in a library. Delivery matters as much as content. The pupil could try: a flyer with QR code in the lunch queue; a one-page summary in each classroom; presence at a parents' evening; an assembly mention. Same writing, different path to the reader.
feedback.wrong: Untouched copies tell you the delivery did not reach the audience. The writing may be fine - the PATH to the reader was wrong. Different delivery routes: classroom presence, parents' events, lunch queue distribution, assembly mention. Match the delivery to where the audience actually is.

answer_json.correct: The DELIVERY MODE did not reach the intended audience. The guide was made for pupils who are CONSIDERING getting their first pet - those pupils are not browsing the library section where the guide ended up. The pupil could try a different delivery: a flyer in the lunch queue, a copy in each classroom, distribution at a parents' evening. The work itself may be fine; the path to the reader was wrong.

question_json.question:
Choose the most useful response.

A pupil shared their finished work and was disappointed - the audience seemed bored. They want to give up on the project entirely. 

What is the BEST response?
options: Tell them to throw the project away and start over.

Help them analyse what happened. Was the audience the RIGHT audience for this work? Was the delivery format suited to the work? Was the work shared at a time when the audience could attend properly? Disappointment after sharing is data, not failure - it tells the pupil something specific about the celebration plan.
Tell them all writers fail and they should expect it.
Tell them their work was probably bad.
question_json.correct: Help them analyse what happened. Was the audience the RIGHT audience for this work? Was the delivery format suited to the work? Was the work shared at a time when the audience could attend properly? Disappointment after sharing is data, not failure - it tells the pupil something specific about the celebration plan.

feedback.correct: Correct! Disappointment after sharing is data, not failure. The pupil and teacher should investigate: WHO was the audience? Was that the audience the work was made for? HOW was it delivered? Did the format fit the work? WHEN was it shared? Did the audience have time and attention to receive it? Often the writing is fine and the celebration plan needs adjusting. The lesson the pupil learns - that audience matters, that delivery matters, that format matters - is exactly what the chapter was about.
feedback.wrong: Disappointment after sharing is data. The right response is investigation: was this the right audience? was the delivery suited? was the timing right? Often the writing is fine and the celebration plan needs adjusting. The lesson is precisely what the chapter teaches - audience, format, and delivery all matter.

answer_json.correct: Help them analyse what happened. Was the audience the RIGHT audience for this work? Was the delivery format suited to the work? Was the work shared at a time when the audience could attend properly? Disappointment after sharing is data, not failure - it tells the pupil something specific about the celebration plan.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this principle of celebration.
question_json.template: "A celebration is genuine when the work___its intended audience - through reading, display, distribution, or performance - and the audience has a chance to respond."
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Reaches" - the celebration only happens when the work meets its intended audience. Saving a PDF, having a teacher mark it, or showing it to your immediate family alone may all be valuable, but they are not the celebration the project was designed for. The celebration is genuine when the work reaches the audience the writer named in their concept.

question_json.question:
Type the missing word.

Complete this Chapter 6 reflection principle.
question_json.template: "Looking back across Chapter 6, the most important question to ask yourself is: what did I LEARN about myself as a___?"
question_json.feedback: Correct! "Writer" - the reflection at the end of Chapter 6 is not just about the project but about the pupil as a writer. What kinds of writing fit your strengths? Where did you struggle? What did you learn about your audience? The project was the vehicle; the lasting outcome is what you have learned about yourself as a writer.

question_json.question:
Diagnose the issue.

A pupil shared their work and the audience was warm and engaged. The pupil now says "I never want to write again because nothing will be as good as this." 

What is the BEST response?
options: Agree - they should stop while they are ahead.

Help them see this as evidence they CAN do this kind of work. The success was not luck. They followed a process (proposal, concept, plan, draft, celebration) and the process produced a piece of work that reached its audience. The same process will produce more good work. The fear of "never as good" is a common after-success feeling and is not a true description of what comes next.
Tell them the audience was just being polite.
Tell them they should focus on a different subject.
question_json.correct: Help them see this as evidence they CAN do this kind of work. The success was not luck. They followed a process (proposal, concept, plan, draft, celebration) and the process produced a piece of work that reached its audience. The same process will produce more good work. The fear of "never as good" is a common after-success feeling and is not a true description of what comes next.

feedback.correct: Correct! Success after a sustained project produces a complicated feeling. The pupil should see this as evidence of capability, not a peak they can never repeat. The process they followed (proposal, concept, plan, draft, celebration) is repeatable. The success was not random - it was the result of doing the work. Future projects will benefit from the same approach plus everything they have learned about themselves as a writer in this chapter.
feedback.wrong: Success at the end of a sustained project produces a fear that nothing will match it. The right response: the success was not luck. It came from following a process. The same process will produce more good work. The pupil now has experience of completing a meaningful project - that experience makes future projects more achievable, not less.

answer_json.correct: Help them see this as evidence they CAN do this kind of work. The success was not luck. They followed a process (proposal, concept, plan, draft, celebration) and the process produced a piece of work that reached its audience. The same process will produce more good work. The fear of "never as good" is a common after-success feeling and is not a true description of what comes next.

question_json.question:
Choose the strongest preparation plan.

A pupil is preparing to read aloud their picture book ("When Granny Was Small") to a class of Year 1 pupils. They have 6 minutes total. 

Which preparation plan is BEST?
options: Walk in. Read fast. Walk out.

BEFORE: practice reading the whole book aloud at home, holding the pages so listeners can see. Plan to introduce yourself and the book in 30 seconds. Plan to ask one question at the end ("Which page did you like most?"). DURING: read at a steady pace, look up between pages, show the illustrations clearly, leave 2 seconds between pages for absorption. AFTER: thank the class.
Read the book silently to the class while holding it up.
Skip the first page so it goes faster.
question_json.correct: BEFORE: practice reading the whole book aloud at home, holding the pages so listeners can see. Plan to introduce yourself and the book in 30 seconds. Plan to ask one question at the end ("Which page did you like most?"). DURING: read at a steady pace, look up between pages, show the illustrations clearly, leave 2 seconds between pages for absorption. AFTER: thank the class.

feedback.correct: Correct! A 6-minute reading aloud to Year 1 pupils needs careful structure. Practice means the reading goes smoothly. The 30-second introduction tells the audience why this book exists. Showing the pictures clearly is essential for picture books - the book is meant to be seen as well as heard. The 2-second pause between pages lets young listeners absorb each page. The single closing question creates a brief audience response moment. Total: a complete, professional-quality reading.
feedback.wrong: Strong preparation includes: practising at home; introducing yourself and the book in 30 seconds; reading at a steady pace with the pictures clearly visible; pausing between pages for absorption; asking one closing question. The aim is to deliver the book so Year 1 pupils can fully experience it.

answer_json.correct: BEFORE: practice reading the whole book aloud at home, holding the pages so listeners can see. Plan to introduce yourself and the book in 30 seconds. Plan to ask one question at the end ("Which page did you like most?"). DURING: read at a steady pace, look up between pages, show the illustrations clearly, leave 2 seconds between pages for absorption. AFTER: thank the class.

question_json.question:
Choose the strongest plan.

A pupil is preparing to launch their journalism project ("Three Quiet Heroes") at a school assembly. They have 4 minutes. 

What should they DO during these 4 minutes to celebrate the work effectively?
options: Read all three articles in full.

Introduce the magazine in 30 seconds (what it is, who it is for). Read ONE short excerpt (60-90 seconds) - perhaps the strongest paragraph from one article, OR the opening of each of the three articles. Hold the magazine up so the audience can see the layout. Tell the audience where they can find a copy after assembly. Thank the people who agreed to be interviewed.
Read silently while the audience watches.
question_json.correct: Introduce the magazine in 30 seconds (what it is, who it is for). Read ONE short excerpt (60-90 seconds) - perhaps the strongest paragraph from one article, OR the opening of each of the three articles. Hold the magazine up so the audience can see the layout. Tell the audience where they can find a copy after assembly. Thank the people who agreed to be interviewed.

feedback.correct: Correct! In 4 minutes, the pupil cannot read all three articles in full - and reading the whole magazine would not be the right format anyway. The right structure: brief introduction (so the audience knows what they are about to hear), one strong excerpt (to show the writing), distribution information (where copies can be found), and acknowledgement of sources. The audience leaves wanting to read more - which means they may pick up a copy after assembly.
feedback.wrong: A 4-minute assembly slot needs structure: introduction (30 sec) + one strong excerpt (60-90 sec) + distribution information + thank-yous to sources. Reading all three articles would take too long and would not show the magazine off well anyway. The aim is for the audience to leave wanting to read more.

answer_json.correct: Introduce the magazine in 30 seconds (what it is, who it is for). Read ONE short excerpt (60-90 seconds) - perhaps the strongest paragraph from one article, OR the opening of each of the three articles. Hold the magazine up so the audience can see the layout. Tell the audience where they can find a copy after assembly. Thank the people who agreed to be interviewed.

question_json.question:
Write 2-4 sentences that you will say BEFORE sharing your work, telling the audience what you have made and who it is for. This is your "doorway" into the celebration.

Keep it under 60 seconds when read aloud (about 100-150 words maximum).

Write your INTRODUCTION for sharing your work.

Example introductions by pathway:

STORY: "I have written a picture book for my five-year-old cousin Ezra, who has just started school. It is called 'When Granny Was Small'. It is about my grandmother's first day at school in 1962, told through eight pages with one surprise on each page. I wrote it because Ezra was nervous about starting school, and I wanted to tell him a story that would help."

JOURNALISM: "This is a magazine called 'Three Quiet Heroes'. It contains three feature articles about people who do important work in our community without being noticed - the lollipop lady, the library helper, and the football coach. I wrote it for school families, because I think we should know more about the people around us."
question_json.prompt:
Write your introduction here. Aim for 100-150 words.

My project is called...
It is for...
I made it because...
question_json.instruction:
Write 2-4 sentences that you will say BEFORE sharing your work, telling the audience what you have made and who it is for. This is your "doorway" into the celebration.

Keep it under 60 seconds when read aloud (about 100-150 words maximum).
question_json.modelAnswer: Example introduction for Pathway B (Poetry): "I have written a small collection of poems called 'Things My Mother Does'. There are eight poems, all about small daily kitchen actions of my mother that no one usually notices - the way she chooses fruit, the order in which she locks the doors at night, the kettle she always picks. I wrote it for my mother and aunts, because my eldest aunt told me she would like to read poems where she could 'recognise her sister'. I am going to read three of the poems now. Listen for the recurring image of light through the kitchen window."

answer_json.modelAnswer: Example introduction for Pathway B (Poetry): "I have written a small collection of poems called 'Things My Mother Does'. There are eight poems, all about small daily kitchen actions of my mother that no one usually notices - the way she chooses fruit, the order in which she locks the doors at night, the kettle she always picks. I wrote it for my mother and aunts, because my eldest aunt told me she would like to read poems where she could 'recognise her sister'. I am going to read three of the poems now. Listen for the recurring image of light through the kitchen window."

question_json.question:
Choose the most useful three changes.

AUDIENCE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 1):

A pupil completed a Pathway A short story for Year 4 readers. They want to celebrate by reading it aloud to a Year 1 class on a Friday afternoon, with the story projected as text on the wall behind them.

What THREE specific changes will make this celebration work better?
options: Three changes: (1) Match audience to intended reader - read it to a Year 4 class instead, since the story was made for Year 4 readers. (2) Drop the projected text - listeners absorb spoken stories through ears, not eyes; projected text creates competition between listening and reading. (3) Choose a time when listeners are alert - Friday afternoon is one of the lowest-attention slots in the week. A Tuesday morning slot would serve the celebration better.
The pupil should write a different story.
Use a microphone to be louder.
question_json.correct: Three changes: (1) Match audience to intended reader - read it to a Year 4 class instead, since the story was made for Year 4 readers. (2) Drop the projected text - listeners absorb spoken stories through ears, not eyes; projected text creates competition between listening and reading. (3) Choose a time when listeners are alert - Friday afternoon is one of the lowest-attention slots in the week. A Tuesday morning slot would serve the celebration better.

feedback.correct: Correct! Three changes address the three problems: wrong audience (Year 1 not Year 4), wrong format (projected text competes with listening), wrong timing (Friday afternoon is low-attention). All three are about delivery, not the writing. The story may be excellent - the celebration plan needs work.
feedback.wrong: Three changes: match audience to intended reader (Year 4); drop the projected text (listeners use ears not eyes for spoken stories); choose an alert time (not Friday afternoon). Each change is about the celebration plan, not the writing.

answer_json.correct: Three changes: (1) Match audience to intended reader - read it to a Year 4 class instead, since the story was made for Year 4 readers. (2) Drop the projected text - listeners absorb spoken stories through ears, not eyes; projected text creates competition between listening and reading. (3) Choose a time when listeners are alert - Friday afternoon is one of the lowest-attention slots in the week. A Tuesday morning slot would serve the celebration better.

question_json.question:
Choose the most important fix.

AUDIENCE DOCTOR (Diagnosis 2):

A pupil completed a Pathway D campaign about reducing single-use plastic. They have done all three pieces (poster, speech, letter). Their celebration plan: print the poster and put it under their bed, give the speech alone in their bedroom, and post the letter to themselves.

What is the SINGLE most important fix?
options: NONE of the three pieces is reaching the intended audience. The whole celebration plan is private acts that do no campaign work. The fix is to deploy each piece as planned in the L65 AMC Grid: poster goes on the school noticeboard at high-traffic break time; speech is delivered at year assembly; letter is sent to the school catering manager. Each piece has a real audience and a real channel - use them.
The pupil should switch to a different cause.
The campaign should have more pieces.
question_json.correct: NONE of the three pieces is reaching the intended audience. The whole celebration plan is private acts that do no campaign work. The fix is to deploy each piece as planned in the L65 AMC Grid: poster goes on the school noticeboard at high-traffic break time; speech is delivered at year assembly; letter is sent to the school catering manager. Each piece has a real audience and a real channel - use them.

feedback.correct: Correct! A campaign in private is not a campaign - it is paper. The fix is deployment. Match each piece to the audience and channel from the L65 AMC Grid. Poster goes where pupils see it (noticeboard). Speech goes where pupils gather (year assembly). Letter goes to the decision-maker (catering manager). Each deployment makes the campaign actually do its work.
feedback.wrong: The campaign must deploy. Each piece needs to reach its intended audience through the channel from the L65 AMC Grid. Poster on noticeboard, speech at assembly, letter to catering manager. Without deployment, the campaign cannot do its work.

answer_json.correct: NONE of the three pieces is reaching the intended audience. The whole celebration plan is private acts that do no campaign work. The fix is to deploy each piece as planned in the L65 AMC Grid: poster goes on the school noticeboard at high-traffic break time; speech is delivered at year assembly; letter is sent to the school catering manager. Each piece has a real audience and a real channel - use them.

question_json.question:
In your PWP Booklet, write your final reflection on the Chapter 6 journey.

Three sentences minimum, six sentences maximum.

Cover all three: (1) what you have learned about yourself as a writer, (2) what worked best in your project, (3) what you would do differently if you started over.

Progressive Writing Practice (PWP)

Example reflection:

"What I have learned about myself as a writer: I work best when I have a real audience in mind. When I imagined Ezra reading my book, the writing got better; when I drifted away from imagining him, the writing got general. What worked best: the moment I deconstructed the model picture book in L64 - that taught me techniques I would never have invented on my own. What I would do differently: I would do my audience research earlier and more carefully. I made some choices in the concept stage that I had to undo when I learned more about Ezra. Knowing my audience deeply made everything easier when I knew it; everything harder when I didn't."
question_json.prompt:
Write your reflection here. Three to six sentences.

What I have learned about myself as a writer:
What worked best:
What I would do differently:
question_json.instruction:
In your PWP Booklet, write your final reflection on the Chapter 6 journey.

Three sentences minimum, six sentences maximum.

Cover all three: (1) what you have learned about yourself as a writer, (2) what worked best in your project, (3) what you would do differently if you started over.
question_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection addresses three things specifically: 

1. WHAT YOU LEARNED ABOUT YOURSELF: Be specific. "I work best when..." or "I struggle when..." - name the conditions under which you write well or poorly. This carries forward to every project after this one.

2. WHAT WORKED BEST: Identify the SPECIFIC moment or technique that made the most difference. Not "the project worked" - which moment, which decision, which piece of work made the project succeed?

3. WHAT YOU WOULD DO DIFFERENTLY: Be honest. Look back across L63-L67 and name a specific decision or stage where you would now do something different. The point is not to feel bad - the point is that this lesson is what you carry forward.

answer_json.modelAnswer:
A strong reflection addresses three things specifically: 

1. WHAT YOU LEARNED ABOUT YOURSELF: Be specific. "I work best when..." or "I struggle when..." - name the conditions under which you write well or poorly. This carries forward to every project after this one.

2. WHAT WORKED BEST: Identify the SPECIFIC moment or technique that made the most difference. Not "the project worked" - which moment, which decision, which piece of work made the project succeed?

3. WHAT YOU WOULD DO DIFFERENTLY: Be honest. Look back across L63-L67 and name a specific decision or stage where you would now do something different. The point is not to feel bad - the point is that this lesson is what you carry forward.
